I’ll open to 3x the big blind (300 total) to apply pressure on Flash's wide calling range while keeping the pot controllable given his ability to 3-bet. Plan: C-bet most favorable flops, fold to a polarizing 3-bet unless I pick up strong equity or a good read.

I am in the Big Blind with King-Four offsuit. The Small Blind (Minni) has raised to 300. I am getting 2:1 odds to call and, crucially, I will have position on Minni for the rest of the hand. Given Minni's frequent opening raises, my King-high has decent equity against her range, and the positional advantage makes this a standard call to see a flop.

I raised preflop and was called by the BB; on Qh Ks 4c I have top pair (Kc8h) with a mediocre kicker. Against the BB’s calling range (lots of Qx, Kx, medium pairs and broadways) this is a clear continuation-bet spot — many worse kings and queens will call and there are few real draws to worry about. I want value and to deny free cards OOP, while keeping the pot controllable. Pot is 600 so I’ll size to ~1/2 pot to extract value and avoid bloating the pot out of position. If he folds I take it down; if he calls I proceed cautiously on the turn; if he check-raises big I’ll assume a polarized strong hand (two pair/set) and fold, while a small raise may merit a call depending on sizing.

I have flopped two pair (Kings and Fours) on a board of King-Queen-Four. This is a very strong hand in a blind-vs-blind situation. Minni, the Small Blind, has led out with a half-pot bet. While there are some hands that beat me (KQ, QQ, KK, 44), I am ahead of a significant portion of her range, including hands like KJ, KT, and various Queen-x or drawing hands like JT. I will raise here to build the pot for value and to gain information about the strength of her holding. A raise to 900 represents a standard increase that puts pressure on her weaker kings and draws.

Call to 900 total (add 600). The board is very dry (Qh Ks 4c) and Flash’s check‑raise is weighted to strong value (Kx with better kicker, KQ two pair, or sets) more often than pure bluffs — but there are still some worse Kx/Qx and speculative hands that will continue. I have top pair but a mediocre kicker and I’m out of position, so raising OOP here would be thin and likely to get me committed to a dominated spot. Calling controls the pot, gets the correct 25% pot odds (600 into 2400) to see a turn, and lets me re‑evaluate on the next card: fold to heavy turn aggression unless I improve or the action/patterns suggest a bluff; call small turn bets or extract value when appropriate.

Board: Qh Ks 4c 7c, pot 2400. I have top pair Kc8h but a very mediocre kicker and I’m out of position vs Flash, who check‑raised my flop c‑bet to 900 — a line weighted to strong value (Kx with better kicker, KQ, sets) with some rare bluffs. The turn 7c is a blank and Flash checked it, so betting OOP here would be thin: it risks getting raised/committed by better hands and folding out only a few worse Kx/Qx that I want to keep to extract value from. I prefer pot control and to keep the pot manageable: check and let Flash show aggression (or donk-bet small) so I can call small bets but fold to large polarizing overbets. Plan: check now; if Flash bets a small amount (about <= ~1/3 pot, e.g. ≲800) I’ll call for value; if he overbets/raises large I’ll fold (he’s usually polarized to two pair/set/strong Kx). If he checks back I’ll go to showdown or re-evaluate the river for a thin value bet only if the card and action are favorable.

I have two pair (Kings and Fours) on a relatively disconnected board. After I raised the flop and Minni called, her check on the turn likely indicates she is pot-controlling a marginal hand like a King with a weaker kicker or a Queen. While the 7 of clubs brings a backdoor flush draw, it is unlikely to have completed a primary draw for her given the action. I will bet for value to extract chips from her Kings and Queens and to prevent her from realizing equity for free. A half-pot bet of 1200 is appropriate to keep her range wide enough to call.

Flash check‑raised my flop which skews his range toward strong value (KQ, sets, strong Kx) but there are still Qx and occasional bluffs in there. The turn 7c is a blank and he bets 1,200 into the ~2,400 pot (pot becomes 3,600); calling 1,200 gives me ~3:1 pot odds (~25% equity required). My Kc8h (top pair, mediocre kicker) is ahead of a sizable portion of his continuing range (Qx, some worse Kx and bluffs) and has reasonable equity to continue for one street. Raising OOP here would be thin and likely commit me against two‑pair/sets/better Ks; folding surrenders value vs worse hands. I’ll call 1,200 to control the pot, realize equity, and re‑evaluate the river — fold to a large polarizing shove, call or extract small value versus small river bets or check‑backs when appropriate.

River 8♣ pairs my 8 and gives me two pair (K8) on Q♥ K♠ 4♣ 7♣ 8♣. Flash’s line (check‑raise flop, bet turn) is weighted toward strong value (KQ, sets, strong Kx) but also contains worse Kx/Qx and occasional bluffs. The river check is ambiguous — could be a trap with KQ or a refusal with marginal hands. I want to extract thin value from worse Kx and Qx while keeping the pot controlled against the polarized top of his range (sets, KQ, or a two‑club flush). A small value bet (~1/3 pot) achieves that: it will get called by many worse hands and bluffs but is less likely to induce a large raise I’d be forced to call. Pot is 4,800 — I’ll bet 1,600. Plan: collect if called, call a modest raise but fold to a large polarizing shove (he shows up with KQ/sets/flush far more often than bluffs).

I have two pair (Kings and Fours) on a board that has become quite wet with the 7c and 8c on the turn and river, completing potential backdoor flushes. Minni's decision to lead out for 1,600 on the river after check-calling the turn is a classic 'donk bet' that often signifies a hand that has improved or is looking for a thin value call. However, the pot is 8,000 total, and I only need to call 1,600, giving me 5:1 odds. Given the strength of my hand in a blind-vs-blind scenario and the excellent price I'm getting, folding is not an option. Raising would be too thin given the potential for a flush or a better two pair like K7 or K8. A call is the most prudent play to see the showdown.

On the button with A♠7♦ in a 3-handed game this is a clear open-raise — we have position and a playable ace that benefits from fold equity. Opponents are only ~43–45 BB, and standard open sizing ~3x the BB is appropriate to isolate the blinds and take the pot down or play postflop in position. I’ll raise to 300; if I face a very large 3-bet/shove I’ll fold to preserve my large stack, but I can call or continue against small-to-medium 3-bets given my positional advantage.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Four suited, a strong hand in a 3-handed game. Minni, the chip leader, has raised from the Button, which is likely a wide range. Calling allows me to see a flop with a hand that has nut-flush potential and decent top-pair value while keeping the pot manageable given my stack size of approximately 43 big blinds. I prefer calling over 3-betting here to avoid a potential 4-bet shove from the chip leader and to see how the Big Blind reacts.

K9h is a strong suited connector in the BB facing a button raise and SB call. Excellent post-flop playability with straight and flush potential, live king. Pot odds are favorable (700 pot, 200 to call, over 3.5:1 direct + implied). Defending wide vs button range which is often loose. Raising is unnecessary and risks bloating pot OOP with speculative hand against deep Minni. Folding too tight given position and odds. Call to realize equity.

On a flop of Qc-4s-3c, I have hit bottom pair with an Ace kicker. While bottom pair has some showdown value in a three-handed game, checking to the pre-flop aggressor (Minni) is the standard and most cautious play from the Small Blind. This allows me to see how both the Button and the Big Blind (Elon) evaluate the board before committing more chips. My hand is not strong enough to lead out into two opponents, but it is certainly strong enough to consider calling a standard continuation bet depending on the sizing and the action behind me.

Flop misses K9h completely: no pair, no flush draw (hearts), no straight draw (board 3c-Qc-4s uncoordinated for my hand). King-high has little showdown value against Minni's wide button opening range (often includes Qx, Ax suited, clubs) and Flash's speculative SB calling range. Board favors club draws and Qx pairs. Betting offers no value/thin value and risks larger pots OOP when behind; fold equity low vs calling stations observed in history. Checking preserves stack, allows free cards or check-downs if missed, fold to justified bets. Consistent with preflop speculative defend realizing equity multiway.

I opened pre and we’re now in a 3‑way pot on a Qc 3c 4s flop with A♠7♦. This is essentially Ace‑high with no club backdoor and the Queen heavily hits my perceived raising range, so my hand has poor showdown value and limited fold equity against two callers. Betting here will often be called by Qx, pocket pairs and club draws and simply bloats the pot out of position on later streets. I’ll check to control the pot and realize equity; plan is to reassess on the turn (look to barrel only if I improve or pick up significant fold equity, otherwise continue pot control).

I have improved to two pair (Aces and Fours) on the turn. The flop was checked around by everyone, suggesting that Minni (the pre-flop aggressor) likely does not have a set or a strong Queen. The Ace is a very strong card for my range, and since I now have a top-tier hand in this three-way pot, I should bet for value and protection against potential club draws or gutshots. I will lead out for 600, which is two-thirds of the pot, to extract value from any Queens, weaker Aces, or draws that my opponents might be holding.

K9h completely missed the flop (3c-Qc-4s) and turn Ad provides no improvement: no pair, no straight draw, no flush draw (hearts not on board), pure king-high bluff catcher with minimal showdown value. Preflop speculative call realized no equity postflop. Flash's turn bet after flop check-check-check suggests either hitting the Ace (Ax, Qx turning pair), completing club draw, or turning a strong draw/straight (A5, 52, etc.). Facing multiway pot OOP with Minni to act behind (deep-stacked button who often defends wide and can squeeze), calling 600 into 1500 (3.5:1 odds) offers no fold equity realization or implied odds for my nothing hand. Raising as a bluff is suboptimal: poor blockers (no A/Q/c), Flash commits 17% stack, Minni can call/raise wide. Preserve stack for better spots in 3-handed play; consistent with flop check-fold plan when missing.

Call 600. The turn Ad gives me top pair (A7) but with a weak kicker on Qc‑3c‑4s. Flash's 600 into a 1500 pot is a fairly small probe — it can be a value bet with A4/A3 or a bluff/missed club — and I don't want to bloat the pot or polarize my range with a thin raise. Calling realizes equity, keeps worse Aces and bluffs in, and gives me position to control the river. I’ll reevaluate on the river (fold to extreme aggression only if he shows clear two‑pair/set strength).

I have Two Pair (Aces and Fours) on a board of 3c-Qc-4s-Ad-8d. The flop was checked around, and Minni called my lead on the turn when the Ace appeared. This suggests she likely has a Queen, a weaker Ace, or was on a club draw that has now missed. The 8d on the river is a complete brick that shouldn't have improved her hand. I will bet 1100 into the 2100 pot to extract value from her one-pair hands (like Qx or Ax with a lower kicker). If she has a better hand like a set or a better Two Pair, she likely would have shown more aggression earlier in the hand.

Pot 3200, to‑call 1100 → ~25.6% pot odds. My A♠7♦ is top pair but a weak kicker; I lose to most A‑kickers and to two‑pair/trips (A3/A4/A8, 33/44/88) but beat missed club draws and many weaker Ax combos. Flash’s line (call pre, check/call flop, turn raise 600, small river stab 1100) is consistent with a polarized mix of thin value and bluffs/semi‑bluffs — the river sizing is fairly small and looks like a probe/blocker or thin value bet rather than a polar shove. Raising would only polarize and risk a shove from better aces/two‑pair; folding gives up too much equity given the price. I’ll call 1100 to realize equity and capitalize on the bluffs/thin value in his range.

I am on the button with 6h 4s. This is a very weak starting hand, even in a short-handed (3-player) game. Given that Minni (the Big Blind) has a massive chip lead and has shown a willingness to defend and play aggressively, and Elon (the Small Blind) is at a stack size where they might look to shove, there is no reason to commit chips with such a low-equity hand. I will fold and wait for a better spot to utilize my stack.

Preflop in small blind with 3s5d offsuit, one of the weakest possible hands. No high card value, poor connectivity, offsuit. Flash folded, leaving heads-up vs Minni in BB who has been aggressive. Pot 150, to call 50, but no showdown value or implied odds justify playing. Stack 4300 (43BB), better to preserve chips for premium hands. Standard fold to avoid spew.

QhKd is a strong Broadway hand on the button, excellent for raising preflop in position against the blinds. The pot has 150 dead money (blinds posted), to call 100. Opponents have large stacks (Minni very deep, Flash 66BB), and history shows they often limp/check and call raises with wide ranges. Raising to 3x the BB (300 total) is standard: builds the pot, takes initiative, has good fold equity, and plays well postflop in position. My stack of 43BB supports this aggression to maximize over tournament.

Facing a standard 3x button open to 300 from the button, I have K♥5♦ in the small blind. This is a weak, often-dominated hand with a poor kicker and bad OOP postflop play. Calling 250 to see a flop will frequently become a multiway pot (Flash still to act) and I’ll be at a range and positional disadvantage; a small raise to 500 is not attractive either (it invites 3-bets or calls and I have no fold equity or strong equity). With a deep tournament stack I’ll conserve chips and wait for a better spot. Fold and preserve stack for profitable opportunities.

I am in the Big Blind with Jc 3c. The Button (Elon) has raised to 300. Minni (Small Blind) has folded. I need to call 200 more to see the flop in a pot that will be 650. While J3 suited is a weak hand, it has some playability in a heads-up pot from the blinds, especially with the odds being offered (2.25:1). Given the stack sizes and the 3-handed nature of the game, I will call to defend my big blind and see a flop.

I have flopped two pair (Jacks and Treys) on a board of Jd 3h 6h. This is a very strong hand in a heads-up pot against the pre-flop raiser. There are flush draws (hearts) and potential straight draws (4-5) available, so I want to protect my hand and extract value. However, since I am out of position, checking to the initial aggressor (Elon) is a standard play to see if he continues with a bet, allowing me to check-raise or just call to keep his bluffs in. If he checks back, I can lead on the turn. I'll check for now.

Preflop, I raised with KQo on the button for value, position, and initiative against loose BB defenders like Flash, who frequently calls raises from the blinds based on hand history (e.g., Hands 9, 12, 20, 23). Flop Jd3h6h is fairly dry but coordinated with two hearts and possible wheel/5-high straight draws (45x). My hand has Queen-high overcards (K>Q>J), backdoor flush draw (Qh), and blocks broadway (KQ). Range advantage as preflop raiser: hits sets, overpairs, AJ/KJ, flush draws, etc. Flash checks BB, typical for wide calling range missing flop often. Pot 650 (95 effective BB), stacks 40BB mine/64BB Flash. Half-pot c-bet ~325 denies equity to his floats/draws, takes it down frequently given his fold history to flops (e.g., Hands 1,5,10,20,23), builds pot if called with equity in position. Larger bet risks overbetting air vs stubborn caller; smaller risks cheap calls. Bet 350 for pot control, fold equity, and balanced range.

I have flopped two pair (Jacks and Treys) on a board with a heart draw. This is a very strong hand in a heads-up pot. Elon has made a continuation bet of 350 into a pot of 650. I want to extract value from his range, which includes top pairs like AJ, KJ, or overpairs, as well as flush draws. By raising now, I build the pot while his range is still wide and protect against draws. A raise to 1050 is a standard size that charges draws and gets value from worse made hands.

Facing a check-raise from Flash on a coordinated flop Jd3h6h (flush draw heavy, possible straight draws like 45,57). My QhKd is Queen-high with a King overcard, backdoor heart flush draw (Qh blocks some), but no made hand and likely behind Flash's check-raising range from BB: strong hearts (flush draws Ahxh, Khxh, 5h4h etc.), sets (JJ/33/66 unlikely but possible), two-pair combos (J6, J3 suited, 63s). Preflop, Flash defended wide BB call to my button raise (loose per history: Hands 9,12,20,23,27 calls pre). My c-bet sizing (350 into 650) was for value/protection but he check-raised pot-committing size (1050 total, SPR drops to ~1.5 post-call). Pot odds 2050:700 (~2.9:1) but implied limited (my 3650 stack, but hand weak); position advantage diminished by board texture favoring his range. History shows Flash capable of trapping/value-owning (e.g., Hand 17 big pots won calling stations). Equity ~25-35% vs his range (sims: vs flush draws ~40%, but vs made >20%). Folding preserves 36BB stack vs deep Minni (289BB), avoids cooler on turn heart/straight card. Re-steal later spots; discipline maximizes long-term EV in tournament.

I'm on the button with K♥5♠ in a 3-handed game — a marginal hand but very playable in position and worth opening to steal. The pot is 150 and it costs 100 to see the flop; a standard 3x BB open to 300 gives good fold equity against both blinds and leverages my huge stack/position. Elon (BB, ~35bb) is short and likely to fold or shove; Flash (SB, ~73bb) may defend but I can play postflop in position. Plan: raise to 300 to either take the blinds or play the flop in position; if faced with a large 3-bet or shove from a short-stacked Elon, I’ll be cautious and likely fold rather than get in with a dominated K5o.

I am holding Jack of Hearts and Trey of Clubs in the Small Blind. Minni has raised to 300 from the Button. J3 offsuit is a very weak hand with poor playability, especially when out of position against a deep-stacked aggressive player like Minni. Given the tournament context and my current stack of 7350 chips (73.5 BB), there is no reason to defend with such a marginal hand. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

43o is a trash hand with minimal playability, offsuit low connectors lacking suited potential. Facing a button raise from aggressive Minni (huge stack), my 35.5BB stack is decent but no need to defend wide OOP preflop. Pot odds 2.25:1 require ~30% equity, but vs Minni's likely wide-strong range, I have poor equity and postflop disadvantage. Folding preserves stack for better spots in tournament.

I am on the button with Queen-Four offsuit. While this is a 3-handed game, Q4o is a very weak starting hand to open with, especially with Minni holding a massive stack in the Big Blind and Elon being short-stacked in the Small Blind. Minni has been quite active and aggressive, and I don't want to bloat a pot with a marginal hand out of position or get into a confrontation where I am likely behind. Folding here is the safest and most disciplined play to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

AJ offsuit is a premium hand, roughly top 10% of hands, especially strong in a 3-handed tournament spot after Button folds. From SB vs BB, raising is standard to take initiative, build the pot for value, and potentially steal blinds or get called by worse. Minni's BB stack is deep at 29k, but my 3.5k (35BB) effective is fine for a 3BB raise. OOP postflop but hand has good playability. Standard size 3x BB (300 total) balances value and fold equity. History shows Minni defends wide but folds to aggression sometimes (e.g., recent raises). Avoid limping as it invites multiway pots OOP with marginal strength.

Elon (SB) opened to 300. I’m in the big blind with Q♠T♠ — a very playable, suited Broadway hand with good equity and postflop flexibility. Calling the 200 more (300 total committed) is the right play: it gives ~3:1 immediate pot odds, defends a wide SB opening range, and keeps the pot manageable out of position. A tiny 3-bet to 500 would have minimal fold equity and needlessly bloats the pot with a marginal hand, while a larger 3-bet is too commit-heavy OOP. Plan: call, evaluate the flop — continue/raise with strong hands or draws, otherwise use pot control and fold to large pressure on dry, unfavorable boards.

Preflop raiser with AJ offsuit on a paired 662 board with spade flush draw possible. Board is relatively dry and favors my raising range (overpairs, sets, Ax), while Minni's BB calling range is capped low (small pairs, suited connectors, weak Ax). AJ has ace-high showdown value, blocks strong Ax and nut spade flush draws (As), and decent fold equity against his floats. OOP c-bet standard to take it down ~60-70% time; checking invites check-back or tough spots later. 2/3 pot size (400) balances value from worse Ax/pairs and protection/fold equity. Stack 32BB, pot 600, sizing leaves room for postflop play. History shows Minni calls flops but folds to continued aggression.

Board: 6♦ 2♠ 6♠. I hold Q♠T♠ — no pair but two overcards plus a strong four-card spade draw (two spades in hand + two on board). Pot is 1000 and Elon bet 400; calling 400 gives ~2.5:1 pot odds (break-even ~28.6% equity). My hand has well over that equity (9 spade outs to a flush plus pair-up outs), so calling is +EV. Raising here as a semi-bluff would bloat the pot out of position against a player who can shove or continue — unnecessary given the draw and stack dynamics. Plan: call to see the turn; if a spade or Q/T appears I will apply pressure, if the turn is a blank and Elon barrels large I will fold to strong pressure.

Board is 6d 2s 6s 2h, extremely paired (66 and 22), low, with spade flush draw possible. I hold AsJc: nut spade flush draw (As blocks the nuts), ace-high equity, and blocks strong Ax/spade combos in villain's range. Preflop I raised AJs as a strong hand; flop c-bet continuation was standard on dry paired board. Minni BB defended wide pre (calls 200) and called flop 400 into ~500 pot, so range includes 6x (overpairs like 77-TT somewhat unlikely post-call, but 66/22/62 trips/fulls, suited connectors, small pairs 33-55, weak Ax/spades, floats). Turn pairing 2s improves some to boats/two pair (e.g., 62, 26), but also bricks many draws/pairs, while giving me massive equity (9 outs to nuts flush + straight outs? but mainly flush). Pot 1400, my stack 2850 (~2:1 SPR), Minni deep 28k. Checking risks giving free card to his equity (spade draws, straight draws like 3x/5x/4x) or check-raise bluffs; invites tough river. Betting extracts value from his trapped pairs/6x/trips worse than flush, denies equity, represents strength (full houses common now), and leverages fold equity vs floats/small pairs. Size ~2/3 pot (900) balances value from worse + thin value, protection, sets up river shove if called (SPR ~1.6 post-call). History: Minni calls stations but folds to turn aggression (e.g., Hand 23 river fold, Hand 24 turn fold). AJs crushes his capped range here; bet to maximize EV long-term stack growth in 3-handed MTT.

Board on the turn is 6♦ 2♠ 6♠ 2♥ — the community now pairs both ranks, so any opponent holding a 6 or a 2 already has a full house. Pot is 2,300 and Elon has bet 900 (to call 900). Calling 900 puts my break-even equity at ~900/3200 ≈ 28.1%. Naïvely I have ~15 outs (9 spades for a flush + 3 queens + 3 tens) → ~15/46 ≈ 32.6% to improve on the river. However most of those outs are irrelevant if Elon holds a 6 or a 2 because a flush or a single-pair river won’t beat his full house. Elon's line (SB open preflop, flop bet, then a large turn raise) plus his short stack (1,950) strongly favors value/full-house holdings, so my effective equity vs his realistic range is much lower than the naive number. Raising as a semi-bluff would bloat the pot out of position and likely get called by hands that still beat or cram by a shorter-stacked villain. My previous plan specifically said to fold to a large barrel on a blank turn; Elon just fired a big turn raise. With a very large stack advantage and many better spots to apply pressure, folding preserves chips and avoids being drawn dead. Therefore I fold.

Pocket 3s on the button in a 3-handed pot preflop. This is a strong hand with good equity against the loose short-handed calling ranges of SB and BB opponents. Raising builds the pot for value, charges draws, and protects against overcards. Standard button raise size of 3x the BB (300 total) balances fold equity and induces calls from worse. Minni has a huge stack but we've seen aggression; Flash shorter. Avoid limping as it invites multiway pots where set-mining is less ideal without deep stacks.

Three-handed, SB vs Button open to 300 — I hold T♣7♠, which is marginal but playable in this short-handed spot. Elon (button) has been opening to 300 a lot and is short-stacked (≈3.95k), so his range is relatively wide; Flash (BB) can still defend or squeeze. With my deep stack (28.35k) I prefer to call and see a flop rather than 3-bet light: a min-raise to 500 is small and will usually be called, leaving me OOP with a weak kicker, while a larger 3-bet risks isolating versus a shove or a stronger range. Calling 300 (250 more) keeps the pot controllable, preserves postflop maneuverability, and gives me implied odds to realize equity when I hit a ten or make a disguised hand. Plan: evaluate flop — play aggressively if I hit top pair or a strong draw; fold to heavy turn/river pressure if I miss and face significant aggression.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Nine offsuit. The Button (Elon) has raised to 300 and the Small Blind (Minni) has called. I am getting excellent pot odds (4.5 to 1) to see a flop. While A9o is a strong hand 3-handed, calling allows me to keep the pot manageable and see a flop out of position against two opponents, one of whom (Minni) is the chip leader. Elon's stack is also relatively short, so I want to avoid bloating the pot unnecessarily unless I hit the flop hard.

Board: 6♥ 5♥ 4♠. I hold T♣7♠ — I missed. I only have an 8‑out straight draw (any 3 or any 8): ~17% to hit the turn, ~31.5% by the river. No heart backdoor and I’m out of position with two opponents (BTN Elon ~3.95k, BB Flash ~7.05k). Against two callers my fold equity is low and Flash has shown he calls down a lot, so a c-bet here is unlikely to fold both opponents and will often put me OOP in a bloated pot with a marginal draw.

Given my deep stack advantage I don’t need to bluff to win this pot now — I prefer pot control and to realize equity cheaply. Plan: check and see how opponents act. I’ll call small bets to try to realize my 8 outs, but fold to large bets or shoves (Elon’s ~3.95k shove is too expensive to call with only 8 outs). If the action checks to the turn I’ll only start betting/pressuring if I improve (pair/straight) or get a very favorable runout; otherwise keep the pot small and re-evaluate. Therefore: check now.

The board is extremely coordinated (6-5-4) and favors the ranges of the players in the blinds more than the pre-flop raiser, but I have only Ace-high with some backdoor potential. In a 3-way pot, this board is very likely to have connected with someone. I will check to see how the original raiser (Elon) reacts to the board. If there is heavy action, I will likely fold, as Ace-high is weak on such a wet texture.

Flopped a perfect disguised set of 3s on a highly coordinated 4-5-6 rainbow-ish board (two hearts) with flush draw and multiple straight draws (2-3, 3-7, etc.). This is the nuts or near-nuts against most calling ranges, vulnerable only to unlikely sets of 45 or 56. Both opponents checked, likely weak or trapping, but pre-flop calls suggest wide ranges including draws and floats. Betting extracts value from top pair, two-pair (e.g., 67, 45), weaker sets, and charges max equity to live draws. Pot 900, bet 2/3 pot (600) for protection, value, and fold equity from pure air while keeping SPR manageable vs. short-ish Flash stack and deep Minni. Avoids check-raise risk and builds pot for stacks.

Board 6♥5♥4♠ with T♣7♠ gives me an 8‑out straight draw (any 3 or any 8) — ~17% to hit the turn, ~31.5% by the river. Elon (BTN) c‑bets 600 into a 1500 pot, a relatively small bet (~0.4 pot). Calling 600 is the correct play: the price is cheap and my ~31.5% equity to the river is well above the break‑even threshold (≈22% heads‑up, ≈18% if both opponents call). Raising would be overcommitting out of position with a semi‑bluff that has limited fold equity, and folding here would be too tight given the odds. I’ll call to realize my equity and re‑evaluate on the turn — fold to heavy shove/pressure if I miss, or start betting/raising if I improve to a made hand.

The board texture (6h 5h 4s) is extremely dangerous and highly coordinated, favoring the ranges of my opponents more than mine. Elon's continuation bet and Minni's subsequent call indicate that at least one of them likely has a made hand or a strong draw. With only Ace-high and no significant draws or backdoors on this wet board, I have very little equity. Folding is the most prudent play to preserve my stack.

I just improved to a made straight on the turn: board 6♥-5♥-4♠-8♥ and my T♣7♠ gives me 4-5-6-7-8. This is a very strong hand but the board is dangerous — there are three hearts, so any two-heart combo for Elon is already a flush and beats me. Elon was the preflop raiser and c-bettor on the flop, so his range is wide and contains lots of single-heart and two-heart combos. I want to extract value from hands that will call (pairs, 8x, single-heart callers) and charge single-heart combos that can turn into a flush on the river, while still giving myself a chance to fold to extreme action if it appears very polarized. Because I improved to a made hand I should be the aggressor here: I’ll bet to both get value and deny correct odds to heart‑one-card draws on the river. A bet of 1,500 (~2/3 pot) is big enough to charge draws and get decent value from weaker pairs/top pairs, but not so huge that I overcommit my entire stack to a polarized shove. If Elon jams, I’ll be willing to call — his shove will be for a relatively small amount (≈3.35k) against my large stack and his jam will often be polarized to flushes or bluffs; given my straight I’m ahead of a large portion of his range and the call is +EV in this spot.

Facing a turn check-raise? No, Minni leads turn after calling flop bet into coordinated board. My set of 3s remains very strong, beating all flushes (heart blocker with 3h), two-pair, top pair, draws that missed. Vulnerable mainly to 7x straights (4-5-6-7-8) or unlikely sets/overpairs that slowplayed, but her SB call pre and flop call fits wide range including 7x suited, flush draws, floats turning bricks. Turn 8h completes some straights but also pairs draws. Pot 3600, facing 1500 (pot odds 3.4:1 excellent). Stack 3350 commits most (~94%). Calling leaves 1850 into ~5100 (SPR <1), vulnerable to river shove even when ahead. Raising all-in (3350 total) maximizes value from worse hands (overpairs, two-pair like 45/56, turned flush draws, bluffs), applies max pressure to her range, denies equity to draws, and gets full value now while stacks allow. Minni deep (26k) but shown aggression; shoving exploits her calling station tendencies seen in history (e.g., Hand 17 huge pot). Folding sets too nitty; this is monster on board where we bet flop for value/protection.

I just made a 4-5-6-7-8 straight with T♣7♠ on 6♥-5♥-4♠-8♥. The board is dangerous (three hearts), so the only hands that currently beat me are two‑heart combos (flushes) or very specific higher-straight combos — but Elon jammed all‑in for 3,350 over my 1,500 raise, and I only have to call 1,850 more. The pot is 6,950, so calling 1,850 to win 8,800 requires roughly 1,850/8,800 ≈ 21% equity to break even. Elon's short‑stack shove will be polarized — some flushes and some bluffs/mediocre pairs — and my straight is ahead of a large portion of his range (pairs, sets, single‑heart hands, many broadway combos). The call is a small fraction of my 26k stack and meets the pot‑odds threshold, so this is +EV. I will call the all‑in (total commitment on the turn = 3,350).

I’m on the button (small blind) with 5♣2♠ — a very weak hand, but in heads-up play you defend extremely wide. The cost to see a flop is only 50 chips (complete to 100 total) so calling is a cheap, low-variance option that keeps the pot small while giving me positional advantage postflop. Raising to 200 could occasionally pick up the pot preflop, but it also invites a 3-bet or tougher postflop decisions with a junk hand; with deep stacks I prefer to see flops in position and fold to heavy aggression unless I improve. Plan: call to 100 total, evaluate the flop — continue only with a pair, a clear draw, or a favorable board/line; fold to large, non-giving action on dry boards.

I hold Ace-Queen offsuit, which is a very strong hand in heads-up play. Minni limped from the Small Blind, which often represents a capped or speculative range. I will raise to 400 to extract value, take control of the hand, and potentially win the pot pre-flop.

Fold. I completed to 100 with 5♣2♠ intending to see a cheap flop, but Flash’s raise to 400 makes this a much more expensive spot. The pot is 500 and I must call 300 to make it 800 — I need ~37.5% equity to justify a call. 5-2o has very poor equity and playability against a raising range (it will be dominated and rarely hit strong hands), so calling is a negative-EV play. 3-betting is out of the question with this junk hand. Preserve my deep stack and wait for a better spot — fold here.

I am on the button in a heads-up situation with a stack of 71 BB. While Jack-Five offsuit is a weak hand, playing in position is a significant advantage. I will raise to 300 to take control of the pot and potentially win it pre-flop. If Minni calls, I can use my positional advantage to navigate the post-flop play.

I’m in the big blind with 3d 3c facing a button open to 300. Pot is 400 and I need to put 200 more (300 total). Villain’s stack (6850) is much shorter than mine (32750), but still ~68 BB — not shove-short. Against a button open in heads-up, a small pocket pair is best played by calling to set-mine and keep the pot controlled: I get ~3:1 immediate pot odds on the call (need ~25% equity), and 33 has comfortably more equity versus a wide opening range. 3-betting OOP with a low pair commits more chips into an out-of-position spot and invites 4-bets or large pots when I’m unlikely to have a lot of postflop edge unless I hit. If Villain jams later I can still call (33 fares well vs a wide shove); if I flop a set I can extract big value. Plan: call, evaluate flop, check/call reasonable c-bets and extract value when I hit a set.

Check. I called preflop as a set-mine with 3♦3♣; the A‑T‑7 rainbow flop is very bad for my hand (little showdown value, many Ax/Tx/broadway combos in the opener’s range). I want to keep the pot small OOP and realize my equity rather than turn this into a big bluff or inflated spot. I only have 2 outs to a set (~4.3% on the turn, ~8.4% by the river), so plan is to check and either call small‑to‑medium continuation bets where implied odds make sense, fold to very large overbets that deny those odds, and if I hit a set extract value. If villain checks back I take a free turn to try to improve.

I raised pre-flop with J5o and was called by Minni. The flop comes T-7-A rainbow. This board favors my perceived range as the pre-flop raiser, as I have more combinations of strong Ax, Kx, and Broadway hands. Minni has checked to me. While I have no direct connection to this board, a continuation bet of 300 (half pot) is effective here to fold out her non-connecting hands like small pairs or low suited connectors that missed. Given the texture, she is unlikely to continue without at least a pair or a gutshot.

I called preflop to set-mine with 3♦3♣ and the A‑T‑7 rainbow flop is terrible for my hand — almost no showdown value and only two outs to a set. Flash’s 300 into the current pot is a small/medium c‑bet; I prefer to realize equity and keep the pot manageable rather than fold away my set-mine or bloat an OOP spot with a bluff/3‑bet. Calling 300 to win ~1200 gives ~25% immediate pot odds (turn hit ~2/47 = 4.3%, by river ~8.5%), so I’m calling for implied odds — if I hit a set I can extract value, if I miss I’ll fold to very large turn overbets that deny those odds. Plan: call to see the turn, re-evaluate on the turn (check/call small bets, fold to big overbets, and bet/raise for value if I hit a set).

The turn paired the board (Ah) and I now have two pair (Aces & Threes). That’s a clear improvement from the flop but still vulnerable: Flash’s preflop raise + flop c‑bet make A‑x and T‑x very common in his range (both beat me), and he can also have turn‑improved hands that crush me. I’m OOP and should pot‑control rather than build a big pot with a hand that’s often second‑best. Checking induces bluffs and lets me call small/medium bets for pot odds to see the river (I still have two outs to trips), while folding to very large bets that are almost always value. Plan: check now, call small/medium bets to see river, fold to big overbets or shoves, and re‑evaluate if he checks back.

The board has paired on the turn with a second Ace. My pre-flop and flop betting represented an Ace, and the second Ace further decreases the likelihood that Minni has one herself. Minni has checked again, showing weakness. I will check behind to keep the pot small and see a free river, as I have no showdown value and a bet here is unlikely to fold out a Ten or a Seven given the pot odds she would be getting.

River decision: with 3♦3♣ on Th 7s Ac Ah 6s my best 5 is two pair (Aces and Threes). Villain opened pre, c‑bet the flop, then checked the turn and river — a line that contains many A‑x, T‑x and medium broadway hands that either already beat me (any Ace or Ten, many 7s) or will call a small bet. Betting OOP here risks building a pot for hands that dominate mine while only extracting thin value from worse hands; checking controls the pot and forces villain to show down. I’ll check and take showdown (and only call a small river bet; fold to large overbets).

The board is Th-7s-Ac-Ah-6s. I hold Jh-5h, which has no showdown value. I raised pre-flop and bet the flop, representing the Ace. After checking the turn for pot control, Minni has checked the river as well. While a bluff could potentially fold out a weak seven, Minni's call on the flop suggests she has a piece of the board (like a Ten or a Seven). Given her massive stack size relative to mine, she is likely to call a river bet to see my cards, especially since my turn check showed weakness. I will check behind and concede the pot, preserving my stack for better opportunities.

We’re heads-up on the button/SB with 4♦7♠ — a weak hand, but in heads‑up play the button should open a very wide range. Flash (BB) has ~64 BB, and table dynamics show frequent 3‑00 opens, so a standard preflop open to ~3x the big blind is appropriate to seize initiative and maximize fold equity while keeping the pot manageable. Raising to 300 (total) accomplishes that: we’ll win the blinds often, and if Flash calls we retain position to bluff, float, or fold to heavy action on later streets. If he 3‑bets large we can release this marginal holding — no need to bloat the pot with 4‑7 offsuit.

I am in the Big Blind with King-Six offsuit. The action is heads-up against Minni on the Button, who has raised to 300. My stack is relatively short compared to Minni's, but K6o is a standard defending hand in this position. I need to call 200 more into a pot that will be 600, giving me 3-to-1 odds. This price is too good to fold a King-high hand, so I will call and see a flop.

I have King-high on a Queen-Ten-Four rainbow board. This board texture hits the pre-flop raiser's range better than mine. I will check to the aggressor to see how they react. There's no reason to lead here with no pair and limited draw potential.

I hold 4h7s — a small pair (pair of fours) on Tc 4s Qh. Heads‑up the BB’s calling range is wide (Qx/Tx, Kx, Ax, small pairs, assorted broadways and connectors) and Flash checked to me, so I can take the initiative in position. I want to extract value from worse hands (Kx, Jx, lower pairs) and deny free cards to overcards, but I don’t want to build a huge pot with a vulnerable kicker, so I’ll make a half‑pot c‑bet. Bet 300 gives good fold equity and value while allowing me to fold to a strong check‑raise and continue cautiously if called — re‑evaluate on the turn.

On this Queen-Ten-Four rainbow flop, Minni's 300 bet into a 600 pot (half-pot) is a standard continuation bet. I currently have King-high with no direct draw or pair. While I have two overcards to the Ten and Four, they are likely behind any piece of the board Minni has, and my King is outkicked by several hands in her range. I have no back-door equity besides a running straight. Calling out of position with nothing but King-high against a frequent aggressor like Minni is likely to lead to further difficult decisions on the turn. I will fold and wait for a better spot to commit my remaining 62 big blinds.

In a heads-up situation, King-Six offsuit is a strong enough hand to open for a raise from the Button/Small Blind. Raising allows me to take the initiative and potentially win the pot pre-flop or set up a continuation bet. Given my stack size relative to the Big Blind, a standard 3x raise is appropriate.

I have 9♥4♦ — a very weak, frequently dominated hand. Flash opened to 300 from the button; calling 200 to play out of position on the flop for a relatively small pot is a poor spot. Pot odds (~3:1) require ~25% equity, and 9-4o has well under that vs a standard heads-up raising range. I also have a large stack advantage, so there is no need to gamble or isolate with a marginal hand. Fold and wait for a better spot with position or real equity.

Heads-up on the button with A♣2♣ — a hand I want to play aggressively in position. Pot is 150 and calling 50 is passive; raising takes the initiative, leverages position, and extracts fold equity against the BB. I’ll open to the minimum allowed (200 total) — a standard 2x BB-ish size that keeps postflop play manageable while still pressuring weaker holdings. Flash has shown aggression but also calls sizable pots; plan is to call modest 3-bets and play postflop in position, and fold to very large 3-bets that commit most of his 6,250 stack. If called, proceed with c-bets on many A-high/low-connectivity flops and be careful on boards where my weak kicker is likely dominated.

In this heads-up situation, Minni has raised to 200 from the button, meaning I need to call 100 more to see the flop. The pot is currently 300, giving me 3:1 pot odds (25% equity required). While Queen-Four offsuit is a weak hand, it possesses enough raw equity against a standard button opening range to justify a call, especially since I am getting a discount from the big blind. I will play cautiously post-flop given I am out of position against a significant chip leader.

I am heads-up and out of position against the chip leader. The flop of Ace-Jack-Eight misses my range almost entirely. Queen-Four offsuit has very little equity here, and this board is likely to have hit Minni's button-raising range. I will check and plan to fold to most bets, as I have no pair and no real draws.

I raised preflop and was called; on Js Ad 8h I have top pair (A♣2♣) with a weak kicker but I’m in position. Villain checked, so a value/protection c-bet is standard: it extracts from worse A‑x, Jx and medium pairs, folds many non‑made hands, and keeps the initiative. I’ll make a half‑pot bet (≈200) — large enough to charge any overcards/backdoor tries and get value, but small enough to induce calls from worse hands and keep the pot controllable against Flash’s aggression (he has ~6,150). Plan: if he calls, re-evaluate on the turn and continue for thin value on safe bricks; if he check‑raises or jams heavily I’ll respect a strong range and fold to extreme polarizing action, while calling smaller/standard raises to keep a wide range of bluffs in his range.

On a flop of Ace-Jack-Eight, my Queen-Four offsuit has virtually no equity. Minni, who raised pre-flop from the button, is very likely to have hit this board or has a range advantage that makes a bluff unprofitable for me to call. I have no pair and no draws. Folding is the only sensible play.

I am on the button with KdTh, which is a strong starting hand in a heads-up situation. I have 61 big blinds, and my opponent has a massive lead. I will put in a standard 3x raise to 300 to take control of the hand and build a pot with a hand that has good high-card value and equity.

Facing a standard 3x open to 300 from the button with Ks7h in the big blind, I’ll call (300 total). The hand is marginal but heads-up the opener’s range is wide and I’m getting ~33% pot odds to see a flop (call 200 into a 400 pot). I don’t want to bloat the pot out of position with a thin 3-bet — a raise would open me to 3-bets or commits against better Kx/Ax holdings. Call to see the flop and proceed based on texture and opponent’s postflop aggression.

Check. On Qh-Jh-Td with Ks7h I have only king-high with runner-runner hearts and 8 clean turn outs to a straight (4 A + 4 9s) plus 3 K outs to pair (pairing K is often a weak showdown hand here). The board heavily connects with the opener’s wide button range (many AK/K9/Qx/Jx/Tx/JT combos make straights or strong pairs), so I don’t want to bloat the pot with a marginal hand. I’ll check to control the pot size and see how Flash reacts: call a small c-bet (~≤1/3 pot ≈ 200) to realize equity, but fold or seriously reassess to a large c-bet/raise/jam since those line up with many hands that already beat me. If he checks back I’ll re-evaluate the turn (bet/raise only if I pick up strong equity like K/A/9 or specific bluffs).

On a flop of Qh Jh Td, I have bottom pair (Tens) and an open-ended straight draw (any Ace or 9 completes my straight). Minni checked to me. I have a lot of equity here and should bet to maintain the initiative and get value from hands like weaker pairs or straight draws. A half-pot bet of 300 is a standard continuation bet on this texture.

Call 300. Pot is 900 and calling 300 gives ~25% break-even pot odds (300 to win 1,200). On Qh-Jh-Td with Ks7h I have 11 clean outs to a pair/straight (3 kings + 4 aces + 4 nines) and a small backdoor heart possibility — raw two-card equity to the river is ~41% (turn ~23%). Flash opened/CB frequently and his c‑bet size is smallish, so his range is wide enough that calling to realize equity is +EV. I don’t want to inflate the pot by raising with only K‑high on such a coordinated board (raising would fold out some hands but also invite 3‑bets or big calls from made Q/J/T combinations). Plan: call to see the turn; if I hit a K/A/9 or pick up a meaningful heart I’ll re-evaluate and apply pressure/value bet. If I miss and face significant aggression on the turn/river I will fold unless I’ve improved.

I’ve just made the Broadway straight (A-K-Q-J-T) with Ks7h on the turn — this is essentially the best possible hand on this board except for a very rare straight‑flush/royal (not possible here) or a tie if Flash also holds a King. Flash has a wide button opening/c-bet range and called the flop, so he will still have many Qx/Jx/Tx/Ax and some Kx in his continuing range — all of which are profitable to extract value from. Pot is 1,200; betting ~2/3 pot (~800) will get called by most worse hands (top pair/two pair/second pair and some Ax) without folding too much of his range and keeps the weaker holdings and potential all‑ins in play. I’m happy to call a shove (he’s short-stacked at 5,550) — I’m ahead versus virtually his entire range except ties with a K. Bet 800 to maximize value while avoiding shipping the pot away.

On a board of Qh Jh Td Ad, I have made the nut straight (Broadways) with my Kd Th. My Kd also blocks the nut diamond flush draw. Minni has led out for 800 into a 1200 pot, which indicates strength—likely two pair, a set, or a straight themselves. Since I currently hold the nuts, I want to raise for value and to build the pot. I will raise to 2400, which is a 3x raise of her bet, setting up a potential shove on the river while extracting value from her strong-but-inferior range or preparing for a chop if she also holds a King.

I turned the Broadway (A‑K‑Q‑J‑T) with Ks7h — essentially the nut straight on this board. Nothing beats it on this runout; only any hand containing a King can tie. Flash’s large raise to 2400 after my 800 bet is aggressive, but he’s short‑stacked and his continuing range (Qx/Jx/Tx/Ax/pairs/weak two‑pairs) is almost entirely behind me. Raising here risks folding out marginal calling hands and turning him off; calling keeps worse hands in and lets him commit the rest if he shoves. I’ll call the extra 1600 (2400 total on the turn) and call any all‑in — folding is clearly wrong and an overbet raise could reduce my value against his likely calling range.

I have the Broadway straight (A–K–Q–J–T) with Ks7h on Qh Jh Td Ad Ac — an extremely strong hand. The only real dangers are full houses (pocket QQ/JJ/TT or combos containing an A+Q/A+J/A+T that make a full house with the paired aces) — and any King in Flash’s hand would simply tie. Flash’s line (button open, small flop raise/call, big turn reraise to 2400 then check on the paired river) is polarized: it can be a strong value hand or a blocker/induce line, but his short stack (≈3150) makes his turn raise aggressive and river check ambiguous. Checking back surrenders value to many worse hands (single Q/J/T pairs, ace-high, Kx that will tie), so I want to extract. I will make a modest value bet to keep worse hands in while giving him the option to commit if he truly has the nuts. A ~2,000 chip bet (~1/3 pot) maximizes calls from worse holdings without overfolding him; if he jams I’ll call his all‑in.

On the turn, I held the nut straight (Broadway). The river brings a second Ace, which pairs the board and creates potential full house combinations for my opponent (specifically AQ, AJ, AT, QQ, JJ, or TT). However, my straight still beats any Three of a Kind (any other Ax) and I chop with any other King. Minni's lead of 2000 into an 8000 pot represents a significant bet, but given the pot odds and the strength of a Broadway straight in heads-up play, I cannot fold. Since a raise would likely only be called by a full house that beats me or another straight resulting in a chop, calling is the most optimal move to see the showdown.

Heads-up on the button (I’m SB) with 3c6s — a marginal holding but perfectly playable in heads-up ranges. Limping is weak (gives opponent free flops and surrenders initiative); raising gets fold equity and makes better use of my positional advantage postflop. The big blind has ~6050 (≈60BB) effective, so a standard 2x BB open to 200 is cheap relative to potential reward and keeps the pot manageable if called. Plan: open-raise to 200 to take the initiative; fold to any sizeable 3-bet jam since 3-6 offsuit has poor equity vs a shove, and otherwise play the flop using position (c-bet or check based on texture).
Q8o is a standard defense in the Big Blind against a min-raise from the Small Blind. I am getting 3:1 odds on a call, and the hand has reasonable high-card value and playability post-flop.

On a paired board like 7-7-4, the big blind typically checks to the pre-flop aggressor. Queen-high has some showdown value but is not strong enough to lead into the pot. I will check and see how the small blind reacts.

I opened preflop to 200 with 3♣6♠ and BB called. Flop 7♥7♣4♠ is a paired, relatively dry texture. I have no pair but I do have a one-card straight (a 5 gives 3-4-5-6-7), so a little equity but nothing made. As the preflop aggressor heads-up I should continuation-bet frequently to capitalize on my wide opening range and extract folds from A/K-high and other air. I’ll bet small (≈1/3 pot) to apply pressure while keeping the pot manageable if called. If Flash raises significantly I’ll fold this marginal holding; if called I’ll re-evaluate on the turn (check for pot control on blanks, value/raise only if I actually hit the 5).

On a very dry, paired board like 7-7-4, my Queen-high has significant showdown value against a wide Small Blind range. Minni's bet of 150 into 400 is small, offering me excellent pot odds. Folding here would be too tight, as I may still have the best hand or can improve on later streets. I will call to see the turn.

On a board of 7-7-4-2, my Queen-high still retains some showdown value against a wide Small Blind opening range. The Deuce of diamonds is a complete brick that doesn't change the board texture. Having checked and called on the flop, the standard play is to check again to the aggressor. Leading here would turn my hand into a bluff unnecessarily, whereas checking allows me to potentially see a cheap river if Minni checks back or decide based on sizing if she bets.

I opened preflop and c-bet the paired flop, but on the turn (7♥7♣4♠2♦) my hand 3♣6♠ has no pair and only a 5 gives me a straight (4 outs ≈ 8–9%). Villain called the flop and now checks, so betting the turn will mostly be called by better hands and just bloats the pot with very little fold equity. With such minimal equity I prefer pot control heads‑up: check and either see a cheap river or fold to a large turn bet. This keeps the pot manageable and preserves chips for better spots.

The river brings an Ace, which is a significant card for the pre-flop aggressor's range. After the turn went check-check, my Queen-high has very little showdown value, but betting doesn't accomplish much as I'm unlikely to fold out better hands like an Ace or a pair. Checking allows me to reach showdown if Minni checks back or fold to a bet from a range that now includes many top-pair hands.

I have 3♣6♠ on 7♥7♣4♠2♦A♥ — I missed; my only realistic draw was a 5 (4 outs) which didn’t arrive. I was the preflop aggressor and c‑bet the dry paired flop, villain called and then checked the turn and river, showing a fairly passive line. I could attempt a river bluff representing an Ace, but Flash’s history is that he checks/calls down a lot postflop, so my fold equity is low and a bluff will likely be called by any decent pair or even many K/Q/J highs. Pot is only 700 and villain still has ~58BB, so there’s no need to bloat the pot with a very weak hand. Best play is pot control: check and fold to a value bet. If he checks behind I lose the small pot but keep chips for better spots; if he bets I’ll fold unless it’s a very tiny probe I believe is a pure bluff.

I am on the Button with 9c 5c, which is a suited semi-connector. With a stack of 64.5 big blinds, I am the short stack against Minni, but I still have plenty of fold equity and room for post-flop play. Raising to 300 has been my standard opening size throughout the tournament, and 95s is a reasonable hand to open for value and potential board coverage in a heads-up situation.

Heads-up vs a standard 3x open to 300 from the button. My hand 8♦4♥ is very weak (offsuit, disconnected) and has poor playability out of position. Calling 200 requires roughly ~33% equity to break even; 8-4o is rarely good enough against a reasonable opening range, and a 3-bet with this hand would be spewy. With deep stacks I can wait for much better, higher-expected-value spots and avoid marginal, dominated situations. Fold and preserve stack.

I’m on the button (small blind) with T♠7♠. Heads-up this is a good raising hand — suited, connected and playable postflop with position. Pot is 150 and I only need to call 50, but I prefer to seize initiative, build the pot with position, and generate fold equity against the big blind. Flash has ~65BB effective, so a standard heads-up open to ~3x BB keeps SPR manageable and gives me room to c-bet or double-barrel favorable textures. If Flash folds I pick up the blinds; if called I’ll c-bet on spade/connected flops and play aggressively in position; if faced with a large 3-bet I’ll re-evaluate (likely fold to very large 3-bets, call small ones sometimes to see a flop). Raise to 300 to apply pressure and leverage position.

In a heads-up situation, King-Ten offsuit is a very strong hand against a standard button opening range. Minni has a significant chip lead, so I want to play cautiously but profitably. Calling the raise allows me to see a flop with a hand that has good high-card value and straight potential, while keeping the pot manageable and hiding the strength of my hand. This prevents me from getting into a massive pre-flop confrontation where I might be forced to fold my equity against a 4-bet.

On a flop of 6h-9h-Qs, my Kd-Tc hand has some backdoor potential and a gutshot straight draw (a Jack would complete a straight). However, I am currently holding only King-high and am out of position against a very large stack. Checking is the standard play here to gauge Minni's aggression. If Minni bets, I will evaluate based on the sizing, but for now, checking allows me to see the next card as cheaply as possible or fold if the pressure is too high.

I opened preflop and have position with T♠7♠ on Q♠–9♥–6♥. I have no pair but I have the initiative, some backdoor spade equity and a hand that fairs better vs weaker parts of the BB’s defending range (many Kx, Jx, suited connectors). Flash called preflop fairly wide and will have a lot of hands that fold to pressure on this board, plus some Qx and heart draws that will continue. I will make a small continuation bet to extract folds and keep the pot controllable against a short-stacked opponent: 200 into a 600 pot (~1/3 pot). If he folds I take it down; if he calls I’ll re-evaluate on the turn (barrel selectively on spades/blank cards, pot‑control or fold to strong aggression on hearts/Queens or a check‑raise). This size preserves fold equity while limiting downside versus his calling/raising range.

On this flop of 6h-9h-Qs, I have King-high with a gutshot straight draw to the Jack. Minni has bet 200 into a pot of 600, giving me 4-to-1 odds to call. With a gutshot alone, I have about an 8% chance to hit on the turn, but considering the pot odds and the potential value of my overcards (King and Ten) if they pair up, as well as implied odds if I hit my straight, a call is profitable here. Folding would be too weak given the small bet sizing, and raising is unnecessary as I want to keep the pot manageable while out of position against the chip leader.

On the turn 5s, the board remains relatively coordinated with several draw possibilities (hearts and spades). I still hold King-high with a gutshot straight draw to a Jack. Being out of position against a massive chip leader like Minni, I should play cautiously. Checking is the most appropriate action here. It allows me to see how Minni reacts to the turn card. If Minni checks back, I get a free card to try and hit my straight or a King on the river. If Minni bets, I will have to evaluate the bet size against my pot odds and the likelihood that my King-high or gutshot has enough equity to continue.

Heads‑up on the button with T♠7♠. I c‑bet the flop (200) and was called; Flash then checked the turn. The 5♠ is a perfect card to continue — it’s a “blank” for rank but it adds a spade to the board, giving me a 4‑to‑a‑flush (one spade away) plus a handful of pair/straight outs. Facing a check from a wide, calling BB range, a turn semi‑bluff both represents spades/top pair and extracts folds from Kx/Jx/weak pairs and many floating hands. My equity if called (≈19.6% to hit the flush on the river, plus pair/straight outs) is in the ~35–40% range versus a generic calling range, so a bet here is justified. I choose a sizable but non‑committing size — 700 into a 1000 pot (~70%) — to maximize fold equity while preserving the option to fold to a large shove. Plan: take it down now if he folds; if he calls, use position and my draw to decide on the river; if he check‑raises or jams I will fold this turn semi‑bluff (I don’t have the odds to call a large shove).

On the turn, the board is 6h-9h-Qs-5s. Minni has fired a second barrel of 700 into a 1000-chip pot. I hold King-high (Kd-Tc) with a gutshot straight draw (needing a Jack). The pot odds are roughly 2.4-to-1, meaning I need about 29% equity to call. However, a gutshot only hits about 8.7% of the time on the river. Even if I include my overcards as potential outs, the King might not be good against Minni's range which includes many Queens, and the Ten only gives me third pair. Given the high bet sizing and the fact that I am out of position against a massive chip leader, my equity is insufficient to continue. Folding is the most disciplined and mathematically sound play here to preserve my stack.

Holding Ace-Jack offsuit (AJo) on the button in a heads-up situation is a premium hand. With a stack of 60 big blinds, I want to play this aggressively to build a pot and capitalize on my positional advantage. A standard 3x open is appropriate here to narrow the range or take the blinds and antes, while still being committed to playing a large pot if called.

Heads-up vs a button open to 300 (from SB). I hold J♥8♥ — a suited, connected hand that performs well in heads-up pots and has good equity against a wide opening range. Pot is 400 and I need to call 200 more; calling 200 to win ~600 gives ~3:1 pot odds (≈25% equity needed), which J8s comfortably meets vs a reasonable open. I’ll call to see a flop rather than 3-bet small and inflate the pot out of position; if I connect (pair, two pair, flush/straight draw) I can apply pressure, otherwise I can control pot size and fold to large aggression. Call 200 (total commitment 300).

Check. I hit second pair (8♥) on a very wet, three‑club flop (Qc 8c 4c). Flash opened wide from the SB preflop, so his range contains lots of Qx, pocket pairs and two‑club combos that either already have the flush or strongly favor turn/river. I’m out of position and my hand is vulnerable to clubs and top pairs, so I want to control the pot rather than bloating it with a marginal holding. By checking I keep his bluffs and thin value bets in range — I can call a small/medium c‑bet to realize equity and re‑evaluate on the turn, but fold to large overbets/raises that polarize to nutted hands. If he checks back I can take a free card and continue to pot‑control.

On this monotone board of Qc 8c 4c, I hold the Jc, giving me the fourth-nut flush draw. While I have the range advantage as the pre-flop aggressor, monotone boards are highly polarizing. Minni's check is standard. By checking back, I control the size of the pot and allow myself to realize the equity of my flush draw and overcards for free. Betting might force out hands I beat while getting called by better made hands or higher flush draws. Checking back also keeps my range protected.

Check. I have J♥8♥ — second pair with a decent kicker but the board is highly hostile (Qc 8c 4c with a 2♦ turn): three clubs on board and lots of Qx/club‑draws in a wide SB opening range. Flash checked the flop, so his range includes many top pairs, medium pairs and club draws; betting out OOP with a vulnerable, marginal hand just bloats the pot and forces uncomfortable decisions if he shows aggression (and he’s short‑stacked enough to jam). By checking I control the pot, keep his bluffs and thin value hands in, and can call a small turn bet to realize equity or fold to a polarized raise. If he checks back I get a free river to improve. This line maximizes long‑term value while avoiding committing too many chips with a second pair on a flushy board.

On the turn 2d, the board remains monotone from the flop. I have Ace-high and the Jc, which is a strong flush draw. Minni has checked twice, suggesting she likely doesn't have a made flush or a strong Queen, though she could be trapping. By checking back again, I am exercising pot control and ensuring I see a free river card. This allows me to realize the equity of my flush draw and my overcards without being forced to fold to a check-raise. If the river is a club or an Ace, I will likely have the best hand. If it's a blank, Ace-high still has some showdown value against her checking range.

River: I hold J♥8♥ (second pair) on Qc 8c 4c 2d 7h. Pot is 600 and Flash (SB) has checked through every street, which leaves a lot of missed club draws, small pairs and weak unpaired hands in his range. My 8s has decent showdown value vs that passive range but is behind any Qx, sets or club flushes. I want to extract thin value without bloating the pot or turning the hand into a cooler if he shows strength. A small value bet (~1/3 pot) will get called by worse (small pairs, some Jx, missed draws) while letting me fold comfortably to a large raise. If he jams or puts significant pressure I’ll fold — this is a probe for thin value. Bet 200 chips (≈1/3 pot).

On the river, the board is Qc 8c 4c 2d 7h. I have Ace-high with the Jc. Minni has checked the flop and turn, but leads for 200 into a 600 pot on the river. This small sizing often indicates a thin value bet with a marginal pair (like a 4, 7, or 8) or a weak Queen, trying to get a call from Ace-high. Since I have no pair and my Jc blocks some of her possible missed club draws that I might have beaten, Ace-high is unlikely to be the best hand here. Calling 200 to win 800 requires 20% equity, but against a range that checked twice and then led, Ace-high rarely holds up. I will fold and preserve my stack.

Heads-up on the button (I’m SB) with Ac Jd — a very strong heads-up hand: top-ace with a good kicker and decent postflop playability. I’ve already posted 50 and need to call 50, but raising to seize initiative and build the pot is standard here. Flash (BB) has ~57bb effective, so a 3x-ish raise applies pressure without overcommitting and keeps worse aces and broadways in. Min-raising to 200 is too small and invites easier exploitation; I’ll raise to 300 total to get value and fold equity. Plan: continue as aggressor postflop with c-bets on many textures; if I face a large 3-bet re-evaluate based on sizing and opponent tendencies.

I am in the Big Blind with K8o. Minni, who has a significant chip lead, has raised to 300 from the Button (Small Blind). Calling costs 200 more into a pot of 400, giving me 3-to-1 odds. K8o is a standard defending hand in a heads-up scenario against a button raise, especially given the pot odds and the fact that folding too often would allow the chip leader to exploit me. I will call to see a flop and evaluate based on board texture.

I have flopped top pair (Kings) with an 8-high flush draw on a monotone board of hearts. This is a strong hand, but it is also vulnerable to higher flushes or sets. Being out of position against the chip leader, checking is the most prudent move to gauge her interest in the pot and control the size of the bet. If she bets, I have an easy call given my top pair and redraw to the flush.

I was the preflop aggressor (raised to 300) and flopped top pair — Jacks — with the best possible kicker (Ace). The board (Jh Kh 2h) is monotone hearts, so Flash’s calling-range contains a lot of heart draws and some Kx or better hands that can currently beat me. Despite the risk, this is a spot to continue as aggressor: betting extracts value from worse pairs, Jx and many draws, and protects my hand against free equity outs. I’ll c-bet ~2/3 pot to charge flush draws and get value from weaker holdings while keeping fold equity (~400 into a 600 pot). If he raises, I’ll re-evaluate (likely call small/medium raises to see a turn but proceed cautiously vs large polarized raises).

I have flopped top pair with an eight-high flush draw. While the monotone board is dangerous, my hand is too strong to fold to a single bet. Calling is the best option here to control the pot size and see a turn card, as raising might commit me against a better flush or a set from the chip leader.

I currently have top pair (Kings) with an 8-high flush draw. The 6 of diamonds on the turn is a blank that doesn't change the board state significantly. As the first to act post-flop in this heads-up match, I will check to control the pot size. My hand has strong showdown value, but the monotone flop makes it vulnerable to made flushes or sets in the chip leader's range. Checking allows me to see if Minni continues her aggression or takes a free card, and I intend to call most reasonable bet sizes.

I have A♣J♦ on Jh Kh 2h 6d — top pair with the best kicker, and I was the preflop/flop aggressor. The flop is monotone hearts which gives my opponent lots of 4‑card heart draws and a smaller number of made flushes (only if he held two hearts). Flash’s flop call and turn check is ambiguous (pot‑control, a draw, or a made hand like Kx/pair). The 6♦ is a fairly blank turn that doesn’t improve obvious draws.

Given my huge overall stack and the fact I’m still ahead of a sizeable portion of his calling range (Qx, Jx, lower pairs, one‑heart draws), I want to apply pressure to fold out medium-strength hands (Kx that might prefer pot‑control, second pairs, etc.) and charge the one‑heart draws while keeping the pot manageable if I’m behind to a made two‑heart flush. A turn bet around ~60% pot makes sense: it extracts value from worse hands and folds a chunk of marginal holdings, while leaving room to fold to a large check‑raise. If he check‑raises very large I’ll re‑evaluate (likely fold to a polarizing shove since I’m behind made flushes and many Kx combos); I’ll call smaller raises and proceed cautiously.

Decision: continue as aggressor and bet ~60–65% of the pot to charge draws and fold out marginal hands.

I am holding top pair (Kings) with an 8-high flush draw on a board showing three hearts. While the monotone texture is concerning, folding top pair plus a flush draw in a heads-up match is too exploitable, especially given that Minni's bet of 900 into a 1,400 pot offers me nearly 2.5-to-1 pot odds (requiring roughly 28% equity). My hand currently beats all of Minni's bluffs, any Jack, and smaller Kings. Additionally, I have 5 outs to improve to a flush and 2-3 outs for trips or two-pair that could be the best hand. Calling allows me to control the pot size and see a river card without committing my entire stack while out of position against the chip leader.

On the river, the board shows three hearts (Kh, Jh, 2h) and two diamonds (6d, Td). I hold a pair of Kings (Kd) with an 8h kicker. While top pair is a strong hand in heads-up play, the board is quite coordinated, making a flush, two-pair, or a straight (AQ) possible for Minni. I have checked to Minni on every street, and the Ten of Diamonds on the river doesn't improve my hand. Checking again is the most prudent move, allowing me to see if Minni checks back with a worse hand or attempts a bluff, at which point I will have to evaluate her bet size to decide if a call is profitable. Given my stack size and her chip lead, I want to reach a showdown as cheaply as possible.

Board: Jh Kh 2h 6d Td. I hold A♣J♦ — top pair, best kicker. I was the aggressor (bet flop 400, bet turn 900) and Flash passively called both streets. His line (call/call, then check river) is consistent with a lot of Kx, single‑heart draws that missed or medium pairs — and two‑heart combos (made flush) are possible but would often have shown more aggression. On the river there are relatively few worse hands that will confidently call a value bet (maybe some Jx or strange Tx combos), while many of the hands that will call a river bet (Kx, made hearts) actually beat me. Flash still has ~4.2k behind so any bet-sized attempt to extract will often price in calls or shoves from hands that dominate me. With thin value and a high frequency of beating combos in his calling range, the +EV line is to avoid bloating the pot and take showdown: check and see his action. If he now leads out big I’ll re-evaluate (call only if the shove frequency and sizing make it correct), but given the line so far I’ll check to showdown.

In a heads-up situation, $Q\diamond 5\diamond$ is a strong enough hand to open from the Button/Small Blind. Raising to 300 allows me to take the initiative and potentially win the blinds or see a flop with a hand that has decent playability and flush potential.

I'm in the BB with 9♠7♥ facing a button open to 300. Pot is 400; I need 200 more to call (my total committed this street will be 300). Preflop I have decent equity against a wide heads-up opening range and the call gives me ~3:1 pot odds (need ~25% equity). I'm out of position, this is a marginal offsuit hand, and Flash has shown frequent aggression — I don't want to 3-bet light and get into a bloated pot OOP or face a shove/4-bet. With a deep personal stack I can comfortably see a flop, fold to heavy action on bad boards, and extract value or apply pressure on good ones. So I will defend and see the flop.

I have 9♠7♥ on Tc 3h 9d — middle pair with a weak kicker and I’m out of position. Flash is a frequent, wide aggressor who will c-bet this texture a lot. This is a dry board where my hand has decent showdown value versus his bluffs and worse one-pair combos, but I don’t want to inflate the pot OOP with only a marginal made hand. Checking lets him barrel air or weaker hands so I can call reasonably-sized continuation bets and re-evaluate on the turn. Plan: check/call small-to-medium c-bets to realize equity and fold to very large polarizing overbets that represent tens/sets, and avoid thin check-raising here.

On a flop of T-3-9 rainbow, I have Queen-high and a backdoor diamond flush draw. As the pre-flop aggressor, a continuation bet is a standard play to take down the pot immediately, as this board doesn't perfectly hit a standard Big Blind defending range. A half-pot bet of 300 should be sufficient to fold out weaker hands and allow me to maintain the initiative.

I call 300. I have middle pair (9♠) with a weak kicker on a pretty dry T♣ 3♥ 9♦ board and I’m out of position. Flash is a frequent/aggressive c-bettor who will have a lot of air and overcards in this spot, and the pot (900) vs 300 to call gives ~3:1 pot odds (~25% equity needed) so calling to realize equity and keep the pot controlled is correct. I don’t want to inflate the pot OOP with a marginal hand by check-raising or leading; plan is to call, see the turn, and fold to a large polarizing raise or continue vs moderate turn bets to extract value vs worse pairs/bluffs.

I have middle pair (9♠) with a weak kicker on a relatively dry T♣-3♥-9♦-6♣ board and I’m out of position. Flash is a wide, aggressive opener who c-bet the flop but checked the turn — that check looks like weakness or pot-control rather than a strong value line. Checking behind surrenders initiative and allows him to bluff or take the lead on the river. A small value/protection stab denies equity to floats and club/straight draws, extracts from worse pairs and many Tx/9x/air combos, and keeps the pot manageable OOP. I’ll bet about one-third pot to get value while minimizing commitment. Plan: fold to a large check-raise (represents straights/sets); call a normal single bet or small raise and re-evaluate on the river if called.

On a board of Tc-3h-9d-6c, I currently hold Queen-high with no direct draw. Minni has led into me for 400 (a 'donk lead') after I was the aggressor pre-flop and on the flop. This action often indicates that the opponent has either improved (perhaps hitting a straight with 8-7, or making two pair/trips) or is semi-bluffing with a draw and wants to set their own price. While the pot odds are 4:1 (400 to call a 2000 total pot), my Queen-high has very little equity and limited outs to improve to a winning hand. Even if a Queen or Five falls on the river, it may not be the best hand if Minni has a Ten or a straight. Given my stack size relative to the leader, it is more prudent to fold here and wait for a better spot rather than calling with a marginal holding in a pot where I have no showdown value.

Heads-up on the button (I act last postflop) — huge positional advantage. T♠5♥ is a workable heads-up hand and belongs in an opening/steal range versus the big blind, especially given Flash’s frequent preflop aggression and wide-looking ranges from previous hands. Effective stacks: Flash ~6,700 (≈67 BB) while I have a large stack; I can apply pressure without overcommitting. I’ll raise to the minimum open (200 total) to pick up the blinds often and to see the flop with position while keeping the pot controllable. Plan: fold this hand to a large 3-bet (it’s marginal vs heavy preflop aggression), but if Flash calls I’ll use positional advantage to c-bet good flops or float and realize equity.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Seven offsuit. Minni, who currently holds a significant chip lead, has min-raised from the Small Blind/Button. In a heads-up or short-handed situation, Ace-Seven is a strong hand. I am getting 3-to-1 odds to call (100 to win a total pot of 300). While I could consider a 3-bet to take control of the hand, calling allows me to see a flop and keep the pot manageable given the stack disparity. I will call to see the flop.

I am out of position in the Big Blind after calling a pre-flop raise from the button. My hand is currently Ace-high with a backdoor nut flush draw on a Queen-high board. In this situation, the standard play is to check to the pre-flop aggressor. Leading out (donking) would likely be met with a raise or a call, putting me in a difficult spot. By checking, I can gauge Minni's aggression. If Minni bets, I can decide whether to call based on the bet size, given my Ace-high has some showdown value and potential to improve.

Heads-up on the button I opened to 200 and got called — flop Q♠ 5♦ 2♠ gives me a pair of fives with a T♠ blocker to some spade combos. Versus Flash’s wide calling range this is a good spot to c-bet for thin value and to charge spade draws/overcards that picked up no pair. I don’t want to bloat the pot with a medium-strength hand, so I’ll make a standard ~1/2-pot c-bet (200). If he folds I take the pot; if he calls I’ll re-evaluate on the turn (but plan to proceed cautiously vs aggression and fold to a large check-raise).

I have Ace-high with the Ace of Spades, providing a backdoor nut flush draw on a Queen-high board. Minni's bet of 200 into a 400 pot is a small continuation bet, giving me 3:1 odds to call. In a heads-up situation, Ace-high still retains significant equity against a wide range of hands that Minni could be opening from the button and betting on the flop. I will call to see the turn, hoping to either improve to top pair or pick up additional equity like a flush draw.

The turn Seven of Hearts has improved my hand to second pair (Sevens) with an Ace kicker.
