Given that I am out of position against a very large stack, my priority is pot control while also allowing my opponent to continue bluffing if they have air or a draw. Checking is the most strategic move here; it keeps the pot manageable and protects my range. If Minni bets, I have a clear call given the strength of second pair in a heads-up pot. If she checks behind, I likely have the best hand going into the river.

Check. I hold T♠5♥ — middle pair (pair of fives) with a weak kicker on Q♠ 5♦ 2♠ 7♥. I c-bet the flop and was called; Flash’s check on the turn looks more like weakness than strength (could be a wide float or a marginal hand), and the 7♥ is a relatively harmless card. My T♠ also blocks some two-spade combos, so there’s limited need to bluff-protect. With position and a medium-strength hand I prefer pot control: check to see the river, induce potential bluffs, and avoid bloating the pot vs hands that currently beat me (Qx, trips, 7x). If he leads large on the river I’ll fold; if he bets small I’ll call for value or raise selectively depending on the card and sizing.

I have Trip Sevens on a board that ended with a potential spade flush. Crucially, I hold the Ace of Spades, which is the nut flush blocker. Minni checked behind on the turn, suggesting she likely does not have a monster like a full house (QQ, 55, or 22) or she is slow-playing. Since the river paired the Seven, my hand is very strong in a heads-up pot. I will lead out with a value bet of 400 (half pot) to target her Queens or mid-range pocket pairs that might check back if I check. My Ace blocker also makes it safer to bet, as I know she cannot have the nut flush.

Flash's river lead (400 into a 1200 pot) is a small probe — likely a mix of thin value and bluffs given his line (called flop, checked turn). I hold T♠5♥: a medium-strength hand (pair of fives) with a spade blocker that reduces some flush combos. Pot math is simple and favorable: calling 400 to win a ~2000 pot requires only ~20% equity. Opponent's betting range includes Qx and 7x and completed spades that beat me, but also many missed broadways and floaters that I beat. Raising would polarize me and is unnecessary with a marginal value hand. With good pot odds, position earlier in the hand, and a wide/aggressive opponent, the +EV play is to call and get value from worse hands and induce bluffs.

In heads-up play, while many hands are playable from the button, 9h 3s is significantly below the threshold of a profitable opening hand. Given my stack size of 7,550 (approx 75 BB) relative to Minni's 32,300, I should look for better opportunities to commit chips rather than playing extremely weak holdings out of position post-flop or risking a 3-bet from a massive stack. Folding is the disciplined play here.

I’m on the button (small blind) with T♥6♦ in a heads-up spot. Against a single big blind I should be opening a wide range — I have position postflop and this hand has reasonable playability. A min-raise to 200 applies pressure, collects the dead money, and preserves the pot size so I can fold to a strong 3-bet without risking a large portion of my deep stack. If called I’ll use position to c-bet favorable flops or control the pot on dry boards. Raising to 200 is the standard, low-variance steal attempt here.

K8o is a strong hand for defending the big blind in a heads-up situation, especially against a min-raise. I am getting excellent pot odds (3:1) to see a flop. My previous encounters with Minni show that they are active from the button, and I have successfully defended and won pots with similar high-card strength recently.

On a board of 9c-9h-8d, I have hit bottom pair (Eights) with a King kicker. This is a very strong hand in a heads-up pot, especially as the Big Blind defender against a Small Blind/Button raise. The board is paired with Nines, which reduces the likelihood that Minni has a Nine. I will check to allow Minni to continue their aggression with air or weaker hands, planning to check-call or potentially check-raise depending on the sizing.

I opened from the button and the big blind just called. The flop (9c 9h 8d) misses my T6 but I have decent equity: three tens + four sevens = 7 clean outs (~28% to improve to the river). Heads-up the caller’s range is wide, so a small continuation bet can fold out many unpaired hands and lets me realize equity cheaply when called. I’ll make a modest c‑bet (≈1/3 pot) to apply pressure while keeping the pot controllable. If I face significant aggression (a raise) I’ll fold this weak hand; if I get called I’ll proceed cautiously on the turn (check back blanks, continue or shove only on very favorable runouts like a 7 or a T).

On a paired board of 9-9-8, my hand (two pair, Nines and Eights with a King kicker) is very strong in a heads-up situation. Minni's lead of 150 into a 400 pot is a standard continuation bet that could come from a wide range of hands, including air, overcards, or smaller pairs. While I could raise for protection against straight draws like 6-7 or J-10, calling is the superior play here to keep Minni's bluffs in their range and prevent bloating the pot while I am out of position. I am getting roughly 3.6:1 on a call, making this a clear continue.

On the turn, the Six of Spades is a relatively safe card, though it does connect slightly with some straight draws like 7-5 or 10-7. My hand, Nines and Eights with a King kicker, remains a strong bluff-catcher and a marginal value hand in a heads-up pot. Leading out might fold out weaker hands that I want to keep in the pot and would likely only get called by better hands (like a Nine or a higher pocket pair). By checking, I am controlling the pot size and allowing Minni to continue betting with semi-bluffs or air, which I plan to call.

Turn pairs my 6 (T6 → pair of sixes) on a very textured board 9c 9h 8d 6s. The 6 completes a bunch of flop draws (5-7, 7-10, pocket-sixes, etc.) and Flash’s flop call left a range containing trips/nines, 8x, underpairs and many turn-completing draws. Flash checked the turn, which looks like pot-control or a check‑call line rather than folding out. My pair is weak vs the portion of his range that beat me and vulnerable to shoves/raises from him. Betting now simply bloats the pot and creates tough decisions vs aggression from an opponent with a 7.2k effective stack. With position and a marginal made hand I should control the pot and see the river — check and reevaluate on the river (call small river bets for value, fold to big river jams unless I have a clear read).

The river is the Queen of Spades, which is a potential scare card as it completes some air or overcards Minni might have checked on the turn. My hand, Nines and Eights with a King kicker, remains a solid showdown hand. Minni's check on the turn suggests they likely do not have a Nine or a strong overpair. By checking the river, I am controlling the size of the pot and allowing Minni to check back with marginal hands or attempt a bluff with missed draws like JT or 75. If I bet, I risk folding out their bluffs while only being called by hands that have me beaten, such as a Queen that improved on the river or a slow-played Nine. Checking is the most balanced approach here.

Heads-up on a very paired, narrow board (9c 9h 8d 6s Qs). I improved to a weak pair (6s) on the turn, but this is still a marginal showdown hand — many hands in Flash’s calling line (9x trips, 88/full house, Qx, and some underpairs) beat me. Flash’s line (call preflop, call my small c‑bet on the flop, check turn and check river) reads as pot‑control or slow‑play rather than a pure bluff. Betting here only gains thin value from a few Qx or worse pairs but also exposes me to a shove from a much shorter-stacked opponent that I can’t profitably call. With a small pot (700) relative to stack sizes and a marginal hand, I prefer to control the pot and take it to showdown rather than risking a large fold or a difficult call. I’ll check and show down; if Flash bets a small river bet I can call for thin value, but I will fold to a large river shove.

In a heads-up situation, Ten-Deuce suited is a marginal hand. Given that I am significantly out-chipped by Minni (approx 1:4 ratio) and we are only 55 hands into the 150-hand match, I prefer to fold these very weak suited hands from the small blind to avoid playing a bloated pot out of position or against a chip leader who can easily apply pressure. I will wait for better spots to commit chips.

Heads-up on the button I should be opening very wide — T8o is perfectly fine to raise. Limping is passive and lets the BB see a cheap flop; a small 2x BB open (to 200 total) applies pressure, picks up the blinds often, and keeps the pot manageable if Flash fights back. With 32k vs his ~7.7k I can comfortably play postflop in position or call a reasonable 3-bet and re-evaluate. I raise to seize initiative and exploit position/fold equity.

I am in the Big Blind with 9-4 offsuit, which is a very weak hand. Minni has raised to 200 from the Small Blind. While I am getting 3:1 odds to call (100 to call into a 300 pot), 9-4 offsuit plays very poorly out of position and is likely dominated by most of Minni's opening range. Given the significant stack disadvantage (7,750 vs 31,950), I should avoid bleeding chips in marginal spots with low-equity hands. Folding is the most prudent play to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

I am holding Ten of Spades and Trey of Hearts in the Small Blind. This is a very weak hand (T3o) with no connectivity and poor post-flop potential. Given the current stack sizes, where Minni holds a significant lead, and the fact that I have 77 big blinds, there is no reason to risk chips on a sub-par hand. Folding is the most disciplined play here.

Heads-up on the button with 4♠6♠ — a suited, playable hand in position. Folding is too tight and limping/calling 50 surrenders initiative to the BB; I want to seize the initiative, build the pot when I have position and both backdoor spade/straight equity, and deny the BB free or cheap flops. Min raise is 200 but I’ll size to 300 (3x) to be a standard open that gives fold equity and reduces light 3-bets. If Flash 3-bets large I’ll let this hand go; if he calls I’ll play aggressively on favorable flops (c-bet + leverage my position) and evaluate based on texture and his actions.

I am in the Big Blind with Ten-Eight offsuit. Minni, who currently holds a significant chip lead, has raised to 300 from the Small Blind/Button. In a heads-up dynamic, Ten-Eight offsuit is a reasonable hand to defend against a standard open. I am getting 2-to-1 odds to call (200 more to win a total pot of 600). Given my stack size of 76 BBs, I have enough room to play post-flop without being committed. I will call to see the flop and evaluate.

The flop is Ace-Nine-Nine. This board texture significantly favors Minni's pre-flop raising range, as she is more likely to hold an Ace than I am. I have Ten-high with no pair and no real draw. In this situation, the best play is to check to the aggressor. If she bets, I will likely have to fold unless the bet size is extremely small and I feel like floating, but against a chip leader, discretion is the better part of valor with absolute air. I will check and see how she responds.

Flop A♦ 9♠ 9♣; I hold 4♠6♠ — I completely missed this board and only have a two-street (runner-runner) spade backdoor. Flash called preflop and checked the flop, and his defending range will contain a lot of A‑x, 9‑x and pockets that will either call or slowplay a c-bet. Betting here as a bluff has limited fold equity and would often bloat the pot when I have almost no showdown equity. I have position and deep stacks, so the correct line is pot control: check and see the turn for free. If he fires a small turn I can float and try to take it away later; if he fires a large, polarized bet I can fold and preserve chips. If he checks back the turn I can consider a delayed c‑bet on a favorable card or bluff the river when appropriate. For now: check.

I have turned a pair of Tens on a board of A-9-9-T. While this is an improvement, the board remains dangerous with an Ace and a pair of Nines present. Minni checked back on the flop, suggesting she might not have the Ace, but she could still be holding a Nine or a Ten herself, or simply taking a pot-control line with a medium-strength hand. By checking here, I maintain pot control with my medium-strength hand and allow Minni to either check behind or bluff, providing me with more information for the river.

Check. With 4♠6♠ on A♦9♠9♣T♦ I have essentially no showdown equity — the diamond turn kills the backdoor spade (runner-runner) and I have missed entirely. Flash checked flop and checked the turn, so his range is full of A‑x, 9‑x and medium/overpairs that will rarely fold to a probe bet; betting here would bloat the pot with very low fold equity. I’m in position and stacks are deep, so the lowest-variance, highest-expected-value line is pot control: check and see the river. Plan: if he fires a small bet on the river I can float and plan a targeted bluff or call if I pair; if he barrels a large, polarized bet I’ll fold and preserve chips; if he checks back I’ll check down unless the river gives me real value (pair).

On a board of A-9-9-T-Q, my pair of Tens is a medium-strength hand. Minni checked back on both the flop and the turn, which typically indicates she doesn't have a strong Ace or a Nine. However, the Queen on the river is a scare card that could have easily improved her range (any Qx or KJ for a straight). By checking, I am looking to go to a cheap showdown and capture the pot if she was holding a smaller pocket pair or air. Betting likely won't get called by worse hands, and I don't want to get raised off my marginal showdown value.

Board: Ad 9s 9c Td Qs. I hold 4s6s — no pair, backdoor spade missed and essentially zero showdown equity versus reasonable calling ranges. Flash checked the flop and turn and checked the river as well; that line is most consistent with A-x, 9-x (trips/slowplays) or medium/overpairs that will rarely fold to a river bluff. The Q on the river actually improves many of his plausible hands (Qx) and does not help me. The pot is only 600, so a bluff would need an unreal fold frequency to be +EV and would just bloat a pot I’m unlikely to win at showdown. I’ll control the pot and preserve chips for better spots — check and fold to a value bet (only consider calling a tiny bet with a very specific read that he’s overbluffing).

I am on the button with 4-3 offsuit, which is one of the weakest possible starting hands. Given the current stack dynamics where Minni has a significant lead, there is no reason to play such a marginal hand and risk losing more chips. Folding is the most disciplined play here to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

Heads-up on the button with K♠8♥ — a playable hand. I’m in position postflop (button/SB acts last after the flop) and can open a wide range. Calling 50 to just see a flop is passive and gives the BB the initiative; raising to the minimum (to 200) seizes initiative, gains fold equity and lets me play postflop from position. The raise is a small, low-risk investment relative to my 32,000 stack and simplifies postflop play. Plan: fold to a large 3-bet, continue aggressively on dry flops (c-bet) and value top-pair Kx; check/control on obvious Ace or very coordinated boards. Raise to 200 to open the pot and use positional advantage.

I am in the Big Blind with 4 of Clubs and 2 of Clubs. Minni, the chip leader, has raised to 200 from the Button/Small Blind. This is a minimum raise (2x), and I only need to call 100 more to see the flop. The pot is offering me 3-to-1 odds. While 42s is a very weak hand, in a heads-up situation with these pot odds, it is generally worth seeing a flop to look for a flush draw, straight draw, or two pair. I will call the raise but remain prepared to fold post-flop if I don't improve significantly, especially given Minni's large stack advantage.

I have flopped the absolute nuts at this moment: a 5-high straight (the wheel, A-2-3-4-5). Additionally, I hold a flush draw (four clubs to a flush). Given that I am out of position against Minni, who was the pre-flop aggressor, the most effective play to maximize value is to check. This allows Minni to follow through with a continuation bet, which I can then check-raise to build a larger pot. With a massive stack disadvantage against the chip leader, I need to capitalize on strong hands like this to double up.

Flop is A♠ 5♣ 3♣ and I hold K♠8♥. I opened preflop, but this is an “obvious Ace” board and I have very weak showdown equity (no pair, no club backdoor). C-betting here would be a very thin bluff and will be called by most Ax, pocket pairs and any club draws — I should avoid bloating the pot out of position. I’ll check to control the pot, keep weaker hands and bluffs in the opponent’s range, call small continuation bets, and look to realize equity or bluff later if a favorable turn (e.g. K) appears. If Flash leads large I can fold; I’ll only continue vs reasonable sizing.

I currently hold the nuts with a 5-high straight (the wheel) on a board of As-5c-3c-9h. Additionally, I have a flush draw with the 4c and 2c. On the flop, both I and Minni checked. Since Minni was the pre-flop aggressor and checked back on an Ace-high board, it's possible she is either very weak or slow-playing. I will check again on the turn to continue the trap, hoping to induce a bluff or a bet from a marginal hand that she might have been cautious with on the flop. If she checks back again, I will lead out on the river to ensure I get some value from this monster hand.

I hold K♠8♥ on A♠-5♣-3♣-9♥. I opened preflop but the flop was an “obvious Ace” so I checked to control the pot; both checked again on the turn. I have no pair and no club backdoor, only 6 outs to pair (~13% to hit on the river). Pot is 400, effective stacks are huge relative to the pot (my 31,850 vs Flash’s 7,750), so a turn bluff has very thin fold equity against a checking/calling range that includes Ax and pocket pairs. Betting here risks bloating the pot when I’m behind and gains little value when I’m ahead. With position and low equity, optimal play is to keep the pot small and check to the river, preserving the option to bluff only on a favorable river runout or to value-bet if I pair up. If Flash leads into me on the river I’ll fold to large, call small, and look for a K/8 improvement to extract value. Action: check.

I have the nuts (a 5-high straight) on a board of As-5c-3c-9h-4h. Both players have checked the flop and the turn. Since the pot is currently very small (400), I need to bet to extract some value from any Ace, pocket pair, or a potential '2' in Minni's hand that would result in a chop. Given the passive action so far, a bet of 300 (75% of the pot) is a standard value bet that can be called by a wide range of hands or even potentially raised if Minni is holding a '2' or trying to represent the straight.

I checked flop and turn to control the pot with K♠8♥ and no pair. On the river Flash bets 300 into a 700 pot — calling 300 gives me pot odds of 300/(700+300) ≈ 30%. My hand is static king‑high and loses to any Ace and any made pair; villain’s line (call preflop, check flop and turn, small river bet) looks much more like thin value (Ax, small pairs) or a small probe than a wide bluff range. The bluff frequency required to make a call profitable is higher than I’m willing to assign here. Raising would be a pure bluff with little fold equity and unnecessary risk given my deep stack. Fold and preserve chips for better spots.
In heads-up play, Jack-Seven offsuit is a reasonable hand to play from the button. I will raise to 300 to exert positional pressure and attempt to win the blinds or see a flop with the initiative.

Heads-up vs the button (Flash) who opened to 300 from the SB. I hold 2♠6♠ — very weak but suited. I’ll be out of position postflop (I’ll act first), so I don’t want to inflate the pot by 3-betting light against an opponent who looks sticky/aggressive. Calling an extra 200 (300 total committed including my BB) is cheap to see a flop, lets me realize equity and keep pot control. I’ll fold to heavy turn/river aggression on dry, unfavorable boards and only continue when I hit a pair, good draws, or can leverage position with a strong read. Therefore: call 300.

Heads‑up on the flop I have 2♠6♠ on 8♦‑2♦‑A♠: bottom pair with a very weak kicker and only a backdoor spade/fold‑both spades flush possibility (needs both turn and river). I’m out of position and Flash is a sticky/aggressive opener who will often continue with Ax, pairs or floats. I don’t want to bloat the pot with a marginal hand that is easily dominated by any Ace or many pairs. The correct line is pot control — check and see what Flash does. If he fires a small c‑bet I’ll call to realize equity and re‑evaluate on the turn; if he barrels big or raises I’ll fold. If he checks back I get a free turn to improve or to take a stab later. Therefore: check to keep the pot small and avoid committing chips with a vulnerable, low‑value hand.

I am the pre-flop aggressor and the board has an Ace, which favors my perceived range significantly. Minni checked to me, which is standard. Although I have nothing but Jack-high, a continuation bet here is likely to take down the pot immediately if Minni missed the flop. I will bet 300 (half the pot) to exert pressure.

I have 2♠6♠ on 8♦‑2♦‑A♠: bottom pair (deuces) with a very weak kicker and only a backdoor spade possibility. Flash is a sticky/aggressive SB opener who just fired a small c‑bet (~1/3 pot). This is a cheap bet to call to realize equity and keep the pot under control out of position. Folding is too tight given the pot odds and the chance to improve; raising would bloat the pot with a marginal hand and give him easy ways to shove back. I’ll call the 300 to see the turn and re‑evaluate — fold to large turn/river aggression on dry cards, proceed if I improve or get a favorable action.

I have 2♠6♠ on 8♦‑2♦‑A♠‑T♦: second pair (pair of deuces) with a weak 6 kicker and no diamonds. There are only two outs to trips (very small equity), and the turn completes a three‑diamond board meaning only opponents holding two diamonds in hand now have a flush. Flash opened and c‑bet small on the flop but checked the dangerous turn — that line can represent pot‑control with an Ace/pair or a slow‑played strong hand trying to induce a bet.

I’m out of position with a marginal pair and a very low chance to improve. Betting here bloats the pot and can get check‑raised or called by hands that currently dominate me; checking keeps the pot manageable and lets me see the river for 2 outs or fold to real strength. Plan: check and re‑evaluate on the river — call small river bets only with improvement or reasonable showdown value, fold to large aggression unless I hit trips/full. Therefore: check.

On the turn, the board now shows three diamonds (8d, 2d, Td) and I hold the seven of diamonds (7d), giving me a four-flush. Minni has checked to me for a second time. While I could attempt to represent a completed flush or a strong Ace with a bet, Minni's call on the flop suggests she has a piece of the board or her own draw. Checking back is the most prudent play here; it allows me to see the river for free and potentially realize my equity if a fourth diamond or a Jack appears, without risking more chips against a hand that might not fold.

I have 2s6s — only a pair of deuces with a weak 6 kicker on 8♦-2♦-A♠-T♦-7♣. The board has three diamonds so I'm highly vulnerable to a flush and I'm behind any Ace, Ten, or any pocket pair above deuces. Flash opened preflop, fired a small flop bet and then checked both the turn and river; that line tends to show weakness or pot control rather than a made monster. With a marginal hand and a large chip lead, I don't want to bloat the pot or bluff into hands that dominate me. Best play is pot control: check and take the showdown — fold to meaningful river aggression unless I get a very clear shove/bluff line or attractive odds to call.

I started the hand with a pre-flop raise and followed up with a continuation bet on an Ace-high flop, representing a strong range. On the turn, the board became more dangerous with three diamonds, and I chose to check back to control the pot and potentially realize equity with my diamond draw. On the river, I improved to a pair of Sevens. While this is a weak hand, it possesses some showdown value against missed draws (like a missed flush or straight draw). Betting now would essentially be a bluff, but since I have showdown value and it is unlikely a better hand (like an 8, 10, or weak Ace) will fold, checking back is the most prudent play to see the showdown without risking more chips.

Heads-up on the button (I’m the SB) with T♠2♥ — a very weak, often-dominated hand. The pot is 150 and I only need to put in 50 more (100 total committed including my SB) for correct pot odds (~3:1). With deep stacks I don’t want to bloat the pot preflop with this marginal holding by iso-raising; calling keeps me in position to see a cheap flop and allows me to realize equity or fold to strong action. Plan: call to 100; if I pair or pick up a strong draw I’ll continue, otherwise I’ll proceed cautiously and fold to significant aggression.

I am in the Big Blind with Queen-Seven of clubs. Minni has limped from the Small Blind, which suggests a wider or potentially weaker range. Q7s is a very strong hand in heads-up play and performs well post-flop. I will raise to 300 to take the initiative and build a pot with a hand that has significant equity and playability.

Call to 300. Heads-up on the button with T♠2♥ — a weak, often-dominated hand, but I have position and good pot odds. Flash raised to 300; I’ve already posted 50, so calling 200 more (total committed 300) gets roughly 3:1 on a flop. With deep stacks I don’t want to bloat the pot by 3-betting this marginal holding. Calling preserves my positional advantage, lets me realize equity, and keeps my post-flop options open: continue aggressively if I pair a Ten/Deuce or hit strong draws, otherwise play small-ball/pot-control and fold to heavy aggression.

I am the pre-flop aggressor and have flopped a strong flush draw with an overcard (Queen). On a board of J-4-9 with two clubs, a continuation bet is standard to maintain initiative and potentially win the pot immediately. If called, I have significant equity to improve on the turn or river.

Heads-up on the button with T♠2♥ vs Flash’s 300 flop bet into a ~600 pot. Calling 300 gives me ~3:1 pot odds (need ~25% equity). I have 6 outs to pair by the river (~12.8% on the turn, ~24.2% by river) — borderline but close, and I have positional advantage. Flash has been a wide preflop raiser/c-bettor, so his range contains many bluffs and weak hands I can exploit post-turn. Raising here with a raggy T2o would bloat the pot with little equity and is unnecessary given my position and deep stacks. I’ll call 300 to see the turn, keep the pot controllable, and re-evaluate: if I pair a Ten or Deuce I’ll extract value; if the turn is a blank and he fires a big bet I will fold; if he checks I can consider a delayed small bet.

On the turn, the 8h is a somewhat coordinated card that doesn't improve my hand but might have helped my opponent's range if they are holding 10-9, 8-9, or straight draws. Since I have a strong flush draw and a decent overcard with the Queen, checking back allows me to realize my equity for free and see the river without facing a potential check-raise. This keeps the pot controlled while preserving my stack for a river decision.

I have T♠2♥ on J♠-4♣-9♣-8♥ — I completely missed. Flash was the preflop raiser and c-bet the flop, then checked the turn, which shows a lot of hands that missed or are pot-controlling. Checking back surrenders all fold equity; a small delayed probe leverages my position to take the pot now or keep it small if called. The pot is 1,200, so a ~1/3-pot bet (400) applies pressure to overcards and air while not bloating the pot against clubs, 9x or Jx that will call. I will bet 400; if he folds I win the pot, if he calls I re-evaluate on the river (check back most blanks, consider a bluff on a safe card or value if I pair), and if he raises I will fold this marginal holding.

I am holding a flush draw with a Queen overcard on a J-4-9-8 board. Minni has bet 400 into a pot of 1200 after I checked the turn. The pot odds are 4:1, meaning I need 20% equity to justify a call. With 9 club outs and potentially 3 outs for a Queen to be the high pair, I have roughly 24-26% equity to improve on the river. This makes calling a mathematically sound decision to try and realize my equity.

The river King of Clubs is an excellent card for my hand, completing a Queen-high flush. On this board (Js-4c-9c-8h-Kc), there are no pairs, so a full house is impossible. The only hand that beats me is a flush headed by the Ace of Clubs. Since Minni checked to me on the river after I called her turn bet, it is highly likely I have the best hand. I will bet 1200 for value, as I expect to be called by a wide range of hands including any King, any Jack, or potentially a lower flush.

I have T♠2♥ on J♠-4♣-9♣-8♥-K♣. Line: Flash (preflop raiser) c‑bet the flop, checked then check‑called my 400 on the turn, and now bets a small 1,200 into a ~3,200 pot on the river. That line and sizing is much more consistent with thin value (Jx, 9x, a turned K, or other marginal made hands) than a polarized shove-bluff. To call 1,200 I must produce ~21.4% equity (1200 / (3200+1200+1200) ≈ 21.4%). My hand has essentially no showdown value here — I missed — so my true equity versus his likely range is far below the threshold. I briefly consider a bluff‑raise, but my blockers are poor, the raise size (min raise to 2,400) likely won’t fold enough of his value-heavy range and could get punished by a shove or call. I also have a big chip lead, so preserving chips and waiting for a better spot is the right long‑term play. Consistent with my plan to fold marginal holdings to river aggression, I fold.

In a heads-up situation, Ace-Trey offsuit is well ahead of an average random hand and should be played aggressively from the button. A standard 3x open (300 total) is consistent with my previous betting patterns and forces Minni to either fold or play out of position. Given the stack sizes, I want to continue building my stack without taking unnecessary risks, but A3o is too strong to simply limp or fold.

I'm heads-up in the big blind with A♦5♣ facing a 300 open from the button (Flash). I need to call 200 more to match. Heads-up A5o is a defendable hand: I have an ace-blocker and decent postflop equity against a wide opening range, but I'm out of position so I want to keep the pot under control. A light 3-bet is an option, but with a weak kicker I don't want to bloat an OOP pot or get into a costly 4-bet war. I'll call the 200 (total commit to 300) to see a flop, plan to fold to heavy aggression on dry boards, and continue aggressing or extract value when I hit top pair or strong draws.

I have Ad5c on Ac-4h-Ts — top pair with a weak kicker, out of position in a heads-up pot of 600. Flash opened from the button so his range is wide (lots of broadways and medium pairs that I beat), and the flop is fairly dry with few realistic draws. I want to extract value from worse A-x, Kx/Qx and some tens/pocket pairs while denying free cards and keeping the pot controlled OOP. A medium-sized c-bet (~1/2 pot) accomplishes that: it gets called by worse hands, folds pure floats, and leaves room to fold to a large raise without overcommitting with a marginal kicker. If he raises big I will re-evaluate (leaning to fold to a large, polarized raise); for now I bet for value.

On a dry flop of Ac-4h-Ts, I have top pair with an Ace, though my kicker (3) is very weak. Minni has led out with a 'donk bet' of 300 into a 600 pot. This lead could represent a range of hands, including a weak Ace, a Ten, a draw like KQ, or a complete bluff. Raising here is risky because I am likely only called by better Aces or sets, while folding is too tight given the strength of top pair in heads-up play. Calling allows me to control the size of the pot, keep Minni's bluffs in her range, and re-evaluate on the turn based on her subsequent action.

I have top pair (A♦5♣) with a weak kicker on A♣-4♥-T♠-9♥. Flash called my flop c-bet, so his range is wide (worse A‑x, Kx/Qx, some Tens, medium pairs and occasional floats). The turn 9♥ doesn’t change my relative hand strength meaningfully and there are no single-card flush/straight completions that kill me frequently (two hearts on board aren’t an immediate flush unless he held both hearts). I want to extract value from worse A‑x, tens and medium pairs and deny free cards to floats, while keeping the pot manageable out of position. A half‑pot second barrel (~600 into a 1200 pot) is the right balance — it gets called by worse hands and folds pure floaters, but doesn’t overcommit me if I run into big resistance. If Flash check‑raises large I will strongly consider folding this weak‑kicker top pair; if he simply calls I’ll re‑evaluate on the river based on the runout.

I have top pair on a board that hasn't changed significantly with the Nine of Hearts turn. Minni's half-pot bet is a standard continuation of her aggression from the flop. In heads-up play, top pair is a very strong hand, even with a weak kicker like a Three. Folding here would be too exploitable, and raising would likely only isolate me against better Aces or two-pair hands. By calling, I keep the pot controlled, allow Minni to continue with bluffs, and provide myself the opportunity to reach a showdown.

River analysis: I have Ad5c for top pair but the 5 is a very weak kicker. Villain (Button) called a flop half‑pot and a turn half‑pot — that narrows his continuing range to A‑x, T‑x, medium pairs and broadway cards. The Qh on the river is a bad card for me: it gives him many Qx combos that now beat me and also brings a three‑heart board (so double‑heart combos would make a flush). Betting here for thin value risks getting called by hands that beat me (Qx) or folding out only the very weakest hands; a larger bet would fold out worse and only get called by better. Pot control and showdown are preferable with a marginal top pair out of position against a caller-heavy line. I will check and take it to showdown; if Flash fires a big river bet I will fold most of the time given my weak kicker and the likelihood he’s improved.

I have top pair with an Ace on a board of Ac-4h-Ts-9h-Qh. However, my kicker (Trey) is very weak. Minni led out on both the flop and the turn, but has now checked the river. This river card (Queen of Hearts) is quite coordinated; it completes a potential straight (King-Jack) and a back-door flush. While my hand has significant showdown value in a heads-up match, betting for value here is risky. I am unlikely to be called by a worse hand than an Ace, and most better Aces or two-pair combinations (like AQ, AT, A9) might check to me to induce a bluff or control the pot. By checking back, I ensure a showdown and minimize risk against potential traps or better hands while still beating her missed bluffs or weaker pairs like a Ten.

Qc5c is a reasonable heads-up hand: suited, Q-high, and playable from the button. In heads-up I want to seize initiative and use position postflop, so I’ll open to a standard 2xBB (to 200). This sizing applies pressure to Flash’s wide BB defending range, gives me fold equity, and keeps the pot manageable against his ~9.6k stack. If he 3-bets big I’ll likely fold; if he calls I’ll proceed aggressively on favorable flops, leveraging my positional advantage and backdoor flush/straight potential.

I am in the Big Blind holding Ace-Four offsuit. Minni has min-raised to 200 from the button/SB. Given the pot odds (calling 100 to win a total pot of 300) and the fact that Ace-high hands perform reasonably well against a wide button opening range, this is a standard defend. I will call to see a flop rather than reraising, as A4o is a marginal hand that plays better in a smaller pot when out of position.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-high on a very dry King-high board (Kc 3s 7h). As the defender against a pre-flop raise, my standard play is to check to the aggressor. Ace-high has some showdown value but cannot withstand much pressure on this board unless I improve. I will check and see how the opponent reacts.

I opened preflop and on this dry Kc‑3s‑7h flop I should seize the initiative. Flash defended a wide BB range and his check is often weakness; my Qc5c has no pair (only Q‑high) and only backdoor club outs, so my best path is to c‑bet for fold equity rather than check and allow him to take control. A half‑pot continuation bet (200 into 400) pressures his missed hands and floats while keeping the pot manageable versus top pair or a set. Plan: if he calls I’ll re‑evaluate on the turn and only continue with a real improvement (a club or a Q) or a favorable turn to barrel; I will fold to a substantial check‑raise.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-high on a very dry board of K-3-7. Minni, as the pre-flop aggressor, has made a standard 1/2 pot continuation bet of 200. While I do not have a pair or a direct draw, Ace-high has reasonable showdown value against a wide button opening range that might be betting air or undercards. Given the pot odds of 3:1, folding here would be too tight. I will call to see a turn card; if the turn doesn't improve my hand or the board texture remains unfavorable and Minni continues her aggression, I can fold then. For now, calling is the most balanced play to protect my range.

On the turn, I have improved to bottom pair with an Ace kicker. The board remains relatively dry, though a heart flush draw has appeared. Since I am out of position against the pre-flop aggressor, the standard play is to check. Leading here would likely only be called by better hands (Kings, Sevens, or pocket pairs) while folding out the bluffs that I want her to continue with. By checking, I can evaluate her bet sizing and potentially reach a cheap showdown if the river is safe.

The turn (4h) did not improve my Qc5c — my backdoor club draw is dead after the turn, so I have only Q‑high with no pair. Flash called my flop c‑bet, so his range is weighted to hands that beat Q‑high (Kx, pocket pairs, 3/7, sometimes a heart draw), and barreling again into a caller is too thin. In position I should control the pot and give up equity-seeking bluffs when I’ve missed — check to see the river for a real improvement or a better bluff spot. If Flash leads into me I’ll fold to meaningful pressure and only call very small probes if pot odds justify it.

On the river, the board is Kc-3s-7h-4h-6s. I have bottom pair (fours) with an Ace kicker. Minni's check on the turn after C-betting the flop suggests she likely does not have a King, as she would typically bet for value or to protect against heart draws that appeared on the turn. My pair of fours has a reasonable amount of showdown value against her range of missed draws or lower pocket pairs. Since I am out of position, checking is the most prudent play. If she checks behind, I can evaluate my hand's strength at showdown. If she bets, I will have to decide whether my pair of fours is sufficient to call based on her bet sizing and perceived bluffs.

Final board Kc‑3s‑7h‑4h‑6s; my Qc5c has no pair (Q‑high) and missed all meaningful outs. I c‑bet the flop and Flash called, which polarizes his postflop calling range toward Kx, pocket pairs and some cautious floats — not a lot of pure air. Both checked the turn and the river blanked, so a river bluff here would have to fold out hands that already called a flop bet; given his call I expect many of those hands to call a small river bluff as well. Pot is only 800 and Flash has a healthy stack, so risking chips for a low‑EV bluff is unnecessary. I’ll check and take the showdown; if he then leads into me I will fold to meaningful pressure and only ever consider calling tiny probes that give correct pot odds.

I am in the Small Blind/Button position with Ace-Seven offsuit. This is a strong starting hand in a heads-up or short-handed scenario. Following my established pattern of aggressive play from the button to put pressure on the Big Blind, I will raise to 300 (3x the BB). This allows me to take control of the hand and potentially win the pot pre-flop or set up a continuation bet on the flop.

I have JACK OF SPADES (Js) TEN OF CLUBS (Tc) heads-up facing a Button (SB) open to 300. Pot is 400 and I need 200 to call (total 300 committed) — that gives me ~25% break-even equity vs a wide SB opening range. JTo has good playability postflop and I don’t want to bloat the pot out of position with a marginal hand by 3-betting (the min 3-bet to 500 won’t buy much fold equity vs this opponent). I’ll flat call to keep the pot controlled, see a flop, and then proceed aggressively with top pair/strong draws or pot-control/fold with misses.

Board is Qc Qh 3d and I hold Js Tc — no pair, no meaningful draws and I’m out of position vs the preflop raiser. Flash’s opening range is wide but on this paired flop he will have a lot of Qx and many hands that should continue for value. I don’t want to bloat the pot with a naked bluff or commit chips with a hand that has to improve. Better to control the pot: check and give myself the option to fold to a sizable c‑bet, call a small probe to see a turn, or take a free card if he checks back. This follows my plan to only build big pots with top pair/strong draws.

On a paired board like Q-Q-3, the pre-flop raiser usually has a range advantage as it is difficult for the defender to have a Queen. I have Ace-high with a decent kicker (A7), which holds significant showdown value in heads-up play. I will place a standard continuation bet of 200 (1/3 of the pot) to capitalize on the dry board texture, take down the pot immediately, or force Minni to reveal the strength of her hand.

I checked the flop to keep the pot small with J♠T♣ on Q♣Q♥3♦. Flash fires a small c‑bet (200 into a 800 pot) — that gives me ~20% pot odds to call. I have ~6 clean outs to a pair (three J’s, three T’s) and some post‑turn playability, so folding would be too nitty for these odds. Raising as a bluff is thin out of position (few blockers and low fold equity vs a wide opener) and would bloat a pot I don’t want to commit to without improvement. Plan: call the small bet, see a turn, and fold to a large turn barrel unless I improve or pick up a clear read/line to continue.

Board is Qc Qh 3d 5d and I hold Js Tc — I have no pair and no meaningful draw (only 6 outs: three J’s and three T’s ≈ 6/46 ≈ 13% to improve on the river). I already exercised pot control by calling the small flop c-bet; out of position against a wide preflop opener who will have lots of Qx and many hands that will call or fold very rarely to a turn bluff, barreling the turn is a thin play that just bloats the pot with poor equity and little fold equity. With a healthy stack and only one card to come, the prudent line is to keep the pot small: check and see what Flash does. If he checks back I take a free river; if he bets, I’ll fold to a large barrel and only consider calling very small bets that offer correct pot odds.

On a board that is double-paired with Queens and has very little connectivity, my Ace-high (As7c) still holds significant showdown value. Minni checked to me on the turn after calling a small continuation bet on the flop. By checking behind, I control the size of the pot and ensure I reach the river to see if I can win at showdown or evaluate a final bet. Betting again risks being check-raised off the best hand or building a pot where I am only called by better hands like small pocket pairs or a slow-played Queen.

Board: Qc Qh 3d 5d 6d. I hold Js Tc — missed completely (no pair, no diamond). Flash was the preflop raiser and c‑bet small on the flop, then checked the turn: his river checking line is consistent with a wide range that contains many Qx, some diamonds, and plenty of hands that will call a river bluff. I have almost no showdown value and very little blocker value against his likely calling hands, so a bluff will be called too often and simply bloats the pot when I have nothing. I played the hand for pot control earlier and the safest +EV line here is to check and go to showdown rather than firing a bluff into a sticky wide opener. Action: check.

On this double-paired board (QQ) with three low diamonds, my Ace-high (As7c) still has substantial showdown value. Minni has checked on every street post-flop, including the river. While she could be trapping with a marginal pair or a slow-played Queen, betting into her is unlikely to get a call from a worse hand, and I risk getting check-raised. Checking back allows me to realize my showdown value and win against missed draws or weaker Ace-high combinations without risking further chips.

Heads-up on the button (small blind) with 5♦3♠ — a weak, non‑suited, low connected hand. Pot is 150 and it only costs 50 more to see the flop (making my total contribution 100). In heads‑up play position is very valuable and calling is cheap insurance to see a flop where I can use position to realize equity or fold cheaply. Raising to the minimum (200) with this junk hand would bloat the pot, leak information, and invite 3‑bets or larger responses from an opponent who has shown aggression in previous hands. Folding loses only the small blind but is too passive given the tiny price to see a flop in position.

Plan: call to 100. On the flop I will proceed cautiously — only continue aggressively with a pair, two pair, sets, or strong draws; give up to significant aggression if I miss. If the flop is checked to me I can attempt a small c‑bet as a bluff in the right texture, but avoid building big pots with this holding.

I am in the Big Blind with a very weak hand (2d 4h). Minni, the Small Blind, has completed the bet. Since there is no additional cost to see the flop, I will check. Raising with such a weak hand out of position against a much larger stack is unnecessarily risky and inefficient.

I have absolutely no equity on this flop with 2d 4h. The board (Kh Qs 8h) is very coordinated and likely hits a significant portion of my opponent's range. Since I am out of position and have no pair or draw, the most prudent action is to check and fold to any subsequent bet.

Flop checked to me on K♥ Q♠ 8♥ with 5♦3♠ — I completely missed. In heads‑up position I can c‑bet a wide range; a small probe here leverages fold equity and keeps the pot controlled. I’ll bet about half‑pot (100 into 200) to try to take it down now or get a cheap showdown if called. If Flash raises I will give up — I have no pair, no relevant blockers, and don’t want to bloat the pot. If called I’ll re‑evaluate on the turn and only continue with improvement or a clear plan.

I have 2d 4h on a board of Kh Qs 8h, which provides me with no equity and no viable draws. Minni has bet 100 into a pot of 200. Although the pot odds are 3:1, my hand is far too weak to continue, especially out of position against a dominant stack. Folding is the only sensible move to preserve my remaining chips.

Holding Ace-Six offsuit on the button in a heads-up situation, I have a standard opening hand. My strategy is to maintain aggression and apply pressure on Minni's big blind. A raise to 3x the big blind (300 total) is a consistent sizing I've used in previous hands to build the pot when I likely have the best hand pre-flop, while also allowing me to see how she reacts to the initiative.

Flash opened to 300 from the button and I’m in the big blind with T♠7♦. Heads-up, the button’s opening range is wide so T7o has reasonable raw equity, and the pot is 400 with 200 to call (about 2:1 pot odds, ~33% equity needed). A tiny 3-bet to 500 (min-3bet) will be called a lot and I’ll be out of position postflop with a weak hand, while folding is overly tight and surrenders too much. I’ll defend the big blind by calling to see a flop and proceed cautiously if I miss.

I have T♠7♦ on Q♠ 7♥ 5♥ — second pair with a decent kicker on a two-heart texture. Heads-up the BTN opened wide, so my hand has decent raw equity vs a lot of their range but is vulnerable to Qx and heart draws. With the lead, betting lets me extract value from worse pairs and chops off equity from draw-heavy hands; checking hands like mine too often gives free cards and lets floats realize equity. I’ll make a half-pot protection/value bet (300). If Flash raises big I’ll respect the action and fold or re-evaluate on the turn rather than get overly committed with second pair.

I raised pre-flop with Ace-Six offsuit and Minni called. The flop comes Queen-Seven-Five with two hearts. Minni leads out with a 'donk bet' of 300 into a 600 pot. This lead often indicates a hand that has connected with the board, such as a Queen, a middle pair, or a heart draw. Holding only Ace-high with no heart in my hand and very limited backdoor potential, I am likely behind her range. While I am in position, calling here would require me to likely face further aggression on the turn. Given the board texture and Minni's recent activity, folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my stack for more favorable situations.

Heads-up on the button (small blind) with 9♣2♦. This is a very weak hand, but heads‑up/button strategy is to open a very wide range and take the initiative. Pot is only 150 and to call is just 50 more, but limp/calling lets the big blind see a cheap flop and surrenders fold equity. I’ll open-raise to the minimum standard size (200 total) to steal the BB or play a small pot in position. If Flash 3‑bets large I’ll fold this hand — 9‑2o has poor playability vs a 3‑bet. If he calls I can use position to continue on favorable flops. Raise to 200 keeps the risk small while applying pressure.

In the big blind, I have 8d 5s and am facing a min-raise from Minni. The pot odds are very favorable, requiring only 100 more to see a flop in a 300 pot (3:1). While my hand is weak, it has some connectivity and the price is good enough to defend my blind against a wide button opening range, especially given our stack depths. I will call to see a flop and evaluate my position thereafter.

I am in the Big Blind with 8d 5s. The flop is 9s 7s 2c, giving me an inside straight draw (needing a 6). As the caller of a pre-flop raise, the standard play is to check to the aggressor. My hand is relatively weak, but I have a bit of equity with the gutshot. I will check and see what Minni does.

Flop: 9♠ 7♠ 2♣ with my 9♣2♦ — I have two pair (9s and 2s), which is a very strong holding heads‑up. The BB's calling range (from the preflop call) can include sets (77, 99, 22), single pairs, and some two‑spade draws. In position I want to both extract value from worse pairs/9x hands and charge/deter spade draws. Pot is 400; a value/protection bet of roughly 60% pot achieves that without bloating the pot too much against potential sets. I will bet 250 to charge draws and weaker pairs, then re‑evaluate on the turn (and respect a large check‑raise as a strong range).

I have a gutshot straight draw with 8d 5s on a 9s 7s 2c flop. Minni's bet of 250 into 400 represents over half the pot, and my direct equity (roughly 8.5% to hit a 6 on the turn) is significantly lower than the 27.7% pot odds required to call profitably. Given that I have no spade to offer a backdoor flush draw and only 8-high, continuing here against an aggressive opponent is likely negative expected value. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am on the button with K9o, which is a strong starting hand in heads-up play. I want to take the initiative and use my positional advantage throughout the hand. A standard opening raise to 3x the big blind is appropriate here to test Minni's defense and build the pot for when I have the best of it.

Heads-up in the big blind with pocket tens facing a 3x open to 300. TTs is a very strong heads-up hand and performs well both as a value 3-bet and as a hand to isolate the opener. Flash has shown frequent preflop aggression, so I want to apply pressure and get value from weaker broadways, suited connectors and medium pairs while keeping the pot controllable against his 8,950 effective stack. I’ll 3-bet to ~3x the open (to 900 total) — large enough to extract value and gain fold equity, but small enough to avoid overcommitting preflop. If he calls I’ll proceed postflop based on texture; if he 4-bets large I’ll re-evaluate given stack depth.

K9o is a respectable hand in heads-up play, and while Minni's 3-bet indicates strength or a desire to take the pot pre-flop, I have the advantage of being in position for the remainder of the hand. Calling 600 more into a 1200 pot gives me decent pot odds and allows me to see a flop where I can evaluate my hand strength against her continuation range.

I have pocket tens — a clean overpair to this dry, rainbow 6♠‑3♥‑7♦ flop. Heads‑up and after Flash called my 3‑bet, his continuing range is wide (we beat most Ax, broadways, medium pairs and many suited connectors). Very few hands legitimately crush me here (sets 66/77 or hands like 7‑6). I want to extract value, deny free cards to backdoor draws, and keep the pot manageable relative to his 8,350 effective stack. A half‑pot bet is the right mix: gets value from worse hands and folds out some equity, but doesn't commit me if he shows big strength. Bet half pot (900 into 1,800). If he check‑raises big or jams I’ll strongly consider that he has a set/two‑pair and re‑evaluate (likely folding to extreme sizing); if he calls I’ll continue based on the turn texture.

I currently hold King-high with no direct draws on a low board of 6-3-7. Minni's pre-flop 3-bet followed by a half-pot continuation bet suggests a strong range that likely includes overpairs or high overcards like Ace-King or Ace-Queen, which are currently ahead of me. Given that I have no pair and no real equity to continue, calling is a losing play in the long run. Preserving my remaining stack for a more favorable situation is the best strategic move here.

Heads-up on the button (I’ve already posted the 50 SB) with J♠5♦. In heads-up play the button should open very wide and exploit position — J5o is perfectly acceptable to raise. The pot is 150 and the min-raise is to 200, but I’ll open to 300 (3x BB) which is my standard sizing here: it applies pressure, discourages cheap flops for the big blind, and keeps my range balanced given how often I open to 300 in prior hands. If called I’ll have position postflop and can c-bet many flops or control the pot; if Flash 3-bets large I will fold this marginal hand. Action: raise to 300 total.

In this heads-up situation, I am in the Big Blind with 9c 6s. Minni, who is the massive chip leader, has raised to 3x the blind from the button. While defending ranges in heads-up play are wider, 9-6 offsuit is a weak hand to play out of position against an aggressive and well-funded opponent. I have approximately 82 big blinds, so I am not yet in a desperate situation where I must defend marginal hands. I will fold and wait for a better spot to contest a pot.

In heads-up play, Queen-Ten offsuit (QTo) is a very strong starting hand from the button. I have 82 big blinds, while my opponent has a significant lead. I will put in a standard raise to 300 to take the initiative and value my position.

Heads-up, I’m in the big blind with 4♣2♠ (4♥2♠). Button opened to 300 (3x). Pot is 400 and it costs me 200 to call (I’d need ~25% equity to break even). 4-2 offsuit is one of the worst possible hands: almost always dominated by a BTN opening range, has negligible flush/straight potential and plays extremely poorly out of position postflop. 3-betting as a bluff here is thin — Flash has ~79bb effective and I don’t want to bloat a deep pot with junk. With tournament life fine and many better spots ahead, the correct, low-variance play is to fold preflop and wait for a hand I can play OOP or with positional advantage.

Heads-up on the button (I’m the small blind) with Qd2h — a weak offsuit hand. In heads-up this is playable, but I want to keep the pot small and use my postflop position rather than bloat the pot preflop with a marginal holding. The pot is 150; calling 50 (total committed 100) gives me 25% pot odds to see the flop, which is a cheap price to realize equity and leverage position. Flash (BB) has shown aggression in this game, so raising can invite a 3‑bet or larger confrontation I don’t want with Q2o. Plan: call to see the flop, fold to a meaningful 3‑bet, and then either fold to large continuation bets on dry boards or continue when I pair a queen / pick up useful draws or can exploit postflop action.

I have been dealt Queen-Deuce of clubs in the Big Blind. Minni, who has a significant chip lead, has just completed the small blind. In heads-up play, Q2s is a hand with some potential due to its suitedness and one high card. Since there is no further action required to see the flop, I will check. Raising here might bloat the pot unnecessarily with a marginal hand against a chip leader who has position on me for the rest of the hand.

I have Queen-high on a board featuring a King and a Ten, with no immediate draws. In a limped pot where I am out of position against the chip leader, there is no value in betting air. I will check and see how the opponent reacts to the board.

Heads‑up on a dry K‑T‑6 flop and I hold Q♦2♥ — no pair, only a weak backdoor heart. I’m in position and Flash (BB, 8,250) checked, which often indicates a wide, uncapped range here. Betting gives me a chance to take the pot now and define his holding while keeping the size small. I’ll c‑bet about half the pot to apply pressure to his air/marginal hands (T9, Tx without kicker, missed broadways) while avoiding bloating the pot with a weak hand. If he raises big I’ll fold — I have little equity and don’t want to get committed out of position. If he calls I’ll proceed cautiously on the turn and re‑evaluate.

On a flop of Ts-Kh-6h, I have Queen-high with no immediate draws. Minni has bet 100 into a 200-chip pot. Given that the board contains two overcards to my Queen (King and Ten) and a flush draw is possible, I have very little equity in this hand. Calling out of position with air against the chip leader is not a winning strategy. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am on the button with Js7h. While this hand is slightly below average, in heads-up play, maintaining aggression from the button is important. Minni has a significantly larger stack, but I still have 82 big blinds, which gives me plenty of room to play. A standard raise to 300 has been my consistent opening size throughout the match, allowing me to take control of the pot early or win it pre-flop. If called, I will have the advantage of position on the flop.

Facing a heads-up open to 300 from the button, I have T♥3♣ in the big blind. Flash’s open is likely wide here and the pot odds to call 200 into a 400 pot are favorable. I don’t want to inflate the pot out of position with a weak kicker, so I’ll defend by calling (total committed = 300). Plan postflop: proceed cautiously — act based on the flop texture, folding to heavy aggression unless I hit a meaningful piece (top pair, two pair) or develop strong draws. This keeps the pot manageable and preserves stack depth for better spots against an aggressive opponent.

Heads-up against a wide button open, I’m in the big blind with T♥3♣ on a very dry 9♦-4♣-6♥ flop. I only have a single overcard and no real draws, and I’m out of position after already defending preflop. Pot control is the priority — don’t bloat the pot with a marginal hand. Check to keep the pot small, let Flash bluff or c-bet into me, and only continue with a call if the bet is reasonable or if I improve on the turn; fold to heavy aggression without improvement. Therefore I check and see his reaction.

I am the pre-flop aggressor with Js7h, and the flop of 9d-4c-6h is relatively dry. Since Minni checked, a continuation bet is the standard play here to maintain the initiative and potentially win the pot immediately. My Jack-high has very little showdown value, but it can force folds from better hands that missed this board, like weak Ace-highs or King-highs. A bet of 300, which is half the pot, is a balanced sizing that puts pressure on my opponent without over-committing chips.

Flash's c-bet is small (300 into a 900 pot) and his heads-up opening range from the button should be fairly wide. I have T♥3♣ — no pair, one overcard (Ten) and no real draws. Calling 300 here gives ~20% pot odds to continue and is the lowest-variance way to realize equity while keeping the pot controlled out of position. I don't want to bloat the pot or bluff-raise OOP with a marginal hand, so I will call to see the turn and re-evaluate: fold to heavy turn aggression if I miss, or proceed if I pair the Ten or pick up a good draw.

I’m first to act on the turn with T♥3♣ on 9♦-4♣-6♥-7♦. I have no pair, no flush draw and only a 4‑out gutshot to a 5 (~8–9% to hit). Out of position against a wide button who has been c‑betting, my priority is pot control — this is not a hand I want to bloat OOP or turn into a bluff‑raising spot. I’ll check and keep the pot small. Plan: fold to any sizable bet (no justification to call big with ~4 outs), consider calling only a very small probe to see the river, and re‑evaluate on the river for a value bet or a targeted bluff if the card and opponent’s action allow.

On the turn, the 7d improves my hand to a pair of sevens. While I have improved, the board is now quite coordinated with straight possibilities (58 and 8T). Minni checked to me after calling my flop continuation bet. Since my hand now has some showdown value but isn't strong enough to value bet for three streets, checking back is a solid play. This allows me to control the size of the pot and potentially induce a bluff on the river or reach a showdown cheaply. Betting here risks getting check-raised by a better hand or a strong draw, forcing me into a difficult situation.

I have T♥3♣ — no pair, no draw on a dry 9♦-4♣-6♥-7♦-2♣ board. Villain c-bet small on the flop then checked the turn, showing weakness or pot control. There is no value to bet and my blockers are poor for a convincing bluff; a river bluff here is thin and likely to be called by any pair. Pot is only 1,200, so I’ll avoid creating a marginal spot and check to see a showdown or induce a bluff. If Flash fires a river bet I will fold (I only beat bluffs).

On the river, the board is 9-4-6-7-2 with no possible flushes. I have a pair of sevens. Minni has checked for the third time (after check-calling the flop and checking the turn). This line heavily suggests a missed draw or a weak marginal hand that is looking to get to showdown cheaply. While I could potentially bet for value, a pair of sevens is a strong enough hand to win at showdown in heads-up play, but not necessarily strong enough to be called by many worse hands if I bet. By checking back, I ensure I win the pot against missed draws and weak Ace-highs without risking a check-raise from a slow-played nine or a better pocket pair.

Heads-up on the button (I am the small blind) with K♠6♦. The pot is 150 and I must put in 50 to call. In heads-up play I should be opening a very wide range from the button to use positional advantage postflop and to pick up the blinds. K6o is playable and belongs in my open-raise range. I prefer a standard 3x BB open rather than completing or a min-raise: raising to 300 total gives meaningful fold equity, discourages light 3-bets, and keeps postflop pots in a size I can control while acting last. If Flash 3-bets I will re-evaluate based on sizing/read; if he calls I can play postflop in position. Therefore I raise to 300 (total).

I am in the Big Blind with Queen-Deuce offsuit (Q2o). Minni has opened from the Small Blind/Button with a 3x raise to 300. Q2o is a very weak hand to defend with, especially out of position against a player who has a significant chip lead and has shown consistent aggression. While the pot odds are 2:1, the hand's playability post-flop is poor, and I would prefer to wait for a stronger holding or a better spot to utilize my stack.

In heads-up play, holding J3 suited on the button (Small Blind) is a hand well within the standard opening range. I have a significant stack disadvantage against Minni, so I need to maintain aggression to pick up blinds and small pots without letting her steamroll me. A standard raise to 3x the big blind (300 total) allows me to take control of the hand with position, while still being able to fold if she responds with a large three-bet.

Heads-up vs Flash (button) who opened to 300. I’m in the big blind with 5♦6♣ — a marginal but playable hand heads-up. Calling 200 more (total commitment 300) gives good pot odds to see a flop and keeps the pot controlled while I’m out of position. I don’t want to 3-bet light here and inflate the pot with a weak offsuit connector against a wide—but capable—opening range and an effective stack of ~8450. Plan: see the flop cheaply, continue with pairs or strong draws, fold to heavy aggression on dry, unfavorable boards.

Check. On A♥3♥2♣ with 5♦6♣ I have no pair and only a one-card gutshot to a 4 (4 clean outs, ≈16.5% to hit by the river). The preflop opener from the button is likely to contain a lot of A-x and high-card hands heads-up, so I’m behind to most of his range. With the pot already at 600 and no bet, I want to keep the pot small and see a turn card for free — check to control the size and induce bluffs. Plan: fold to significant pressure (half‑pot or larger bets require ~25%+ equity, which I don’t have), consider calling very small bets only if pot odds/implied odds justify it, and if I hit my straight or a favorable card on the turn I’ll switch to value/pressure lines.

On a board of Ah-3h-2c, I have bottom pair with my Jd3d. As the pre-flop aggressor, I have a range advantage on this Ace-high board, as I am more likely to have strong Aces and pocket pairs than Minni, who just called from the Big Blind. While I have some showdown value with my pair of Threes, a continuation bet of 300 (half the pot) is a standard play here to maintain my narrative of holding a strong hand and to fold out some of Minni's air or speculative hands that might have equity against me. If she calls, I can consider checking back the turn to realize my showdown value.

Call 300. Heads‑up on A♥3♥2♣ against the button's small c‑bet: Flash is likely c‑betting a wide range, and this bet is only ~1/3 pot so the pot odds are favorable. Pot = 900, his bet = 300; calling 300 to win a 1500 pot after my call requires ~20% equity. My 5♦6♣ has ~10 clean outs to a pair or the straight (≈38% to improve by the river), so a call is +EV. Raising with a weak, non‑heart hand here would overcommit and is unnecessary — I’ll keep the pot controlled and re‑evaluate on the turn. Plan after the call: fold to significant turn aggression unless I pair or complete the straight; if I hit a pair/straight switch to value/pressure.

Board is A♥-3♥-2♣-5♣; I paired my 5 (5♦6♣) on the turn but this is a marginal, vulnerable second pair against a button who opened wide preflop. Pot is 1,200 and I’m out of position. Betting for thin value here is poor because a large portion of Flash’s continuing range contains Ax and other hands that currently beat me; bluffing into a wide range is unnecessary when I have deep stack equity and can play the river. I’ll check to control pot size and induce action: if he bets small I can call for pot‑odds and retain showdown value; if he bets big or check‑raises I’ll fold unless I improve on the river. If he checks back we go to the river cheaply and re‑evaluate (look to extract if I improve to two pair/trips).

The 5c on the turn is a coordinated card that completes straights for any hand containing a 4. My pair of 3s has some showdown value, but it is unlikely to be the best hand if significant action occurs. Having bet the flop and been called, I need to be cautious as Minni could have an Ace, a draw, or even a straight now. Checking back allows me to control the pot size and realize my equity by seeing the river for free without risking a check-raise.

Board A♥ 3♥ 2♣ 5♣ 2♦ with my 5♦6♣ — I have two pair (5s and 2s). Opponent (button) opened preflop, c-bet the dry A-3-2 flop, then checked turn and checked the river. That line weights their river checking range toward wide Ax, some single-pair hands, and air; hands that actually beat me (any 2, pocket 3s/5s, A3/A2/A5 combos, or quads) are possible but far less frequent. 

Heads‑up this is a good spot to extract thin value — many of Flash’s continuing hands (A‑high, weaker pairs) will call a modest river bet, while the very few hands that beat me will often raise or have bet earlier. I’ll make a small/medium value bet to get called by worse hands while keeping the pot manageable. If Flash check‑raises the river I’ll fold — a check‑raise here is almost always trips/full house/quads and I’m behind.

Bet 600 (≈1/2 pot) to target worse A‑x and one‑pair hands; keep pot controlled and fold to any substantial river raise.

On a board of Ah-3h-2c-5c-2d, my hand is Jack-Three, giving me two pair: Threes and Deuces with an Ace kicker. While this sounds decent, the board is highly coordinated. Minni called my continuation bet on the flop, which heavily suggests she has an Ace, a pair, or a draw. The 5 on the turn completed a potential straight for any hand with a 4 (like A4 or 45), and the 2 on the river paired the board. My two pair (3s and 2s) is beaten by any Ace (A-A-2-2-5), any 5 (5-5-2-2-A), any pocket pair higher than 3s, or any 4 (straight). Given that Minni is betting half the pot on the river after I checked back the turn, it is highly likely she is betting for value with a hand that beats my weak two pair. I am only beating a total air bluff or a missed heart flush draw, but those are less likely given her call on the flop. I will fold and preserve my stack.

4♠8♦ is a weak, unsuited hand and not worth a proactive raise against an opponent who has shown frequent aggression. However, heads-up on the button it’s cheap to see a flop — I only need to put in 50 more into a 150 pot. I’ll call to use position and evaluate the flop; I’ll fold to significant aggression on later streets unless I hit a pair or strong draw. (Total committed after my call will be 100 including my SB.)

I am in the Big Blind with King-Deuce of hearts. Minni has limped from the Small Blind. While K2s is a decent hand in heads-up play, checking behind allows me to see a flop for free and keeps the pot small, which is reasonable given that I am out of position for the rest of the hand and currently at a significant stack disadvantage.

In this limped pot, I have King-high on a Ten-high board. Minni has checked, and while my hand has very little equity if called, it still holds some showdown value against her total air. Betting here doesn't achieve much as I'm unlikely to fold out a better hand, and I can't comfortably value bet. Checking behind allows me to potentially see a cheap turn and preserve my stack.

I called preflop with 4♠8♦ intending to play my position and fold to aggression unless I hit. The flop 3♠7♠10♥ is a complete miss — I have no pair and only a runner‑runner backdoor spade flush (~4% to hit both turn+river). Flash checked, which can be weakness or a trap, but given how weak my hand is and that I have position, the correct line is to keep the pot small and see a turn. I will check to realize equity and only continue if I pick up a meaningful improvement or face a very small bet; I will fold to any significant bet (especially pot‑sized or larger). No reason to bluff/raise into an aggressive opponent from this spot.

On the turn card 9d, the board is becoming more coordinated. Minni has checked for the second time in this limped pot. While King-high might occasionally be the best hand here against her missed draws or low air, it has very little showdown value. However, betting as a bluff is risky because this board texture hits many hands in a limping range (like 8x, Jx, or small pairs). Checking back allows me to see the river for free and potentially realize what little equity I have or hit a King, without risking more chips in a spot where I have no made hand.

I hold 4s8d on 3s7sTh9d — no pair and my backdoor flush is now impossible after the diamond turn. My only realistic outs are any 6 or any J (8 outs), about 8/46 ≈ 17% to hit a straight on the river. The pot is small (200) and Flash checked the turn, showing either weakness or pot control. With position and a free card coming, the lowest-variance, highest-expected-value play is to check and realize equity rather than bluff into a possibly strong or sticky opponent. If I miss the river I will fold to any significant bet; if I hit (or get a very favorable river) I can extract value then.

The board is 3s-7s-Th-9d-9c and the action has gone check-check on every street in a limped pot. My hand is King-high, which has some showdown value in this specific scenario against Minni's likely range of missed low cards or weak high cards (like Queen-high or lower). There is no reason to bet here as I'm unlikely to fold out a better hand (like a small pair) and I don't want to get check-raised off the best hand if I happen to be ahead. Checking to see a free showdown is the most logical play to preserve my stack.

I completely missed the river with 4♠8♦ on 3♠7♠T♥9♦9♣ — no pair, no flush and no meaningful showdown value. The board pairing on the river makes many hands (9x, Tx, 7x, pocket pairs, and rare straights) that beat me, and Flash checked flop/turn which could be either weakness or pot control with a strong hand. The pot is small (200) and I have poor blockers and little fold equity, so bluffing is low EV — most better hands will call. I’ll check to realize my (very small) showdown equity and fold to any meaningful bet.

I am on the button (Small Blind) with 7s 6c, a speculative but playable hand in a heads-up situation, especially given the stack dynamics and the need to maintain aggression. The pot is small (150) and it only costs 50 more to call, but standard strategy here is to either raise to put pressure on the Big Blind or fold. Given my current stack of 8200 against Minni's 31650, I want to play a controlled game. I will raise to 300 to take control of the hand and potentially win the pot pre-flop or set up a continuation bet.

Heads-up, button (SB) opened to 300. I’m in the big blind with A♠ T♣. Pot is 400 and it costs me 200 more to call (300 total) — immediate pot odds about 2:1 (need ~33% equity). ATo has good equity vs a wide button opening range and plays well post-flop heads-up. I’m out of position, so a small 3-bet (min to 500) would bloat the pot OOP and won’t gain much fold equity against an aggressive player with ~80BB; bigger 3-bets risk getting 4-bet and creating tough decisions. With deep stacks and many hands left in the tournament I’ll keep the pot manageable, realize equity, and make postflop decisions based on texture and opponent tendencies. Call to 300.

Heads-up vs a wide button open I’ve flopped top pair with the best possible kicker (A♠ T♣ on Th 7c 5c). The board is relatively dry with only two clubs — few immediate draws — and Flash’s range contains many worse hands (Kx, Qx, Jx, smaller pairs, Ax) that will call. I want value and to charge any backdoor club/straight combinations while keeping the pot manageable out of position, so I’ll bet about half the pot. If he raises I’ll re-evaluate (call bigger sizings for value vs draws/sets; fold only to extreme, unlikely lines).

On the flop of Th 7c 5c, I have middle pair (sevens) with an inside straight draw (needing an eight for a straight). The opponent, Minni, has led out for 300 into a 600 pot (a half-pot bet). This 'donk lead' can represent a variety of hands, including a ten, a draw (like clubs or 8-9), or a smaller pair. Folding is too weak given I have a piece of the board and a back-door straight draw. Raising is risky against a large stack that could easily trap or re-raise me. Calling allows me to see a turn card, control the pot size, and evaluate if my middle pair is good or if I improve on the next street.

I have A♠T♣ on Th 7c 5c Qd — top pair with the best kicker. Flash called my half‑pot flop bet and then checked the turn, which signals a lot of marginal hands (Kx, Jx, floats, medium pairs) or outright air rather than a frequent check‑raise from sets/two‑pair. Very few hands now beat me (Qx, sets, TQ), so betting for value is the +EV line. I’ll size modestly to extract from worse tens, Kx, Jx and smaller pairs while keeping the pot controllable OOP — bet ~½ pot. If he calls I’ll re‑evaluate the river; if he check‑raises a small amount I’ll consider calling, but I’ll respect a large shove that polarizes strongly to two‑pair/sets. Action: bet 600 into the 1200 pot.

I am currently holding third pair (sevens) on a board of Th-7c-5c-Qd. Minni has led out on both the flop and the turn. Her turn bet of 600 into a 1200 pot represents a half-pot wager, giving me 3:1 pot odds to call. While the Queen is an overcard that could have improved her range, I am in position and the price is attractive enough to see a river. I am looking for a seven or a six to improve my hand, or a blank river that might lead to a check-down. Given the heads-up nature and the stack sizes, folding a pair to a standard continuation bet on the turn would be overly tight.

I have A♠T♣ — top pair with the best kicker on a relatively dry board (T♥ 7♣ 5♣ Q♦ 9♥). Flash opened preflop and then check‑called my half‑pot flop bet and my 600 turn bet, a passive line consistent with Kx, Jx, medium pairs or worse tens rather than frequent strong two‑pair/set check‑raises. The river 9♥ is a fairly blank card — only a few unlikely straight combos (e.g. J8, 6‑8) and Qx/two‑pair beat me. A half‑pot value bet (~1200 into 2400) will get called by worse tens, Kx, Jx and medium pairs while keeping the pot controlled out of position and avoiding unnecessarily bloating the pot. If he check‑raises large I’ll respect a polarized range and fold or re‑evaluate depending on sizing; if he calls I win value from many worse hands.

On the river board of Th-7c-5c-Qd-9h, I am holding a pair of sevens (fourth pair). My opponent, Minni, has led out on all three streets (triple barrel), which typically indicates significant strength in a heads-up match. The river 9h is a particularly bad card for my hand; it completes a potential straight for any 8 in her range (6-7-8-9-10) and is another overcard to my sevens. While the pot odds are 3:1, my hand is now essentially a bluff catcher against a range that includes Queens, Tens, Nines, and straights. Given her consistent half-pot sizing throughout the hand, it is more likely she is betting for value with a better hand than bluffing with a missed club draw. I will fold to preserve my stack.

Heads-up on the button (small blind) I should open a very wide range. J♠3♦ is marginal but fine to open in heads-up to seize initiative and use position postflop. Pot is 150 and it costs only 50 to complete, but limp-pass is exploitable and I want to pressure the BB (stack ~6950). Min-raise to 200 (standard 2x BB here) often takes the blinds or plays me in position for cheap.
