Plan: raise to 200 to isolate/steal; if Flash 3-bets big I can fold and preserve most of my deep stack; if he calls I’ll play cautiously in position and evaluate flop texture before committing further.

I am holding Ten of Clubs and Queen of Hearts in the Big Blind. Minni, who has a significant chip lead, has raised to 200 from the Button. This is a standard heads-up opening. My hand is well above the average range and certainly strong enough to defend. I am getting 3:1 pot odds (100 to call into a 300 pot). While I could consider a 3-bet, calling allows me to keep the pot controlled and see a flop against an aggressive chip leader while minimizing the risk of a 4-bet shove that would put my tournament life at risk. I will call the 100 and evaluate on the flop.

I have flopped middle pair (Queens) on an Ace-high board. While my hand has decent showdown value, checking to the aggressor is the standard play here to control the pot and gauge Minni's strength. Betting into an Ace-high board when I don't have the Ace is risky against a large stack like Minni. I will check and likely call a standard-sized continuation bet to see if I can reach a cheap showdown or improve to two pair/trips.

I opened a very wide range from the button preflop, so Flash's calling range contains a lot of Ax/Qx, medium pairs and weak broadways. The flop A♣ Q♣ 7♠ completely missed J♠3♦ and actually favors the caller's range (lots of Ax/Qx), but Flash has been passive postflop in this game and will fold a fair number of marginal hands. With position and initiative I want to apply pressure without bloating the pot. A small continuation bet (~1/3 pot) is optimal here: it folds Kx/Tx/missed broadways and charges club draws while keeping the pot manageable if he calls. If he raises decisively I’ll fold this air; if he just calls I’ll slow down on the turn and only continue with a real improvement or a good turn bluffing opportunity.

I have flopped middle pair (Queens) with a Ten kicker on an Ace-high board. Minni has made a small continuation bet of 150 into a 400 pot (about 37% of the pot). While the Ace is a scare card, Minni's aggressive history suggests she could be betting with a wide range of hands, including draws or air. I have enough showdown value to continue here, and the price offered (150 to call into a 550 pot) gives me excellent pot odds. Raising would likely only be called by an Ace or better, so I will call to keep the pot manageable and evaluate the turn.

I am in the Big Blind and it is my turn to act on the turn. The board is Seven of Spades, Queen of Clubs, Ace of Clubs, Eight of Spades. I have a pair of Queens with a Ten kicker. This is a solid marginal hand, but the Ace on the board is a major concern. On the flop, I checked and called a small continuation bet from Minni. On the turn, the Eight of Spades adds a second flush draw to the board. I will check again to control the pot. Betting here would likely only get called by an Ace or better, whereas checking allows Minni to continue bluffing with her air or missed draws, and keeps the pot manageable against a much larger stack. If she bets, I will re-evaluate based on the sizing, but my intention is to try to reach a showdown as cheaply as possible.

I opened a very wide range from the button and continuation‑bet the flop (150). Flash’s flat call on the flop indicates a calling range heavy in Ax/Qx, medium pairs and club combos; he’s been passive postflop. The turn 8♠ is a blank for my hand — J♠3♦ still has no pair and the 8 doesn’t meaningfully change his range. I have very little showdown equity and limited realistic fold equity against his caller‑heavy range, so bluffing the turn would be low EV and could bloat a pot I’m not prepared to commit to. With pot control and position I should slow down, keep the pot manageable (pot 700, villain ~6700, my stack ~32600) and reevaluate the river. If he bets I can fold; if he checks I get a free river. Check to proceed.

On the river, the board is 7s-Qc-Ac-8s-2d. I hold middle pair (Queens) with a Ten kicker. Minni checked back the turn, which is a strong indicator that she likely does not have an Ace, as she would typically bet an Ace for value and protection against the multiple draws present on that board. Her check-back suggests a marginal hand like a Seven, a smaller pocket pair, or a missed draw. My Queen is likely the best hand. Betting here has little value, as most hands I beat will fold, and I will only be called by an Ace or a better Queen. Checking allows me to see a showdown for free or potentially induce a bluff from missed draws (like clubs or straight draws).

Board: 7♠ Q♣ A♣ 8♠ 2♦. My hand J♠3♦ missed completely. I opened wide preflop and c‑bet the flop (150); villain flat‑called, which points to a calling range heavy in Ax/Qx, some medium pairs and club combos. Turn and river are blanks for me and villain has been passive (checked turn and river). With essentially no showdown equity against that range and low fold equity on the river (villain likely calls small river bets with Ax/Qx), a bluff here is low EV. Pot is 700 and villain still has ~6700 — no reason to bloat the pot with air. I keep pot control: check and fold to any river bet.

KJo is a strong starting hand in a heads-up or short-handed scenario. Being on the button (Small Blind), I have positional advantage for the rest of the hand. I will follow my established pattern of raising to 300 to build the pot and take control of the action.

Heads-up in the big blind vs a standard 300 open: pot is 400 and I need 200 more to call, so I’m getting ~33% pot odds. 6♥5♦ is marginal but acceptable to defend heads-up — decent connectivity and realizable equity against a wide button opening range. Flash has been opening to 300 frequently and c-betting a lot; flat-calling keeps his bluffs and weaker hands in while allowing me to see a cheap flop OOP and leverage deep stack implied odds if I hit. I’ll avoid a thin 3-bet here (no relevant blockers and too weak for an isolation raise). Plan: call to see the flop; continue with pairs or strong draws (or attack very favorable textures), fold to heavy aggression on dry high-card boards without improvement. Total committed after calling will be 300.

Check. I have a marginal pair (5s with a 6 kicker) on a fairly dry T♥‑2♠‑5♣ flop. I’m out of position so I want to control the pot and let the preflop aggressor — who opens wide and c-bets frequently — continue with bluffs and weaker overcards. Checking keeps his bluffs and worse hands in, allows me to call reasonable-sized bets to realize equity, and preserves stack flexibility given his ~7.1k stack vs our 32.3k. Plan: check-call small-to-moderate c-bets, fold to very large polarizing pressure if he commits a huge portion of his stack, and if he checks back consider taking the initiative on the turn or barreling favorable turns for value/denial.

I have two overcards (King and Jack) on a relatively dry board (Ten-high). As the pre-flop aggressor, I will place a continuation bet of 300 (half the pot) to test Minni's hand and attempt to take down the pot immediately. This board doesn't hit many hands in a typical Big Blind calling range, so a bet is likely to generate folds from missed hands.

Flash opened wide preflop and c‑bets frequently. On T♥‑2♠‑5♣ I have 6♥5♦ — second pair with a decent (but vulnerable) kicker. He bet 300 into a ~600 pot; calling 300 gives roughly 25% required equity, and my hand has better than that versus a wide c‑betting range (lots of overcards and bluffs). I’m out of position so I want to control the pot and keep his bluffs and weaker holdings in; raising OOP with second pair and no meaningful blockers would be thin and often hurts our fold equity or builds a pot we don’t want to face big turn/river aggression in. Opponent’s stack (≈6.8k) means implied odds are limited but calling still is the correct, low‑risk line to realize equity and re‑evaluate on the turn. Plan: call the 300, fold to extreme polarizing shove/huge turn sizing if I don’t improve, and extract value or fold later based on card and sizing.

Board: T♥ 2♠ 5♣ A♣. I have 6♥5♦ — second pair with a weak kicker. Flash (button) opens wide preflop and c‑bet a lot; he barreled the flop and I called, then he checked the turn. That check polarizes his range toward missed overcards, small pairs and pot‑control Tx/Ax combos rather than big two‑pair/sets (which usually continue). I want to extract thin value from worse (Kx/Qx/Jx/Tx) and charge any free river card, but I don’t want to inflate a pot OOP with a vulnerable second pair. A small probe bet of ~1/3 pot (400 into 1200) maximizes calls from worse hands while folding pure air, keeps the pot manageable, and preserves the option to fold to heavy aggression. Plan: bet 400; if he folds I take the pot, if he calls I re‑evaluate on the river, and if he check‑raises or shoves large I fold (we are often behind to the hands that would take that line). This is a low‑variance, +EV line given his tendencies and our stack dynamics.

Minni has led out on the turn after the Ace appeared, which is a significant scare card. My King-high has no showdown value and no strong draws to improve. Although the pot odds are tempting, calling with air against a lead on an Ace-high board is risky, especially since Minni's lead often indicates she has either paired the Ace or is betting a hand that already beats King-high. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

Heads-up on the button (SB) with K♥9♦ — this is a strong opening hand in heads-up where we should be very wide and take the initiative. Raising to 200 (2x BB / min-raise) applies pressure to the BB, builds the pot when we have position, and keeps the pot manageable against Flash’s ~67BB effective stack. Plan: c-bet many favorable flops and use positional advantage to extract value; call small 3-bets to play postflop but fold to very large 3-bets/4-bets that polarize his range. Overall raising maximizes our fold equity and leverages postflop skill edge.

Holding Jc 8s in the big blind, I am facing a min-raise from the button (Minni). I need to call 100 more to win a total pot of 400, giving me 3:1 pot odds. J8o has sufficient equity against a wide button opening range to justify a call, even out of position. I will defend my blind and see a flop.

I have flopped middle pair with an eight kicker on a board containing a Queen and a Jack. In this situation, checking to the pre-flop aggressor is the standard and most prudent play. My hand has showdown value but is vulnerable to better Jacks or any Queen, and it functions well as a bluff-catcher. By checking, I control the size of the pot and allow Minni to continue with her wider range of bluffs or semi-bluffs.

I opened preflop and have the initiative heads-up. Flop Q♠ J♦ 3♦ is two-tone and Q‑high — my K♥9♦ is just K‑high but has some equity: a gutshot to a Ten (4 outs) and runner/runner diamond possibilities. Flash defends widely from the BB and will have a lot of unpaired hands and diamond backdoors, so a continuation bet here will win many hands and charge draws. I’ll size to get reasonable fold equity while keeping the pot controllable vs his ~66BB stack — bet ~60% pot to force folds and charge draws. If called I’ll re-evaluate on the turn (continue with relevant cards, fold to large polarizing check‑raises).

On a board of Qs 3d Jd, I have flopped middle pair with Jc 8s. Minni, as the pre-flop aggressor, has followed up with a continuation bet of 250 into a 400 pot. This is a standard sizing. While there is an overcard (the Queen) and potential diamond and straight draws (KT, T9), my middle pair is too strong to fold on the flop. However, my kicker (8) is weak, and raising would likely only isolate me against better hands like a Queen or a stronger Jack. Therefore, calling is the most appropriate action to keep her bluffs in and see how the turn develops while controlling the pot size.

The turn King of Spades is a significant card that interacts well with the button's opening range. My middle pair of Jacks has now been demoted to the third-best pair on a board showing King, Queen, and Jack. This board is highly coordinated with straight draws (AT, T9) and diamond draws. Given I am out of position and my hand's relative strength has decreased, I must check to control the pot and see how Minni responds. Betting would likely only be called by hands that beat me and would force out bluffs.

Board Q♠ 3♦ J♦ K♠, my hand K♥9♦ — I just improved to top pair with a weak kicker. I was the preflop raiser and c‑bet the flop; Flash called, so his range is wide (Qx, Jx, diamond draws, some T9/Tx, small pairs). He checks the turn, which often shows marginal hands or draws that I can charge. Betting here extracts value from worse pairs (Qx, Jx and weaker Ks), charges diamond/straight draws (and T9 straights), and keeps the initiative. I’ll size to about 60% pot: 540 chips into a 900 pot. This gives good fold equity, denies equity to draws, and keeps the pot controlled vs his ~6.35k effective stack. If he check‑raises big (polarizing), I’ll seriously re‑evaluate and likely fold to heavy pressure; if he calls I’ll re‑assess the river and extract more value on safe runouts.

The turn King of Spades is a very unfavorable card for my hand. My middle pair of Jacks has been demoted to third pair on a highly coordinated board (K-Q-J-3). This board texture hits the Button's opening range extremely well, making it likely that Minni has at least a Queen, a King, or even a straight (with any Ten). Her 540 bet into a 900 pot shows significant strength. Given my weak kicker and the fact that I am out of position with very little equity to improve to the best hand, calling would be unprofitable. I will fold and preserve my remaining chips.

In heads-up play, K5o is a solid starting hand from the button/small blind. With a stack of 63BB, I want to maintain pressure and avoid being too passive against Minni, who has a significant chip lead. Raising to 300 is a standard play here to either take the pot immediately or set up a post-flop situation where I have the initiative.

Facing a standard button open to 300. I’m in the big blind with Q♠6♣ — a marginal hand but worth defending heads-up. I’ve already posted 100, so calling 200 more (300 total committed) gives decent pot odds (200 to win ~600, ~25% equity needed) and keeps the pot manageable out of position. I’ll avoid 3-betting with this weak kicker and plan to proceed cautiously postflop: continue if I pair the Q or pick up a good draw, otherwise fold to heavy aggression.

I missed this flop with Q♠6♣ on 8♠-5♥-4♣ — no pair, only a 7 gives me a gutshot (4 outs) and there’s a thin backdoor spade possibility. Pot is small (600) and I have a big stack advantage vs Flash’s 6050, so I don’t need to bluff here. I’ll check to control the pot size and induce bluffs; if Flash fires a small-to-medium c-bet (~300–600) I’ll call to realize equity and reevaluate on the turn. I will fold to heavy aggression/large raises without improvement. Checking preserves flexibility and keeps weaker parts of my range in the hand.

I have flopped middle pair (fives) with a King kicker on an 8-5-4 rainbow board. Minni has checked to me after calling my pre-flop raise. While this board can hit a Big Blind's defending range with hands like 67, 78, or small pairs, my hand is currently ahead of most of her air and overcard combinations. I will place a continuation bet of 300 (half pot) to protect my equity against overcards, extract value from straight draws or a pair of fours, and gain information on the strength of Minni's hand. Checking back is a viable alternative to control the pot size with a marginal hand, but a bet here helps deny free equity to hands like J-10 or Q-J.

Heads-up OOP with Q♠6♣ on 8♠-5♥-4♣. I checked to control the pot and Flash made a standard small c‑bet of 300 into a 900 pot. Calling 300 gives me roughly 10 clean outs (3 queens, 3 sixes, 4 sevens) and about ~38% to improve by the river, while my immediate pot odds to call are ~20% (300 to win ~1500) — so a call is +EV. I have a very large stack advantage and no realistic flush draw here (only one spade in my hand and one on board), so a check‑raise/bluff OOP is unnecessary and would risk getting committed with a marginal hand. Plan: call to realize equity, see the turn, and fold to heavy continued aggression if I don’t improve; if I pair or make the straight I’ll extract value. Call 300.

The turn (8♠ 5♥ 4♣ 5♦) leaves me with no pair and only drawing equity (3 Qs + 3 6s + 4 7s = 10 outs ≈ 21.7% to hit the river). Flash checked to me, so there’s no fold equity to seize by bluffing OOP and betting into a pot of 1,200 while holding a marginal hand. With a large stack advantage and a relatively small pot, I should control the pot size and see a free river — check behind. Plan: check to the river, call small bets when I have decent odds to realize my ~22% equity, and fold to large river aggression unless I improve to a pair/straight.

I have improved to trip fives on the turn. Since Minni called my flop bet, she likely has a piece of the board, such as an 8, a 4, or a straight draw like 7-6. I will bet 800 into the 1200 pot to extract value from her marginal hands and draws, while building a pot for my very strong hand.

I’m out of position with Q♠6♣ on 8♠-5♥-4♣-5♦. I have only 10 clean outs to improve (3 Qs, 3 6s, 4 7s) → ~21.7% to hit the river. The pot is 2000 and it costs me 800 to call, so I need ~28.6% equity to make a break-even call (800/(2000+800)). My equity is well below that, so a call is -EV if my only hope is to hit. Flash’s line (open pre, c-bet flop, another bet on the paired turn) is consistent with value (pairs, two-pair, or a five), and his bet sizing makes it unlikely he’s turning a wide bluff here. I have too little fold equity and too poor pot odds to justify a raise or call. With a very large overall stack I can wait for a better spot; fold and preserve my deep-stack advantage.

Heads-up on the button (small blind) with A♠9♣: this is a clear open-raise candidate. I have position postflop, an ace-blocker that reduces opponents’ premium Ax/AA combos, and a hand that plays well multi-street when called. Flash (BB) has a relatively wide defending/calling profile, so I want to apply pressure and build initiative while keeping the sizing reasonable versus his ~68 BB stack. I’ll make a standard 3x-style open to 300 (above the 200 min) — big enough to extract value and get folds, but small enough to keep poorer hands and dominated holdings in his calling range. Plan: if called, use positional advantage to c-bet favorable flops and fold to heavy action on very hostile 3-bets; if Flash 3-bets large, be prepared to fold A9o rather than commit big chips out of position.

Queen-Ten offsuit (QTo) is a very strong hand to defend the Big Blind with against a standard 3x raise from the Button, especially in a heads-up situation. My stack is roughly 68.5 BB, which gives me plenty of room to play post-flop. Minni has been aggressive throughout the tournament, and folding a hand this strong would be overly tight. I will call to see the flop and evaluate based on board texture, as raising might bloat the pot too much against a significantly larger stack.

I have missed the flop completely with Queen-Ten offsuit on an Ace-high board. This board texture significantly favors Minni's pre-flop raising range. While I do hold the Queen of clubs, giving me some backdoor flush potential, I have no made hand and no strong draw. I will check to the aggressor and prepared to fold to most standard continuation bets.

I have A♠9♣ on A♦3♣8♣ — top pair with a decent kicker and one club (three clubs total including board). Heads-up I opened preflop and have position, plus my Ace blocks many strong Ax combos in BB’s calling range. The flop is slightly wet (two clubs), so I want to charge club draws and get value from worse aces and medium pairs while keeping the pot controllable. Flash defended a wide range preflop and checked the flop, so a half-pot c‑bet is the right mix of value and protection. I’ll bet ~50% pot (300). Plan: if called, continue to evaluate on the turn and extract value or pot-control depending on the card; if check‑raised big, assume a very strong hand or committed draw and re‑evaluate (likely fold to heavy aggression to protect my stack).

I have missed the flop completely on an Ace-high board, and Minni has followed up with a continuation bet. Given my hand is only Queen-high with no direct draw (only a runner-runner club possibility with my Queen of clubs), it is not worth continuing. The board heavily favors the pre-flop aggressor's range, and calling here would be a mistake against Minni's likely stronger holdings or continued aggression. I will fold.

I am on the button with T8o. In a heads-up situation, this hand is strong enough to open for a raise. I have approximately 66 big blinds, and while Minni has a significant chip lead, I need to maintain an aggressive posture to accumulate chips. A standard 3x raise to 300 is a consistent play I've used throughout the tournament to take control of the pot pre-flop.

Flash (button/SB) opened to 300. I’m in the big blind with 7♠5♦ — a weak offsuit hand but facing only a 3x open in heads-up. Pot is 400 and I need to call 200 more, which gives ~3:1 pot odds (need ~25% equity). Versus a wide button opening range this hand has borderline but acceptable equity, and I don’t want to create bloated OOP pots by 3-betting with junk. I’ll defend by calling to 300 total and see a flop, then play cautiously out of position: fold to heavy aggression unless I improve to a pair/strong draw, otherwise look for cheap showdown or pot-control lines.

I’m out of position with 7♠5♦ on 8♠ Q♦ 3♥ — no pair and no realistic draws. Flash opened preflop and will c-bet a wide range, but I don’t want to build a pot OOP with air. Check to control the pot and look for a cheap turn; plan is to fold to any meaningful bet and only continue if I improve to a pair or a genuine draw.

I have middle pair (Eights) with a Ten kicker on a dry board of Q-8-3. This is a standard medium-strength hand. While a continuation bet could fold out some overcards, checking back is a solid play to control the pot size and see a turn card for free. This allows me to realize my equity and potentially induce bluffs or get value on later streets if the board stays relatively safe.

I have 7♠5♦ on 8♠ Q♦ 3♥ 10♥ — no pair and no realistic draw. Both players checked the flop, so Flash’s range includes many Qx and better, and he’s an aggressive preflop raiser who can punish bluffs or build pots OOP. I don’t want to bloat the pot out of position with essentially zero equity; a bet just invites a raise and a difficult decision. Pot control is the +EV line: check the turn and fold to meaningful aggression. If checked to on the river I’ll re-evaluate for a cheap showdown or only bluff when the board/blocks justify it.

I have improved to two pair (Tens and Eights) on the turn. After checking back the flop, my hand is well-disguised, and Minni has checked for a second time. This is a clear spot to bet for value against a Queen, an Eight, or various straight and flush draws (like J9 or hearts) that Minni might be holding. A bet of 400 into the 600 pot is a standard sizing to build the pot while still being likely to get called by weaker hands.

Flash fired a meaningful turn bet (400) into a 1000 pot. Calling 400 would win 1400, so I’m getting ~3.5:1 (need ≈22% equity). With 7♠5♦ on 8♠ Q♦ 3♥ 10♥ I have no realistic draws — only 6 river outs to pair (≈13% equity), which is well below the required price. I’m out of position and raising would be a high-variance pure bluff with poor blockers and a willing opponent stack (5950) who can easily force me off or commit me. Fold and preserve my large stack for a better spot.

Heads-up on the button (small blind) with 4♠3♦ — a weak hand but well within a standard heads-up open-raise range. Pot is 150 and it’s only 50 to call; I prefer to take the initiative and apply pressure to Flash’s big blind rather than limp. Raising to 300 (total) is a standard, respectable size that gives good fold equity, keeps the pot manageable against a ~68bb effective stack, and lets me play position postflop. If Flash 3-bets big I’ll fold this holding; if he calls I’ll c-bet favorable textures and proceed cautiously to significant resistance.

I am holding Queen-Ten of hearts in the big blind. This is a very strong broadway suited hand in a heads-up or short-handed situation. Minni, who has a significant chip lead, has raised to 300 from the button. My hand is well within the defending range against a button raise. I will call the 200 additional chips to see a flop and use my hand's post-flop potential, as the price is right and the hand is too strong to fold.

I have flopped top pair with a Ten kicker on a board of 6-7-Q. As the Big Blind defending against the Button's pre-flop raise, the standard play is to check to the aggressor. My hand is strong, and I want to see how Minni acts. Checking also keeps the pot manageable and allows Minni to bluff with her air or marginal hands.

Heads-up I opened a very wide button range with 4♠3♦, so on this Q♦7♣6♥ flop my range is stronger than a single random hand and I have position. The flop is relatively dry (rainbow, only modest straight possibilities), so a continuation bet will fold out many of Flash’s non-pair overcards and weak holdings he called preflop with. I don’t want to bloat the pot too much with air against an effective ~66bb stack, so I’ll use a standard ~1/2‑pot c-bet to get fold equity but keep the pot manageable. If he check‑raises large I’ll fold this holding; if he calls I’ll proceed cautiously on the turn given I have no pair and only backdoor possibilities.

I have top pair with a Ten kicker on a relatively dry board. Minni's bet of 300 into a 600 pot is a standard continuation bet that could represent anything from a total bluff to a monster. Given my hand strength, I am definitely continuing. While a raise is an option to protect against straight draws like 89 or 58, I prefer to call here to keep her bluffs in and minimize risk against a potential better Queen or a set, especially given the significant chip lead Minni holds. Calling allows me to evaluate the turn and keeps the pot manageable.

I have Top Pair, Ten Kicker. On the turn 4c, the board remains relatively safe, although some straight draws like 58 are developing. Being out of position against a massive stack who was the pre-flop aggressor, checking is the most prudent play. It allows me to control the pot size and gives Minni the opportunity to continue her aggression with a wider range of hands, including bluffs. I don't want to build a massive pot and potentially face a difficult decision for my remaining tournament chips against a superior Queen (AQ, KQ) or a slow-played monster, especially given the significant stack depth disparity.

I check. The 4♣ on the turn pairs my 4 and I now have bottom pair, but I'm still behind a large portion of Flash's flop‑call range (Qx, 7x, 6x, pocket pairs). With position and a big stack advantage I prefer pot control — betting here mainly gets called by better hands or only thin value from worse. Checking keeps bluffs in his range, lets me see the river for more information, and allows me to call a reasonable river bet or fold to clear strength. Pot is 1200; rather than bloating the pot with a marginal hand I will check and re-evaluate on the river.

I have top pair with a Ten kicker (Qh Th). After calling Minni's continuation bet on the flop, the turn went check-check, which suggests Minni might have a marginal hand or a draw that she didn't want to semi-bluff. The river Jack of Clubs (Jc) completes a backdoor flush but doesn't change much for most common straight draws. Checking here is the most balanced play; it allows me to see if Minni will bluff with her missed draws (like 89 or 58) while also preventing me from overcommitting my tournament life against a potentially better Queen or a slow-played monster. If she checks back, I likely have the best hand.

Final board 6♥ 7♣ Q♦ 4♣ J♣; my hand 4♠3♦ = bottom pair with a very weak kicker. Flash’s line (call preflop, call my flop c‑bet, then check turn and river) looks like lots of Qx, 7x, 6x and small pairs or missed draws — many of which beat me or will call a small river bet. There is minimal fold equity on the river and little value to extract (hands worse than me are rare or folded earlier); betting risks a raise or a call from all better hands. With a big effective stack advantage and a small pot, the prudent, EV‑positive play is pot control and take it to showdown. I check to avoid inflating the pot with a marginal holding and will respond to any river aggression based on sizing (call small thin bets, fold to strong raises).

I am on the button (Small Blind) with T3o. This is a very weak hand, even in a heads-up situation. Minni has a significant chip lead (over 4x my stack), which allows her to be more aggressive and pressure me. While I could attempt to steal the blinds with a raise, T3o doesn't play well post-flop, and Minni has shown she is willing to defend her blinds and call raises frequently. Folding is the safest play here to preserve my 75BB stack and wait for a better spot to engage with the chip leader.

Heads-up on the button I should be aggressive and open a wide range. 9♠2♦ is a trash hand, but the raise-to-200 (min raise) is a small, low-risk pressure play that wins the pot often preflop and lets me play the hand in position. I’ve already posted 50 SB, so raising to 200 is +150 more chips. If Flash 3-bets to a meaningful size I’ll fold this holding; if he calls I’ll continuation-bet favorable flops and proceed cautiously — this is primarily a steal attempt to take the initiative rather than a hand to commit heavy chips with.

I am in the Big Blind with Jack-Trey of spades. Minni has raised to 200 from the Small Blind/Button. I only need to call 100 more into a pot of 300, giving me 3:1 odds. While J3s is a weak hand, in a heads-up situation against an aggressive chip leader, it is strong enough to defend the blind given the favorable price. I will call to see the flop and play cautiously from there.

The flop is monotone with three hearts (9h 5h 6h). I have no hearts in my hand and no connection to the board with my Jack-Trey of spades. Given Minni's aggressive history and massive stack, there is little reason to lead into this board or attempt a bluff at this stage. I will check and evaluate based on Minni's action, though I am prepared to fold to almost any significant bet.

I have top pair (9♠) with a very weak 2-kicker on a wet, heart‑heavy flop (9♥‑5♥‑6♥). Flash checked to me, so his range is wide: pocket pairs, broadways, suited connectors and some two‑heart combos that would already have a flush. This is still a good spot to continuation‑bet — I want to charge draws (both flush and combo straight+flush hands like 7‑8 suited) and get value from worse one‑pair hands while keeping fold equity. I’ll size for value/protection rather than a tiny probe: ~60% pot forces hearts and many draws to pay to see a turn. Plan: if he folds I take the pot; if he calls I re‑evaluate on the turn (if a heart or a card that completes obvious straights/sets I will proceed cautiously and likely check or fold to heavy aggression). If he check‑raises large I will assume a very strong made hand and fold.

I have Jack-Trey of spades on a monotone heart board (9h 5h 6h). I have no hearts, no pair, and no real draw. Minni has bet 250 into a 400 pot. Given that I have zero equity and am out of position against a massive chip leader, there is no justification for calling or bluff-raising. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am on the button with 6d2c, a very weak hand. The pot is 150 and it costs 50 more to call. Given my stack size of 7250 against Minni's 32600, there is no strategic advantage to playing this trash hand out of position post-flop or trying to steal against a significantly larger stack. Folding is the most prudent play to conserve my remaining chips for better opportunities.

Heads-up on the button with J♠7♠: this is a playable, suited hand with decent post‑flop playability and I have positional advantage after the flop. Pot is 150 and it only costs 50 to see a flop, but limp/calling here surrenders initiative and gives the big blind good odds. I want to take the lead and apply pressure while keeping the pot manageable, so I’ll open to a standard 3x BB sizing. Raising to 300 (total) gives solid fold equity, denies cheap draws to the BB, and keeps room to maneuver with 71.5 effective BBs; if called I can c‑bet many dry flops and use position. If Flash 3‑bets large I will fold this marginal hand; I may call a small 3‑bet occasionally to realize equity, but default is to raise to 300 now.

In this heads-up situation, Minni has opened with a 3x raise to 300. I am holding King-Deuce offsuit (K2o), which is a marginal hand with poor playability post-flop, especially out of position. Comparing this to Hand #82, where I folded Queen-Ten offsuit (QTo) to a similar raise, folding K2o is a consistent and disciplined choice. My goal is to preserve my stack of 71.5 big blinds for hands that offer better equity or post-flop potential, rather than calling and likely being forced to fold on most flop textures against a larger stack.

I am holding Jc Tc on the button, which is a strong starting hand in heads-up play. With a stack of 7100, I want to play aggressively and build a pot with a hand that has good post-flop potential. I will raise to my standard 3x blind amount.

Facing a standard heads‑up open to 300 from the button. My hand 7♥4♣ is unsuited, disconnected and heavily dominated by a typical button raising range. The pot is 400 and I must call 200 (2:1), but my raw equity vs an opener is likely below the break‑even point and the hand has terrible postflop playability and limited implied odds against an opponent with ~6.8k behind. Raising as a bluff is unnecessary and risky; with a large overall stack advantage I should conserve chips and wait for a much better spot. Fold.

Heads-up on the button (postflop position) — 9♦5♣ is marginal but playable. The pot is 150 and it only costs 50 to see a flop, but opening to the minimum 200 (2×BB) is the better exploitative play: it applies pressure to a wide BB range, gives us initiative and fold equity, and lets us play the flop in position. If called the pot stays manageable relative to our 32.7k stack and Flash’s 7.15k. I’ll raise to 200 to open a wide heads-up range and take the lead on the flop.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Six offsuit. Minni, who has a significant chip lead, has raised to 2x (200 total). I only need to call 100 more to see a flop. Ace-high has decent equity against a button opening range, and I am getting 3:1 odds on the call. Folding is too tight here, and while I could 3-bet as a bluff, calling allows me to realize equity and see how the flop connects with my hand without bloating the pot unnecessarily against a massive stack.

On a low, somewhat connected flop of 2-5-4, I have Ace-high and a gutshot straight draw (to a 6-high straight). Being out of position against a large stack like Minni, the standard and most prudent play is to check. This allows me to see if she continues her aggression and decide whether to call based on the bet sizing, or to take a free card if she checks back.

I have top pair (5s) with a 9 kicker on a low, relatively dry flop 2c-5s-4h and I'm in position after opening preflop. Flash's flat-call preflop is wide heads-up, so a lot of his range is behind me (overcards, small pairs, floats) while the hands that beat me (sets, rare two-pair) are uncommon. I want value and to charge the gutshot/runner draws (3x/6x and any backdoor clubs) while keeping worse hands calling. A ~50% pot c-bet is the right balance here: bet 200 into 400 to extract value from worse one-pair hands and deny equity to draws without bloating the pot. Plan: if he calls, continue evaluating the turn (barrel blanks for value, be cautious to strong turn cards like 3/6/2/4); if he check-raises large I’ll give credit to sets/two-pair and fold or re-evaluate by sizing.

On this low, connected flop, I have Ace-high and a gutshot straight draw (any 3 makes a 6-high straight). Minni's 200-chip bet into a 400-chip pot gives me 4:1 pot odds (200 to call a 1000-chip total pot). With several outs to potentially the best hand (any 3 for a straight, and possibly any Ace or Six), I have more than enough equity to justify a call. Folding would be too tight given the odds and the potential of my hand, and raising is unnecessary as it would likely only force out hands I'm already beating while bloating the pot against a chip leader.

On the turn, the board pairs with a second five. This doesn't significantly change the value of my Ace-high and gutshot straight draw, but it is a card that generally favors the caller's range. However, out of position against a massive chip leader, my most consistent strategy is to check. This allows me to see if Minni continues her aggression or checks back, helping me to control the pot size while retaining equity with my straight draw and Ace-high showdown value.

I have trip fives (5c) on 2c-5s-4h-5d — a very strong hand heads-up. Flash called my flop c-bet, so his range is wide (one-pair hands, floats and some backdoor draws). Betting for value and protection is correct: a half-pot bet keeps worse hands and floats in, charges any straight/runner draws, and doesn’t bloat the pot too much against the rarer full-house/quads combos. I’ll bet 400 into the 800 pot to extract value; if he check-raises big I’ll give credit to a full house/quads and re-evaluate (likely fold to extreme aggression), if he just calls I’ll plan to get more value on appropriate rivers.

I have Ace-high and a gutshot straight draw (to a six-high straight) on a paired board. In heads-up play, Ace-high retains significant value, especially against an aggressive opponent like Minni who has shown a tendency to apply pressure with a wide range of hands. The pot odds are 3:1 (400 to call a 1200 pot), which provides sufficient equity to continue given my potential to improve to a straight with any three or potentially win at showdown with Ace-high if she is betting with a draw or a weaker hand. Folding would be too exploitable in this position, and calling allows me to see a river card and evaluate the final board while maintaining some pot control.

On the river, the board is 2c-5s-4h-5d-8d. I have Ace-high (Ac-6h). I have called two streets of value from Minni, who has been aggressive. I failed to improve my hand to a straight or a pair. However, in heads-up play, Ace-high has some showdown value against missed draws (like 76, which missed its straight). Betting here would likely not fold out a better hand and would only be called by hands that beat me. Checking is the most prudent play to either see a cheap showdown if Minni checks back or to evaluate a potential bet from her based on the sizing.

I have trip fives (5♣) on 2♣-5♠-4♥-5♦-8♦ and I’m in position. Flash’s line (flatting pre, check-calling flop and turn, then checking the river) looks like a wide, mostly medium-strength calling range — single pairs, floats and some missed draws — rather than frequent full houses/quads (those would more often have raised earlier). Betting for thin value is correct. I want a size that extracts calls from worse pairs (8x, single pair hands, some turned pairs/overcards that will call) while still looking natural. I’ll make a pot-sized value bet to maximize thin-value calls; if he check-raises big I’ll give credit to a full house and re-evaluate (likely fold to extreme aggression).

On a final board of 2c-5s-4h-5d-8d, I am holding Ace-high (Ac-6h). Minni has triple-barreled this hand, culminating in a pot-sized bet of 1600 on the river. While Ace-high has some showdown value in heads-up play, the board is highly coordinated. Any hand containing a 3 (like A3 or 63) makes a straight, and any 5 makes trips. Furthermore, even a simple pair of deuces, fours, or eights beats my hand. Minni's pot-sized bet is highly polarizing, usually representing either a very strong hand or a complete bluff. Given that I have already committed 800 chips and calling another 1600 would represent a significant portion of my remaining stack, I believe the risk-to-reward ratio for a bluff catch with only Ace-high is unfavorable. Most of Minni's range that she would bet for value here (A3, 36, any 5, or any pocket pair) has me beaten. Therefore, the most disciplined play is to fold and preserve my stack.

I am on the button with 9s 7s, which is a strong starting hand in heads-up play. It has good connectivity and flush potential. I will open with a standard 3x raise to 300 to take the initiative and build a pot against Minni, while keeping my stack size in mind relative to her significant chip lead.

5♦2♥ is one of the weakest hands in poker — virtually no showdown value and poor post‑flop playability. Facing a standard button open to 300, calling 200 into a 400 pot gives ~3:1 pot odds (need ≈25% equity). 5‑2 offsuit is heavily dominated by most of the button’s opening range and doesn’t have the equity or blockers to justify a call or a light 3‑bet. With a large stack advantage I should conserve chips and wait for a much better spot to apply pressure. Fold and preserve stack depth for profitable opportunities.

Heads-up on the button (small blind) I have A♦9♣ — a strong hand to play aggressively. I prefer to raise rather than limp to take the initiative and use positional advantage post‑flop. I’ll make it a standard 3x BB open to 300 total (I’ve already posted 50, so add 250) — big enough to get fold equity and discourage too many light 3‑bets while leaving both stacks deep. If Flash 3‑bets big I’ll re‑evaluate (likely fold to an oversized 3‑bet, call smaller ones), and if he calls I’ll c‑bet favorable boards and extract value from worse aces/broadways.

I am in the Big Blind with Ten-Seven offsuit. Minni has raised to 300 (3x) from the Button. While I am getting decent odds to call, Ten-Seven offsuit is a marginal hand to defend out of position, especially against a player with a significant chip lead who has been active. Since I have around 64 big blinds, I can afford to wait for a stronger starting hand or a better spot to challenge the chip leader. I will fold and preserve my stack.

I am in the Small Blind with 84 offsuit. This is a very weak hand. While I have been aggressive from the button in previous hands, Minni has a massive chip lead and has been defending their big blind or playing back effectively. Given the stack depth disparity and the weakness of my hand, I will simply fold and wait for a better spot to utilize my remaining chips.

Heads-up on the button with Q♠9♣ — a playable hand that benefits from initiative and position. Flash in the big blind has 6,300 (≈63BB effective). I’ll raise to 300 total (3×BB): that sizing maximizes fold equity vs a wide BB defense, builds a pot I can play postflop in position, and is not so large that a standard 3‑bet commits me unnecessarily. If he folds I scoop the 150 pot; if he calls I have decent playability (can hit top pair, second pair, and some straights) and will continuation-bet favorable boards. If Flash 3-bets big I will re-evaluate/fold rather than commit with Q9o. Action: raise to 300 total.

I am in the Big Blind with K7o. Minni has been very active and currently holds a massive chip lead. K7o is a reasonable hand to defend in a heads-up situation against a button open, especially since the price to call is only 200 more into a 400 pot. I'll call to see a flop and see if I can catch a piece of it, though I'll need to be cautious given the stack disparity and Minni's aggression.

I have flopped middle pair (Kings) on a very coordinated board that contains three spades and an Ace. I do not hold a spade, which makes this hand quite vulnerable to both a flush and any Ace in the opponent's range. Given Minni's significant chip lead and aggressive history, I will check to see how they react to this board. This allows me to control the pot size and gather more information before committing more chips.

Flop: Ks As 4s. I hold Qs9c — I don't have a pair but I have the nut-flush draw: my Q♠ is the highest possible hole spade given A♠ and K♠ on board, so I have 9 clean outs (~35% to hit by the river). Heads‑up vs a wide BB calling range this is an excellent semi‑bluff. Betting realizes equity, builds a pot when I hit, and gets folds from many non‑spade hands (we can fold out weak pairs/Kx and other bluffs). I’ll size to ~1/2 pot (300 into 600) to keep decent fold equity without overcommitting; if I get a large check‑raise I’ll fold (Q9o has poor showdown value), if I get a single call I’ll continue to realize my draw and re‑evaluate on the turn (barrel if we improve or take a free card / control pot if not).

I have second pair (Kings) on a very coordinated board with three spades and an Ace. While I don't hold a spade for flush protection, folding second pair to a single half-pot bet is too tight in a heads-up situation, especially given the 4:1 pot odds and Minni's known aggression. Raising would be counterproductive as it would only be called by better hands like an Ace or a flush. I will call the 300 bet to see a turn card and re-evaluate based on the next street's action and whether a fourth spade or another overcard appears.

I have middle pair (Kings) on a board that is extremely wet with an Ace and three spades already present. The Jack of Diamonds on the turn doesn't improve my hand and actually adds more straight possibilities for my opponent. Since I am out of position and facing a massive chip leader who has been aggressive, I will check to see if I can get to a cheap showdown or evaluate their bet sizing. I have no flush draw to fall back on, so pot control is essential.

Board Ks As 4s Jd, my hand Qs9c. On the flop I had three spades on board + Q♠ in my hand (four cards to a flush) so I’m one spade away — I have 9 spade outs (9/46 ≈ 19.6% to hit the river). Villain called my flop c‑bet and then checked the turn, which is a good spot to apply pressure with a semi‑bluff: I can take the pot now against many Kx, weaker Ax and pocket pairs and build a pot if I hit. I’ll bet ~1/2 pot (600 into 1200) — big enough to get folds from a decent portion of his checking range, but not so large that a single raise commits me with only a draw. If I’m raised substantially I’ll fold (I don’t want to call big raises with no pair); if called I’ll evaluate the river (if a spade I’ll have the best hole‑card flush in most cases; otherwise I’ll proceed cautiously).

I currently hold second pair (Kings) on a very wet board featuring three spades and an Ace. Minni, who has been aggressive throughout the tournament and holds a massive chip lead, has placed a half-pot bet of 600 into an 1,200 pot. This gives me pot odds of 3:1, meaning I only need to be ahead 25% of the time to make a profitable call. In a heads-up situation, folding second pair to a single moderate bet on the turn is often too tight, as the opponent's range can include many semi-bluffs (like a single high spade) or pure air. While the Jack of Diamonds is a scary card that adds straight possibilities, I will call this bet to see a river. I will likely fold to significant aggression on the river if the board does not improve my hand or if a fourth spade appears, but for now, the price is right to see the final card.

The river card is the Jack of Clubs, which pairs the board and makes the final community cards As-Ks-4s-Jd-Jc. My hand is two pair, Kings and Jacks with an Ace kicker (Kd-Ks-Jc-Jd-As). While this is a decent hand, I am vulnerable to any Ace (which makes Aces and Jacks), any Jack (which makes three of a kind), or any flush (three spades on board). Given Minni's aggression on the flop and turn, and the fact that she holds a massive chip lead, it's very possible she has a hand that beats mine. Betting into her here is risky because I'm unlikely to be called by worse hands, and I might get raised by her value range. I will check to see her reaction and ideally reach a showdown cheaply.

I bet the flop and turn as a semi‑bluff with the nut spade draw but missed the river (board Ks As 4s Jd Jc). Villain called both streets, which strongly suggests a made holding or a very sticky caller (A‑x, K‑x, pocket pair or a made two‑spade flush). Pot is 2,400 and opponent has ~5,200 behind — to make a river bluff profitable I need a high fold frequency (e.g. a pot‑size bluff of 2,400 requires >50% folds; a half‑pot bluff of 1,200 requires >33% folds). Given he called twice already, his fold frequency here is likely below those thresholds, and a large bluff risks ~5k if called. With almost no showdown value (Q-high) and poor likelihood of folding out enough of his calling range, I’ll avoid unnecessary risk and check to take the showdown or induce a value bet from bluffs. Action: check.

I am on the Button with Five of Hearts and Deuce of Spades, which is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Given the significant stack disadvantage against Minni and the fact that we are nearing the final stretch of the tournament, there is no strategic reason to commit chips with this hand. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity to protect my remaining 75BB.

Heads-up on the button with 9♣2♥ — a garbage hand, but in heads-up play the open-raise range should be very wide. I want initiative and to leverage position, so I’ll open-raise to the minimum (200 total). The small sizing keeps Flash’s calling/3-bet range wide while risking only a small portion of my deep stack. Plan: if he folds I scoop the blinds; if he calls I’ll continuation-bet frequently and use position to fold to heavy resistance given my actual hand is air. If he 3-bets large or shoves I’ll fold this holding.

I am in the Big Blind with J2o, a very weak starting hand. Minni has raised to 200 from the Small Blind, and I need to call 100 more. While the pot odds are 3:1, J2o has very poor playability and is frequently dominated by Minni's opening range. Given the significant stack disparity (I have 7,450 to Minni's 32,250), it is more prudent to preserve my chips for a better hand than to defend with such a marginal holding.

In heads-up play, King-Ten offsuit is a strong hand, significantly above the average range. Holding it on the button gives me the advantage of position for the rest of the hand. I will put in a standard raise to 300 to build the pot and assert initiative.

Heads-up vs a standard 3x open from the button with Q♥5♥ in the big blind. The hand is marginal but suited and has reasonable equity vs a wide SB opening range. Pot is 400; calling 200 to make it 600 gives ~3:1 pot odds (need ~25% equity) — Q5s clears that threshold vs a typical open. I'm out of position, so I don't want to bloat the pot with a thin 3-bet; calling preserves pot control and lets me realize equity. Effective stacks are deep (~71bb effective for Flash), so I’ll play postflop straightforwardly: look to continue if I hit a Q or hearts/strong draws, check-call small C-bets, and fold to heavy aggression without improvement. Action: call to see the flop and keep weaker hands in the pot.

Check. I have Qh5h on Td–7h–Kh: second‑nut flush draw but no pair. Out of position vs a wide button opener I want pot control and to realize equity rather than donk‑bet and bloat the pot. There are 9 hearts remaining (≈19% to hit the turn, ≈35% by the river) plus a few pair outs — enough equity to call a standard c‑bet but not to build a big pot OOP. Plan: check and call small–medium c‑bets to see a turn; if the button checks back I take a free card and can barrel/lead if I improve; fold to very large check‑raises or overbets without improvement. This line preserves stack depth and keeps worse hands and bluffs in the pot when I hit.

I have flopped top two pair (Kings and Tens), which is an exceptionally strong hand on this board. My goal now is to extract value from weaker hands like a King with a lower kicker, a Ten, or a Seven, while also charging the various draws that are present (flush draws and straight draws like QJ). Since Minni checked to me, I will place a continuation bet of 400, which is approximately two-thirds of the pot, to build the pot and protect my hand.

Call 400. The pot is 1000 so I'm getting ~2.5:1 (need ~28.6% equity). Qh5h is a very strong drawing hand here — second‑nut heart draw plus pair outs. There are 9 hearts (≈19% to hit on the turn, ≈35% to hit the flush by river) and 3 queens + 3 fives = 15 total outs to the river (≈54% to make at least a pair or a flush, though pairing Q/5 can be ‘dirty’ versus a Kx). Versus a standard wide button opening range many of the villain’s continuation bets are Kx, Tx, draws and bluffs — my raw equity comfortably exceeds the price. I’m out of position, so raising would bloat the pot with a draw and reduce flexibility; calling preserves my options and keeps worse hands/bluffs in. Plan: call the flop, reevaluate on the turn — fold to very large aggression without improvement, call smaller turn bets to realize equity, and extract value if/when I complete the flush (be cautious vs an Ace‑heart).

Board is Td–7h–Kh–7s; I hold Qh5h — no pair but the second‑nut heart draw plus Q/5 pair outs. On the turn I have 15 clean outs (9 hearts + 3 queens + 3 fives) = 15/46 ≈ 32.6% to improve to at least a pair or the flush by the river. There is no bet to call, so the clear EV‑maximizing line is pot control and equity realization: checking keeps the pot manageable OOP and lets me see the river for free or at a price if Flash barrels. Leading OOP with only a draw bloats the pot and can induce a shove from his shorter stack that I’m a dog against (if he jams his 6750, I’d need ~45% equity to call profitably, which I don’t have). If he bets small–medium on the turn (roughly ≤ ~2/3 pot) I will call to realize equity (e.g., a 900 bet into 1400 gives ~2.6:1 → needs ~28% equity < my ~32.6%). I’ll fold to extreme overbets/jams absent a stronger read. If he checks back I get a free river and can extract value if I hit or bluff selectively. Action: check to realize equity and keep pot under control.

On the turn, the board pairs the Seven, which is a card that could potentially benefit Minni's range given she called from the big blind and then called a flop continuation bet. However, I still hold a very strong hand with Kings and Tens. I want to continue extracting value from weaker Kings, Tens, or draws (like the heart flush draw or the QJ straight draw). While the Seven is a 'scare card', checking back allows draws a free card to improve. I will place a bet of 800 to maintain the initiative and charge those draws, while still leaving myself room to evaluate if I face a check-raise.

Call 800. Pot is 2200 and Flash bets 800 into it (~36% pot). If I call the pot will be 3000 so I need ≈21% equity to justify a call. I hold Q♥5♥: there are 9 hearts + 3 queens + 3 fives = 15 outs to the river (15/46 ≈ 32.6% to make at least a pair or the flush), which comfortably beats the needed equity. Villain's line (small c‑bet on the flop and another small turn bet) is consistent with Kx/TX, thin value hands and bluffs; calling realizes my strong drawing equity out of position without bloating the pot. Raising OOP here would commit more with a draw, open me to a shove I don't want to call, and reduce flexibility on the river. Plan: call the turn, re-evaluate the river — extract value if I complete, bluff/raise selectively if appropriate, and fold to an extreme overbet or shove on the turn without improvement.

Check. Board Td–7h–Kh–7s–7c; I hold Qh5h. I missed my flush and pair — on the river I have only queen‑high and no realistic outs. Flash’s line (preflop raise, c‑bet on flop, another bet on the turn) is weighted toward value hands that now often become full houses (Kx, Tx, some 7x combos); his river check is either a trap with a full house or a concession with a busted draw. Betting as a bluff here has very thin fold equity against a shorter, aggressive opener and risks a check‑raise or a call from many hands that beat me. With no showdown improvement available, the lowest‑variance, highest‑EV play is to check and avoid bloating the pot — fold to any meaningful river aggression and only bluff/raise in this spot with a strong read that the opponent folds too often.

The board has run out Td-7h-Kh-7s-7c, meaning there are three Sevens on the board. My hand (Kc, Th) combines with the board to form a full house: Sevens full of Kings (7-7-7-K-K). While this is a very strong hand, the board texture is such that many hands in Minni's range (any King) now have the same full house, resulting in a chop. Additionally, any player holding the remaining Seven (7d) has Quads, and Pocket Aces would have a higher full house (7-7-7-A-A). Minni has check-called two streets and checked the river. If I bet, it is unlikely she will call with anything I beat (like a Ten or a lower pair) given the action, and she will certainly call with a King to chop or raise with a Seven. Checking back is the most prudent play to realize my equity and reach showdown safely.

Heads-up on the button (I’m also SB). 5♣T♠ is marginal but perfectly fine to raise in heads-up — I want to take the initiative, apply pressure to a shorter-stacked big blind (8,850) and not let him see a cheap flop. A standard open to 300 (about 3x BB / consistent with table sizing) maximizes fold equity while keeping the pot size reasonable if called. If he folds I scoop the 150 pot; if he calls I keep positional advantage and can c-bet or proceed cautiously depending on the flop. Raise to 300.

I am in the big blind with 7d 4d, a suited connector. Minni, who has a significant chip lead, has raised to 300 from the small blind. I need to put in 200 more to call into a pot of 400, giving me 2-to-1 odds. In heads-up play, 74s is a reasonable hand to defend with, as it has good playability post-flop and the potential to hit flushes or straights. Given the stack sizes and the tournament stage, folding is too tight, and 3-betting is unnecessarily risky with this specific hand. I will call to see a flop.

On a flop of Jh-8c-5h, I have a gutshot straight draw with my 7d 4d (needing a 6 to complete a 4-5-6-7-8 straight). Since I am out of position and Minni was the pre-flop aggressor, the standard play is to check to the raiser and see how they react. Betting out here doesn't accomplish much against a massive chip leader who is likely to continue or put pressure on me.

Heads-up and in position with second pair (5♣5 on Jh 8♣ 5♥). Flash’s preflop call indicates a wide range of overcards, weak pairs and some draws — a standard continuation bet will fold many overcards, charge heart/club draws and extract value from worse pairs and K/Q/Tx holdings. I’ll make a half-pot c-bet (300 into 600) to protect my pair and keep pressure on the shorter-stacked BB; if he raises big I’ll give it up, if he calls I’ll re-evaluate on the turn.

On this board of Jh-8c-5h, I have a gutshot straight draw (needing a 6 to complete a 4-5-6-7-8 straight). Minni has bet 300 into a 600 pot. To call, I need to commit 300 more to win a total pot of 900, giving me 3:1 odds. My direct equity with a 4-outer is roughly 8% for the next card, which is significantly lower than the 25% required by the pot odds. Given that I am out of position against a dominant chip leader who frequently barrels, and that my straight would not be the absolute nuts, I cannot justify a call based on equity or implied odds. I will fold and wait for a better spot to commit my remaining chips.

Q3o is a weak starting hand. Although the button generally opens wide in heads-up play, the opponent has a significant chip lead and has been playing aggressively. I choose to fold and wait for a more favorable hand to commit chips.

Heads-up on the button (I’m the SB) with 10♠9♣ — a very playable, aggressive heads-up hand. In heads-up play you should almost always open-raise from the button to take the initiative and leverage position post‑flop. Flash in the BB has a fairly wide defending range and 8.5k behind; I’m deep (31.3k), so I can build a pot and play postflop. I’ll raise to 300 (3x the BB) — large enough to get fold equity and deny cheap equity to many of his weak hands, but standard for the table. Plan: use position to c‑bet favorable flops, realize equity on dry boards, and fold to heavy 3‑bets if the sizing indicates a very strong hand; call small/medium 3‑bets and play postflop when appropriate.

In a heads-up situation, defending the big blind is important, but Q5o is a very weak hand to play out of position against a significant chip leader. While the pot odds are tempting, the hand lacks connectivity and post-flop playability. Given that I have 85 big blinds, I can afford to wait for a better hand to contest Minni's aggression rather than getting involved in a marginal spot where I will likely have to check-fold many flops.

I am on the button with K7o, which is a solid hand to open in a heads-up or short-handed situation. Given the stack depths (84.5 BB), I want to maintain aggression. Raising to 300 has been my standard open size, and it allows me to take control of the hand with position.

Heads-up vs a button open to 300 from the SB. I’m in the big blind with Q♥2♠. Pot is 400 and it’s 200 to me (I already have 100 in). Against a wide SB opening range in heads-up, defending the big blind is correct — folding is too tight given pot odds and the opener’s wide range, and 3-betting Q2o is poor because it’s often dominated and has weak postflop playability OOP. I’ll call 200 more (300 total committed) to see a flop, plan to play cautiously out of position: continue with top-pair/backdoor draws, check-fold to heavy aggression if I miss, and only inflate the pot with clear equity.

Board is 9♠ 6♥ 2♦; I hold Q♥2♠ — a marginal made hand (pair of deuces) and I’m out of position. Flash opened wide preflop, so his checking range includes both missed overcards and medium/weak pairs; there are very few draws on this dry, rainbow flop. Betting OOP with a small, frequently dominated pair risks bloating the pot or getting check-raised by better pairs; it doesn’t gain much fold equity versus hands that beat me (A2, pocket pairs). I’ll check to control the pot and extract at showdown when appropriate: call small continuation bets as a bluff-catcher / thin value call (roughly up to ~1/2 pot), but fold to heavy aggression or raises and re-evaluate if I improve on the turn.

I was the pre-flop aggressor and the flop of 9s-6h-2d is very dry, missing a large portion of the Big Blind's calling range. Since Minni checked, I will lead out with a standard continuation bet of 300 (50% pot) to take the pot down immediately. My King-high has some showdown value, but a bet here is the most effective way to maintain initiative.

I have bottom pair (2♠) with a Q kicker on a dry rainbow flop (9♠ 6♥ 2♦). Preflop I defended vs a wide button open, so this hand has reasonable showdown value. The opponent’s bet puts 300 more to me for a pot of ~900 — I’m getting ~3:1 pot odds (need ≈25% equity). Versus a wide c-bet/continuation range this single pocket pair has enough equity to call and see a turn. Raising OOP with Q2o would be reckless (dominated too often and bloats the pot), so I’ll just call to control the pot and re-evaluate on the turn. Plan: call now, fold to heavy turn aggression or a check-raise line, but continue/call small turn bets and extract value if I improve to two pair or better.

I check. I have Q♥2♠ — bottom pair (pair of deuces) on 9♠‑6♥‑2♦‑T♠. Flash opened wide preflop and c‑bet the flop, but he checked the turn: that line looks like pot control or weakness from a capped range. My hand has modest showdown value vs a wide range and only ~5 clean outs to improve (3 queens + 2 twos), so I don’t want to bloat the pot OOP with a marginal holding. Betting here risks getting check‑raised or simply getting called by all the hands that beat me (T‑x, 9‑x, medium pairs) while folding out very few worse hands. With deep stacks and a long tournament left, pot control is the best line: check to see a cheap river and then call small river bets or fold to large polarized raises; if I improve to two‑pair/trips on the river I’ll extract value aggressively.

On the turn Ten of Spades, the board has become slightly more coordinated. I have King-high, which has some showdown value but is likely behind any pair. Minni has checked twice, suggesting she may have a marginal made hand like a Six or a Nine, or perhaps a draw like 7-8. Betting here is unlikely to fold out a pair, and my hand is too weak to bet for value. Checking back allows me to control the pot size, realize my equity, and potentially win at showdown if the river is checked through.

I have Q♥2♠ — bottom pair (pair of deuces) on 9♠‑6♥‑2♦‑T♠‑J♦. Villain is a wide opener who c‑bet the flop but checked the turn; that line looks capped/weak or pot‑controlling. On this dry board my hand has modest showdown value versus his checking range and very little value‑betting potential OOP — many hands that would call a river bet (Jx, Tx, 9x, two‑pair/sets) actually beat me, and worse hands are likely to fold. Betting here would bloat the pot with a marginal holding and can induce a shove from polarized holdings I lose to. With the pot only 1,200 and villain short (7,900), the safest +EV line is to check to showdown and avoid creating tough decisions or bloating the pot OOP. I will check and call a small river bet, but fold to a large polarized raise representing two pair/set.

On the river Jack of Diamonds, the board is 9-6-2-T-J. I have King-high, which is unlikely to win at showdown if the hand is checked through, as it loses to any pair and all Ace-high hands. However, Minni has checked three times (flop, turn, and river), which suggests she has a marginal hand or a missed draw. While I could attempt to bluff, this specific board runout is quite connected (potential straights with Q8 or 87), and Minni's call on the flop indicates she likely has a piece of the board or a draw that may have arrived. Since I have some minimal showdown value and don't want to risk a check-raise or being called by a weak pair, I will check back and see the showdown.

Heads-up on the button with K♦J♦ — a strong, positionally advantaged hand. With deep stacks and the BB relatively wide, I want the initiative and to deny cheap flops, so I’ll open-raise rather than limp-call. A 3x BB sizing (to 300 total) is standard here: it builds the pot when I’m often ahead, keeps poorer holdings from getting correct odds, and gives good postflop playability in position. If Flash 3-bets I’ll re-evaluate, but for now raise to 300 to maximize fold equity and value.

In the Big Blind with T2o, I am facing a standard 3x open (300) from Minni on the button. While I have 7800 chips (78 BB), T2o is one of the weakest hands in poker and generally does not play well out of position, even in a heads-up situation. Given Minni's significant chip lead and aggressive history of button opens, I should maintain a solid range and avoid getting involved in marginal pots with trash hands. Folding is the most disciplined and long-term profitable play here.

I am on the button with Queen-Seven offsuit. This is a standard opening hand in heads-up play to maintain pressure and steal the blinds. Minni has a significant chip lead, so I should be selective, but Q7o is strong enough for a standard 3x raise to 300 to see where I stand.

Heads-up in the big blind with pocket 7s vs a button open to 300 (pot 400, 200 to call). This is a very playable hand against a wide opening range; I prefer to take the initiative rather than give a free flop. A small 3-bet to 500 (min 3-bet) achieves isolation/fold equity, builds value against worse hands and keeps the pot manageable if he calls. If Flash 4-bets big I’ll re-evaluate, but 3-betting here maximizes my EV in position postflop and leverages my deep stack.

I opened to 300 with Q7o on the button, which is a standard play in heads-up. Minni has responded with a very small 3-bet to 500. This 'min-click' only requires me to call an additional 200 chips into a pot of 800, giving me 4:1 odds. Despite Q7o being a marginal hand, the price is too good to pass up, especially since I will have position for the remainder of the hand. I will call to see a flop and re-evaluate based on the board texture.

Flop is 8h 4d 8d — a paired board with two diamonds. I hold 7c7s: an underpair and I’m out of position (BB acts first on the flop). Villain called my small 3-bet preflop so his range is wide but contains hands that crush me (any 8, pocket 4s, bigger pairs) as well as overcards and diamond combos. Betting out of position here will often get called by draws or better hands and can build a pot I’m not happy to play OOP. Checking keeps the pot under control, lets me realize equity against worse/missed hands, and induces bluffs from overcards or diamond hands. Plan: check and call small-to-medium C-bets to see a turn; fold or strongly re-evaluate to a large raise/overbet since I’m behind to value hands and vulnerable to sets/8x.

On a paired board of 8h-4d-8d, the pre-flop aggressor (Minni) has checked to me. My hand, Q7o, has some marginal showdown value in heads-up play but is not strong enough to bet for value. By checking back, I control the size of the pot and take a free card to potentially improve on the turn. If Minni is trapping with an 8 or a high pocket pair, checking prevents me from losing more chips. If she has missed with overcards like AJ or KT, my Queen-high might still be ahead or can improve to the best hand later.

Recap: I 3-bet preflop with 7♣7♠ and both checked the flop (8♥ 4♦ 8♦). Turn brings 8♠ — the board now shows trips (8♥ 8♦ 8♠) so my underpair has essentially zero fold equity and is behind any hand containing an 8 or a 4 (and behind many pairs that will play confidently). Betting OOP here only builds a pot I’m very likely to lose and will get called by hands that beat me while folding only the very weakest holdings. I prefer pot control: check and let the opponent either bluff or show weakness. If Flash leads with a large bet or shove I’ll fold; if he fires a very small bet I may call sometimes as a bluff-catcher, and if he checks back I’ll re-evaluate the river action.

On a board of 8h-4d-8d-8s, the hand is dominated by the three eights on the board. My best five-card hand is 8-8-8-Q-7. Since Minni 3-bet pre-flop (even though it was a min-raise), her range is likely weighted toward high cards and pocket pairs. If she has any pocket pair (Full House) or an Ace or King (better kicker for the Three of a Kind), I am currently losing. However, she has checked both the flop and the turn. By checking back here, I maintain control of the pot and get a free look at the river. Betting would likely only get called by hands that beat me (A-high, K-high, or pairs) and fold out hands I already beat. I will check to realize my showdown value or re-evaluate on the river.

The board is 8h 4d 8d 8s 4s — the five community cards already make a full house (eights full of fours). My 7♣7♠ cannot beat the board; only quads (an 8 in hand) or pocket 4s can beat me. Flash called my small 3‑bet pre and then checked the flop and turn — a passive line that can be both weak and a slowplay of the nuts. Betting here would be a pure bluff with very limited fold equity and a real chance of building a pot I’m certain to lose if he has the nuts. I’ll check to take the showdown (split the pot unless he has quads/44). If Flash now leads with a large value bet or shove I’ll fold — only very tiny river seizes would ever be considered for a call, but we’re not facing a bet now, so check is the correct, lowest-variance play.

The board has completed a full house: Eights full of Fours (8-8-8-4-4). Since a full house uses all five cards, my Queen and Seven do not improve the hand. Any player without a pocket pair higher than 44 or the case eight (8c) is playing the board. Minni's 3-bet pre-flop followed by three checks suggests she might be playing the board or holding a marginal hand. Betting here is unlikely to induce a fold from a better hand (like a higher pocket pair) and will likely be called by any hand that is currently splitting with me. I will check to reveal the showdown and take my share of the pot.

I have Q♠3♣ on the button (I posted SB=50) and only need 50 more to see the flop — total committed would be 100. Heads-up I should play wide, but Q3o is a marginal holding and I prefer to keep the pot small with position rather than bloat it preflop. Calling is cheap (50 into a 150 pot; pot odds ≈4:1) to realize my equity and use positional advantage postflop. A min-raise to 200 would enlarge the pot and invite 3-bets from Flash (who shows aggression), unnecessarily risking chips with a dominated/marginal hand. I’ll call and evaluate on the flop.

I am in the Big Blind with a very weak hand (3s 5h). The Small Blind (Minni) has completed the blind, and I have the option to check or raise. Given the poor strength of my hand and the fact that I am out of position, checking to see a free flop is the most prudent strategy to conserve my stack and minimize risk.

The flop is As Ad 6s, creating a paired board. I hold 5-high (3s 5h) with no real potential for improvement. Since Minni checked from the Small Blind, I will check behind to take a free turn card. Bluffing into this board texture is risky as the opponent may be slow-playing an Ace or simply holding a better high card that they aren't ready to fold yet.

Flop A♠ A♦ 6♠; I hold Q♠3♣ — no pair, only a weak backdoor spade draw. Flash checked, and heads‑up this board crushes my equity: any Ace or most pocket pairs/strong broadways are ahead. Betting has limited fold equity and risks a check‑raise from an opponent who shows a lot of aggression; I don’t want to bloat the pot with essentially air. With position I should control the pot, realize equity, and fold to real strength. Check and reassess on the turn.

The board has paired Aces and now an Eight. I have absolutely no hand value with 5-high. Minni checked behind on the flop, which could indicate a weak hand or a trap, but since I have no equity and very little chance of successfully bluffing a range that might include small pairs or a 6, I will check and hope to see a free river. There is no reason to commit chips to this pot with my current holding.

I have Q♠3♣ on A♠ A♦ 6♠ 8♦ — no pair and no real draw after the turn. Flash checked both the flop and the turn; given his earlier aggression this line can represent either a slow‑played Ace/pair or a pot‑control with marginal hands, but either way I’m almost always behind. Pot is small (200) and betting here has very little fold equity versus hands that beat me and risks a check‑raise or shove from a relatively short, aggressive opponent (he has ~7.2k). With position and a big stack advantage I should control pot size and see the river rather than bloating the pot with air. I’ll check the turn to realize equity and plan to fold to substantial aggression on the river; if the river is a safe card I can consider a delayed bluff or a thin value bet when appropriate.

On the river, the board is As Ad 6s 8d 5c, and I have paired my five. While two pair (Aces and Fives) is a weak hand on this board, the action has been checked all the way through, suggesting that neither player has a particularly strong hand. There is no value in betting here, as the opponent is unlikely to call with a worse hand but might call with a 6, an 8, or a small pocket pair. Checking ensures I reach showdown for free with my marginal hand, which might actually be the best hand given the passive action.

Board: 6♠ A♠ A♦ 8♦ 5♣ — I hold Q♠3♣ (queen-high, no pair). Pot is only 200 and Flash checked flop, turn and river. Heads‑up this board is dominated by any Ace or most pocket pairs; a river bluff has very little fold equity vs hands that beat me and risks a check‑raise or shove from a relatively short, aggressive opponent. With position, a small pot and effectively no value, the lowest‑risk, highest‑EV line is to check and avoid bloating the pot with air. I’ll check to showdown.

I am holding pocket fives on the button. This is a strong starting hand in heads-up play. I will put in a standard raise to 300 to take the initiative and build the pot, which is consistent with my previous opening sizes throughout the tournament.

Heads-up vs a standard button open to 300. I have A♥4♣ — an ace with a weak kicker but a good blocker against strong Ax hands. Calling 200 to see a flop leaves me OOP against a wide range; I prefer to exert pressure and isolate with a 3-bet. I’ll raise to 900 (about 3x the open) to gain fold equity vs weaker holdings, charge speculative hands, and define the hand. If called I’ll play cautiously OOP (c-bet on dry boards, check/float on coordinated textures); if he 4-bets large I can fold A4o without losing much EV given stack depths.

I have pocket fives on the button. I opened for 300 and Minni 3-bet to 900. Minni has a significant chip lead, and while pocket fives are a decent hand heads-up, a 3-bet from the Big Blind often represents a strong range or a polarized bluff. However, in a heads-up match, folding pocket pairs to a single 3-bet is generally too tight. I have a stack of 7100 (71 big blinds), giving me enough depth to call and play post-flop, looking to set-mine or navigate safe boards. Calling allows me to keep the pot manageable while retaining position for the rest of the hand.

I'm out of position after 3-betting preflop with A♥4♣ on a 7♥9♥2♠ flop. I have no pair but I do block a lot of Ax / nut-heart combos with the Ah. This is a fairly dry, two-heart board that misses a big portion of the caller's range (broadways, weak pairs, non-heart holdings), so a continuation bet should pick up a lot of pots and define villain's holdings. Villain is relatively short (≈6.5k) vs my deep stack, so I want meaningful fold equity without overcommitting OOP. A half-pot c-bet (~900 into 1800) balances folding out missed hands and protecting against giving free cards to draws. If he folds I take it down; if he calls I proceed cautiously on the turn (evaluate for showdown/value or check back to control the pot). I will fold to a large shove/check-raise given I only have Ace-high.

I have pocket fives on a flop of 7h-9h-2s. While these are underpairs to the two highest cards on the board, in a heads-up 3-bet pot, my hand still holds significant value against a wide continuation-betting range that includes many ace-high and king-high hands. Minni's bet of 900 into a 1800 pot provides me with 3:1 pot odds, requiring roughly 25% equity to continue. Folding now would be too exploitable. I will call to see how the turn develops, as I have position and can potentially take the pot down if Minni checks later streets or if I hit one of my two remaining outs.

Turn brings the 8♥ giving me the nut flush (A♥4♣ on 7♥9♥2♠8♥). Villain called my flop c-bet, so his range contains a lot of hearts, single pairs (9x, 7x) and some broadways — many of which will call a large value bet. Villain is short (~5.6k) relative to the pot (3.6k), so I should extract maximum value and close action before the river can pair the board and give a full house.
