Betting the effective stack (5,600) converts this into an all-in or a big call from worse flushes, pairs and two-pair combos — maximizing expected value. I will bet the full 5,600 to get called by most worse hands.

On this turn card (8h), the board has become extremely coordinated, completing several straight draws (T8, J8, 65, 54) and three-to-a-flush possibilities. Minni's shove for my entire remaining stack (5600 into a 3600 pot) is an incredibly polarising and aggressive move. While heads-up play often involves wide ranges, my pocket fives are now essentially a bluff-catcher against a range that includes many overpairs, sets, two-pairs, and completed straights or flushes. My hand does not have the heart for a flush draw and I have very little equity to improve. Given that Minni is risking my tournament life on a very dangerous board, calling with a small underpair is too high-risk for the current tournament status.

Heads-up on the button with 3♦5♣ — a weak hand, but in heads-up play the button should open very wide to use positional advantage and apply pressure to the BB. Current pot is 150 and it costs me 50 to just complete; min-raise is 200. Calling 50 is overly passive and allows the BB to see cheap flops; min-raising to 200 is too small and just invites a loose call. I’ll open to a standard 3x sizing (raise to 300 total) to maximize fold equity against a 55bb opponent and keep initiative postflop. Plan: if Flash folds I pick up the blinds; if called I’ll C-bet frequently on favorable flops and give up vs big resistance with this garbage holding. If Flash 3-bets substantially, I’ll fold and preserve my big stack for better spots.

In a heads-up situation, Ace-Eight offsuit (Ad 8c) is a strong hand to defend from the Big Blind against a standard button raise. Minni has been quite aggressive, and while a 3-bet is an option, calling allows me to see a flop and control the pot size given my stack is significantly smaller than Minni's. Folding is out of the question for a hand of this strength in this position.

I have flopped trip Aces with an Eight kicker. On a board of AA3, I have a very strong hand. Since Minni was the pre-flop aggressor, the standard play is to check to the raiser to allow them to make a continuation bet. This can induce a bluff or value bet from a wide range of hands that I currently dominate. Leading out here might cause Minni to fold weaker hands that she would otherwise bet with.

I raised preflop and the flop (Ah Ac 3c) gives me two pair (Aces and Threes) with 3♦5♣. Heads-up that’s a very strong made hand against the BB’s wide calling range — most hands that call my preflop raise (Kx, Qx, medium pocket pairs, singletons) are now worse and will pay me off. The main hands that beat me are any Ace (trip Aces) or very occasionally a 3 with a better kicker. Flash checked the flop, so I should extract thin value and deny free cards while keeping the pot controllable if I face resistance. I’ll make a half‑pot value bet (300 into the 600 pot) — large enough to charge worse hands and some club/backdoor holdings, but small enough to fold to a sizable check‑raise that would usually polarize to trip Aces. If called I’ll re-evaluate on the turn; if I face a large raise or shove I’ll assign a high frequency of Aces and fold to preserve my stack.

I have flopped trip Aces with an Eight kicker on a board of Ah-Ac-3c. This is a monster hand in a heads-up pot. Minni has been playing aggressively, and since I checked to her as the pre-flop caller, she followed through with a continuation bet. Calling is the best play here to trap her bluffs and keep her wide range in the pot. Raising now would likely force her to fold air or marginal hands that she might continue to bet on the turn or river. I have the Eight of clubs, which is a useful blocker and provides a small redraw if a club flush materializes. By calling, I under-represent my hand and give her the opportunity to continue her aggression.

I have trip Aces with an Eight kicker on a board of Ah-Ac-3c-Jd. This remains a very strong hand in a heads-up pot. The Jack of Diamonds on the turn is a card that could potentially improve Minni if she has AJ or JJ, but it is more likely that I still have the best hand. Following my strategy from the flop, I will check again to Minni. She has been the aggressor throughout this hand and many previous ones. By checking, I am continuing to under-represent the strength of my hand, giving her the opportunity to continue betting with her bluffs or marginal made hands like pocket pairs or a Jack.

I have two pair (A♣A♥ with my 3♦ giving Aces and Threes). Flash called my preflop raise and my 300 flop c‑bet, so his range is wide (Kx/Qx/Jx, medium pairs, A‑x, occasional 3x). The turn J♦ is largely harmless and doesn’t change the fact that most of his calling range is behind my two‑pair; the hands that beat me are trips/quads (Ax, 33, AA) and are relatively rare. I want to extract value from worse hands and deny a free river card, but I don’t want to polarize the pot and encourage big check‑raises from trips/quads. Bet half‑pot to keep weaker hands and pairs in and keep the pot controllable if I face aggression. If Flash check‑raises big or jams I’ll assign a very narrow value range (mostly Aces/sets) and fold.

I have trip Aces with an 8 kicker on a board of Ah-Ac-3c-Jd. This is a very strong hand in a heads-up pot. Minni has been aggressive throughout the hand, and her 600 bet into 1200 is a standard continuation. While the Jack of Diamonds could potentially have improved her if she is holding AJ or JJ, I still likely have the best hand against her wide range. Calling is the most effective play here to continue under-representing my hand. By just calling, I keep her bluffs in the pot and allow her to potentially fire a third barrel on the river with air or a Jack. Raising now would likely only get action from hands that have me beaten and would fold out the weaker hands I want her to keep betting with.

I have trip Aces with an Eight kicker on a board that is now complete. I have been playing this hand by check-calling to under-represent my strength and induce bluffs from Minni, who has been aggressive on every street. The Six of Hearts on the river doesn't change the board texture significantly. By checking again, I give Minni one last opportunity to bluff or bet with a marginal hand like a Jack or a medium pocket pair. If I were to bet now, I would likely only be called by hands that have me beat (better Aces or Full Houses) and force her to fold the bluffs that I have been carefully keeping in the pot. My goal is to maximize value by letting her commit more chips if she is indeed bluffing.

I have two pair (Aces and Threes) on A♥ A♣ 3♣ J♦ 6♥ with 3♦5♣. Villain cold‑called my preflop raise, then check‑called my flop and turn bets — a fairly wide calling line that includes many worse hands (Kx/Qx/Jx, small pairs, some missed draws) but also the thin but real combos that beat me (any remaining A‑card for trips, and the lone combo of pocket 3s that makes a full house). The river 6♥ doesn’t change much. I want to extract value from the many worse hands while keeping the pot controllable vs the rare stronger holdings. A half‑pot river bet continues the same sizing pattern, charges worse hands enough to call, and avoids polarizing the pot (which would invite a check‑raise I don’t want to get into). I’ll bet 1,200 into the 2,400 pot. If he check‑raises big or jams, I’ll assign a narrow value range (mostly Aces/sets) and fold to preserve my large tournament stack.

I have trip Aces with an Eight kicker on a board of Ah-Ac-3c-Jd-6h. I have check-called on every street to allow Minni to continue her aggression with her wide range, which includes many bluffs or marginal hands like a Jack or middle pocket pairs. The Six of Hearts on the river is a blank. Minni has bet 1200 into a 2400 pot. While my hand is very strong, the Eight kicker is relatively weak if Minni also has an Ace (she could easily have AJ, AQ, AK, AT, or A9). If I raise, I likely only get called by better Aces or full houses and fold out the bluffs I've been inducing. By calling, I win against all her bluffs and hands like KJ/QJ, while minimizing losses against superior kickers.

I am on the button with a pocket pair of nines (9d 9s), which is a very strong hand in heads-up play. My goal is to build a pot and maintain aggression. I will raise to 300 to put pressure on the big blind and define the strength of my hand. If Minni calls, I'll evaluate the flop, but I'm looking to commit chips with this strength level.

Heads-up vs a SB open to 300. I’m in the BB with Ah4s (off-suit) and I’m out of position postflop, so I want to defend a wide range but keep the pot controllable. The pot is ~400 and it’s 200 more to call — calling 200 into a 600 pot after my call requires ~33% equity to break even. A4 has good showdown value and an Ace-blocker vs a typical wide SB opening range, so it comfortably clears the break-even threshold. A min-3bet is an option, but it creates OOP postflop and can lead to unnecessary commit against a shorter stack; a large 3bet/all-in is overly confrontational here. Plan: call to see a flop, play straightforwardly when I hit an Ace or strong draw, and fold to heavy, disciplined pressure when I miss. Therefore I’ll defend by calling.

I have top pair (A) with a weak kicker (4) on As Jc 8c. Villain opened wide from the SB heads‑up so his continuing range contains lots of worse Ax, Jx, small pairs and many club draws. With two clubs on board I want to charge/deny equity to those draws and extract value from worse aces and jacks while keeping the pot manageable OOP. Pot is ~600 — a half‑pot bet (~300) achieves that: gets called by draws and worse pairs but doesn’t commit me unnecessarily if I face big aggression. Plan: bet 300 to protect and get value; re‑evaluate on a raise (call reasonable sized raises to see a turn, fold to extreme/clear strength if necessary).

I started the hand with pocket nines, a strong pre-flop holding. On the flop of As-Jc-8c, there are two overcards to my pair. Minni has decided to lead out (donk bet) for 300 into the 600 pot. This lead can represent an Ace, a Jack, a draw (like a flush draw or a straight draw), or a total bluff. Folding immediately to one bet with a mid-pair is too weak, especially with position. Raising is also risky as it likely only gets called by hands that have me beat. Calling allows me to keep the pot manageable, see a turn card, and re-evaluate based on Minni's next action and the turn card. I am getting 3-to-1 odds on the call, which is sufficient to continue one street.

Turn (Tc of clubs) makes the board As Jc 8c Tc — three clubs and additional straight/two‑pair possibilities. I hold top pair (A) but with a very weak 4 kicker and no club; my hand is vulnerable to two‑pair (AT, AJ, JT), Q9 straights, and the rare but crushing two‑club flushes. Villain called my flop bet from a very wide SB opening range, so his continuing range contains many hands that now beat or heavily contest my A4o. With pot ~1,200 and the villain only 7,400 behind, I don’t want to inflate the pot OOP with a marginal hand that is often behind; betting invites a check‑raise or shove and charges only the hands I already beat. I prefer pot control: check the turn, see how villain acts, and re‑evaluate on the river. If he checks back I can consider a small value bet on non‑scary rivers; if he bets a small/medium amount I’ll call some thin value/river bluffs but will fold to large polarized overbets or shoves that represent made two‑pair/flush/straight combinations.

The board has become extremely coordinated with the Ten of Clubs, completing possible flushes and various straights. My pocket Nines have significantly dropped in relative strength on this As-Jc-8c-Tc texture. After Minni led the flop and then checked the turn, it's possible they are pot-controlling a marginal hand like a Jack or a weak Ace, or they might be trapping. I have some showdown value but not enough to bet for value, and betting as a bluff is risky given the board texture. Checking behind allows me to control the size of the pot and see a river card for free, keeping the pot manageable for my mid-strength hand.

Board is As Jc 8c Tc Kc and I hold Ah4s — top pair but a very weak kicker. Villain opened SB and called my small flop bet, then both of us checked the turn; that line keeps lots of Ax, Jx, pocket pairs and club draws in his continuing range. The river Kc is a game-changer: with four clubs on board any single club in his hand now makes a flush, and the river also completes straights/two-pair combos (Q9, AT, AJ) that beat my ace. Betting here will mostly get called by hands that beat me (flushes, better Aces, two‑pair/straights) and can induce a check‑raise or shove that I should fold to given my weak kicker. I avoid bloating the pot OOP with a marginal hand — check to take it to showdown. If he bets a small, non‑polarized amount I can call thin; I will fold to large polarized shoves that clearly represent made flush/straights/two‑pair.

The board has become incredibly coordinated with four clubs (Jc, 8c, Tc, Kc) and an Ace. My pocket nines do not contain a club, meaning I lose to any single club in my opponent's hand. Additionally, any Queen makes a straight. Since Minni has checked, I have a choice between checking behind for a showdown or turning my hand into a bluff. However, betting into a board this 'wet' is extremely risky because Minni is unlikely to fold even a low club or a straight. I have some very marginal showdown value if she was bluffing the flop and has no club or Queen. Checking is the most prudent play to see the showdown without risking more chips in a pot where my hand strength has been completely negated by the board runout.

8♣3♠ is a very weak, easily dominated hand. In heads-up on the button I can see a flop very cheaply: the pot is 150 and calling 50 (completing to 100 total) gives good odds to try to hit while keeping risk low. I don't want to build a big preflop pot with junk — I will fold to a sizable 3-bet, and postflop only continue with a clear pair/strong draw or a good opportunity to apply positional pressure. So I complete to see the flop cheaply and keep options open.

I am in the Big Blind with Jc7s. Minni, who has a significant chip lead, has just completed the small blind. J7o is a mediocre hand in heads-up play, but there is no reason to raise and bloat the pot out of position against a large stack. Checking allows me to see a flop for free and evaluate from there.

I am in the Big Blind with an open-ended straight draw (J-7 on a 6-9-8 board). In a limped pot, I want to keep the pot small while I'm on a draw, especially against a chip leader. I will check to see how Minni responds; I have plenty of equity to call a standard bet or take a free card if she checks back.

I have 8♣3♠ on 6♠9♠8♦ — middle pair (pair of eights) with a spade in hand (small backdoor). Villain checked to me, so I can take the initiative and extract value from worse hands (small pairs, 6x, worse eights) while charging spade/overcard draws. I don’t want to bloated the pot with a vulnerable hand, so I’ll make a modest bet (~half‑pot) to get value and deny free cards; if I face a sizable check‑raise I can fold and preserve my stack. Betting 100 keeps pressure on his wide checking range while leaving room to fold to heavy aggression or continue cautiously if called.

I have an open-ended straight draw on a very connected board (6-9-8 with J-7 in hand). Minni has bet 100 into a 200-chip pot. I am getting 3:1 odds on a call, which is sufficient given my equity (roughly 32% to hit the straight by the river). Calling is the best play here to see the turn cheaply and realize my equity without bloating the pot unnecessarily against the chip leader.

On the turn, the board has completed a 5-6-7-8-9 straight for me with my 7s. While a 10 in Minni's hand would provide her with a higher 6-7-8-9-10 straight, a 9-high straight is exceptionally strong in heads-up play. However, the board is also very 'wet' with both spade and diamond flush draws available. Since Minni was the aggressor on the flop, I will check to her to see if she continues her story. This allows me to keep the pot manageable if she has a 10, or potentially check-raise for value if she bets a pair or a draw.

I have second pair (8♣) with a very weak 3 kicker on a board 6♠-9♠-8♦-5♦. The turn (5♦) actually creates more hands that beat me (any 9, any 6, any 7 makes the 5-6-7-8-9 straight, and a 5 gives trips), and my backdoor spade possibility is dead now (turn is not a spade). I have only two clean outs (the remaining eights) so this hand is vulnerable and not worth bloating the pot. Villain checked the turn, which could be either weakness or a trap; given my kicker and the board texture I prefer pot control. I’ll check to avoid getting raised off my hand, keep the pot manageable, and either call a small river bet or fold to heavy pressure. If villain checks through we go to showdown cheaply.

On the river, the board is 6s-9s-8d-5d-3d. I hold Jc-7s, giving me a 9-high straight (5-6-7-8-9). While this is a very strong hand, the board is highly coordinated. A player holding a 10 has a higher 10-high straight, and since there are three diamonds on the board, a flush is also possible. Minni checked back on the turn, which could indicate a lack of a monster hand or a desire to control the pot. By checking now, I can see how she reacts. If she checks back, we likely chop or I win; if she bets, I have a tough decision but can avoid losing more chips if she has the 10 or the flush.

On the river (6♠ 9♠ 8♦ 5♦ 3♦) my hand improved — I hold 8♣3♠ so I now have two pair (eights and threes). I fired the flop and was called, checked the turn when villain checked, and was checked to on the river. Heads‑up that line indicates a wide calling range that includes many single pairs (9x, 6x, 5x), missed spade draws and overcards — many of those hands I now beat. There are hands that still beat me (sets: 99/66, pocket 88, or 55 making trips on the turn), but they are less frequent than the weaker pairs that will call a value bet. With a healthy stack advantage and the opponent still having chips to call, I want to extract thin value without bloating the pot or folding out marginal callers. I size a modest value bet (~1/3–1/2 pot) to get called by worse hands while keeping the price reasonable; if I face heavy aggression I can re‑evaluate (sets are a concern). Bet 150 to maximize thin value.

I hold a 9-high straight on a board of 6s-9s-8d-5d-3d. While this is a very strong hand in heads-up play, the board is highly coordinated. Any player holding a 10 has a higher 10-high straight, and the third diamond on the river makes a flush possible. Minni checked the turn and is now leading for a relatively small amount (150 into a pot of 400). This could be a value bet with a marginal hand, a blocker bet, or an attempt to induce a call with a superior hand like a 10. Given the board texture and the stack sizes, raising is risky because I'm unlikely to be called by anything I beat, and I could easily be facing a better straight or a flush. Folding a straight for this price is out of the question. Calling allows me to realize my showdown value while keeping the pot manageable.
J7 suited is a solid hand to play heads-up from the button. I will open for my standard 3x raise of 300 to maintain pressure and build the pot with a hand that has good post-flop potential.

Heads-up vs a 300 open from the button. I’m in the big blind with 8♥5♦ — a weak but defendable hand heads-up. Calling 200 into a 400 pot gives me ~3:1 pot odds (need ~25% equity). The button’s opening range is fairly wide here, so this hand has enough equity to see a flop. I don’t want to 3-bet with a poorly playable offsuit hand out of position; raising would put unnecessary chips at risk and make postflop play harder. Plan: call to see the flop, proceed cautiously — fold to heavy aggression if I miss, continue only with pairs/draws or favorable board texture. (Amount = 300 total committed: my 100 BB + 200 call.)

Flop: Ad 9d Qd. I hold 8♥5♦ — no pair, but I have a 4-card diamond flush draw (one diamond in my hand + three on board), roughly ~35% to hit by the river. Out of position vs a wide button opening range, I prefer to check and control the pot rather than lead out with a vulnerable hand. Plan: check to see a c-bet and call small-to-medium bets to realize equity; if Flash jams or posts a very large overbet representing a made flush/top pair I will fold unless the price is mathematically compelling. If he checks back I can take free cards or barre on a safe turn if I pick up extra equity.

The flop is extremely coordinated with three diamonds (Ad-9d-Qd). I do not hold any diamonds, nor do I have a pair or a straight draw. Since Minni checked, I have the option to check behind and take a free turn. Betting here into a monotone board without any equity is risky, as any diamond in Minni's hand will likely call or raise. Checking allows me to see the turn and potentially evaluate if I can represent a hand later or simply concede the pot if the board remains unfavorable.

Board: Ad 9d Qd 4c. I hold 8♥5♦ — I have one diamond for a 4-card diamond draw and a few pair outs. On the turn I have 9 diamonds to make a flush on the river (9/46 ≈ 19.6%) and 5 additional non-diamond pair outs (two non-diamond eights + three non-diamond fives) for 14 unique outs total (~14/46 ≈ 30.4% raw). Flash checked both flop and turn, which often indicates a fairly weak or medium-strength holding in this spot rather than a huge made hand. Betting here is a profitable semi-bluff: a half-pot bet will fold out a lot of overcards and small pairs, and if called I retain decent equity to improve on the river. I’ll bet a controlled half-pot to apply pressure while keeping the price reasonable if called. If Flash check-raises big (shows strong strength), I will fold this turn semi-bluff.

I have no piece of this board, holding no diamonds, no pairs, and no draws. Minni led for 300 into the 600 pot on the turn after checking the flop. While this could be a probe bet with a weak hand, I have zero equity to continue or bluff effectively given my lack of blockers. I will fold and wait for a better spot to use my remaining stack.

Heads-up on the button (I’m the small blind) with T9o — this is a very playable hand in position. I want to take the initiative and avoid playing a marginal pot out of position. Pot is 150 and a raise to 200 (total) is a standard 2xBB open that buys me fold equity while keeping the pot manageable vs Flash’s ~73bb stack. If he calls I’ll use position to c-bet favorable flops and apply pressure; if he 3-bets large I can fold T9o and wait for a better spot. Therefore I’ll raise to 200 to open the action and play the hand in position.

I am holding Ten of Clubs and Six of Hearts in the Big Blind. Minni has raised from the Small Blind to 200. With 100 already in the pot as my big blind, I only need to call 100 more to see a flop, giving me 3:1 pot odds. While T6o is a weak hand, in a heads-up situation, it is worth defending for this price against a button raise to see if I can connect with the board.

On a board of three-of-a-kind (3d 3s 3c), the hand often comes down to who has the better high card or a pocket pair. Since I am first to act with Ten-high, there is little merit in betting into the pre-flop raiser on such a static texture. I will check to see how Minni acts, as my hand has some showdown value if the board stays low, but cannot withstand much pressure.

The flop is 3d 3s 3c — trips on the board. I hold T9o, so I do NOT have a pocket pair or a 3: right now my best five is the board trips + T9 kickers. Many hands in Flash’s calling range (any Ace or King, most pocket pairs) either already beat me (pocket pairs = immediate full house) or have better two-card kickers. There is virtually no meaningful equity to gain by barreling here: a bet will mainly get called by better kickers or pocket pairs and will fold only the very weakest junk combos. With a dry, static board and very limited fold equity, I want to control the pot and gather info. I’ll check to keep the pot small and see how Flash acts on the turn — I can fold to significant aggression (he likely has a full house/quads or superior kickers) or call very small, non-committal bets to probe for bluffs. If the turn pairs one of my hole cards (T or 9) I can reassess and start extracting value.

On a board of 3-3-3-8, the best five-card hand uses the three 3s and the two highest available cards between the board and the player's hole cards. Currently, my best hand is 3-3-3-8-T. If Minni has any card higher than a Ten (J, Q, K, A) or any pocket pair, she is ahead. Since the flop went check-check, it is unlikely either of us has a 3. I will check the turn to keep the pot small and reach a cheap showdown, as my Ten-high has some showdown value but cannot reliably call a significant bet.

Board is 3d 3s 3c 8c; with Td9c I'm effectively playing the board (3-3-3-T-9). Almost everything in Flash's preflop calling range beats me: any pocket pair is already a full house and any A/K/Q/J (or other high card) gives a higher kicker than my Ten. Bet/raise here would have very little fold equity and would mostly be value-bet against better hands or thinly-called by worse hands — not +EV. I keep the pot small and gather info: check the turn, fold to large, committed aggression, and only call small probes to see the river. If Flash checks back I will re-evaluate the river (look to extract value or bluff only if the runout favors me).

The board has completed a full house (8-8-3-3-3). Since my hole cards (Ten of Clubs and Six of Hearts) do not include an Eight or the final Three, my best possible hand is the board itself. In this situation, unless Minni holds an Eight or the Three of Hearts, we will both be playing the board and split the pot. Since the action went check-check on the flop and turn, it is highly likely that neither of us has improved. I will check to reach a showdown and take the split pot, as betting provides no value here.

The river is 3♦ 3♠ 3♣ 8♣ 8♥ — the board itself is a full house (threes full of eights). My hole cards T♦9♣ do not improve me and I have no blocker to 3s or 8s. Against Flash’s preflop calling range many hands will simply tie with the board; the only hands that beat me are any 3 or any 8 (or quads/full houses involving them). Betting here would be a pure bluff with very little fold equity — most reasonable holdings will either call or already beat me, and I have no value to extract. Best line is to check and take the showdown (likely a chop unless Flash holds an 8 or 3). Preserve chips and avoid creating a costly bluff/unnecessary risk.

I am on the button with 3d 5c. This is a weak hand, but it's a heads-up situation and I only need to call 50 more chips to see a flop. However, my opponent, Minni, has a massive stack advantage (over 4:1) and has been playing aggressively. Playing out of position or even in position with such a weak hand against a large stack doesn't offer a good risk-reward ratio right now. I'll fold and wait for a better spot to try and rebuild my stack.

Heads-up on the button with 9♥10♥ — a very playable, suited connector that plays great in position against a wide BB defending range. Pot is 150 and calling 50 is passive; I want initiative and fold equity while building a pot I can play postflop in position. A 3x BB open to 300 is standard here: it avoids giving the BB good odds to see a flop for free, applies pressure to weaker hands, and still keeps stacks manageable with Flash (7,300). I’ll raise to 300 total (that includes my SB50) and proceed to play postflop in position. If Flash 3-bets big or shoves, I’ll re-evaluate given pot odds and ranges.

I am currently significantly outstacked by Minni (7,300 vs 32,300) with only about 38 hands remaining in the tournament. Minni has raised to 300 from the button, and while I am getting 2:1 pot odds to call, 5s3s is a very weak hand to play out of position against a chip leader who has been applying constant pressure. Defending such a marginal hand risks losing a larger portion of my stack on later streets when I am likely to be behind or forced to fold to continuation bets. I will wait for a better opportunity to commit chips.

I am on the button with J9o. This is a standard opening hand in heads-up play, but I need to be cautious as I am the shorter stack. However, J9o is well within a typical button opening range. I will raise to 300 to exert pressure and take initiative.

I have Q♠7♣ in the big blind facing a 300 open from the button. Heads-up this is a defendable holding — decent equity versus a wide opening range and plays well postflop. Calling the extra 200 (300 total committed) gives me ~3:1 pot odds (need ≈25% equity), so calling is the correct, low-variance choice here. A light 3-bet out of position with Q7o is thin and hands me the initiative but leaves me OOP; I prefer to call, see a flop, and exploit textures and Flash's aggression on later streets.

Check. I have top pair (Q♠) with a weak 7 kicker on K♦ Q♦ 8♠ — a decent but vulnerable holding heads-up. Flash opened wide to 300 preflop, so his range contains Kx, Ax, broadways and lots of diamond combos; I’m ahead of many bluffs and worse Qx hands but behind Ks and some dominating Qx hands and threatened by two‑diamond hands (turn/river flush completes). Pot is small (600) relative to effective stacks, and I’m out of position, so pot control and extracting value on later streets is preferable to barreling now. Plan: check and call small-to-medium C-bets to realize equity and re-evaluate on the turn; avoid bloating the pot or exposing myself to a check-raise bluff/over‑bet when my kicker is weak.

On a board of K-Q-8 with two diamonds, my J9o has a gutshot straight draw (needing a Ten). However, this board hits the Big Blind's calling range quite well, as it contains many high cards and broadway connectors. Minni checked to me. I have some backdoor potential, but I lack a made hand or a strong draw. Betting here might get a fold from some very weak hands, but given the stack sizes and the board texture, I prefer to check back and take a free card to try and hit my straight or a pair on the turn without bloating the pot further while out of position against a massive chip leader.

I have top pair Q♠7♣ but a very weak 7 kicker on Q♦ K♦ 8♠ 5♠. Flash checked the flop and hasn't bet the turn — his range is wide (Kx, some Qx, diamond combos, overcards and bluffs). There are two diamonds on board so a river diamond will complete many draws, and betting out of position here to “protect” is thin value and simply bloats the pot against hands that currently beat me (Kx or better). Pot is small (600) relative to effective stacks, so I want pot control and a clean river decision. I’ll check the turn, take a free card, and re-evaluate the river — calling small/medium bets for value or folding to large overbets; if a blank river falls I may lead for a small thin value.

On the turn, the 5s doesn't improve my hand, but it does add another flush draw to the board. Minni has checked for the second time. While this could signal weakness, the K-Q-8 board is quite heavy and likely hit some of Minni's pre-flop calling range. I still have a gutshot straight draw (needing a Ten). Since I have very little showdown value with Jack-high but still have a chance to hit the nuts on the river, I will check back to realize my equity for free rather than turning my hand into a bluff on a board that is difficult to represent. If a Ten hits the river, I will be in a strong position to value bet.

Check. I have top pair (Q) with a very weak 7-kicker on Q♦ K♦ 8♠ 5♠ 2♣ and Flash has been completely passive postflop (raised pre, checked flop/turn/river). The pot is small (600) relative to both stacks, so a river bet is largely thin value — there are few worse hands that will call (some Qx with worse kickers or rare 8x), while many hands that beat me (Kx, two-pair/pocket pairs) will either call or are unlikely to fold to a small bet. I’ll check to preserve chips and take it to showdown; plan is to call small river bets for pot odds but fold to large river overbets since my kicker is weak and Villain’s passive line increases the chance he has a better pair or a slowplayed hand.

I have Jack-high on a board of K-Q-8-5-2. After raising pre-flop, the action checked through on the flop and turn. Minni has checked for a third time on the river. While I have very little showdown value, I do beat some of Minni's potential missed draws like T9 or 76. However, she likely has at least Ace-high or a small pair given her pre-flop calling range. Since the pot is small and I've checked twice already, I don't have much fold equity against a marginal hand, and betting would only likely get called by hands that beat me. I will check and hope my Jack-high is somehow good, or simply concede the small pot to preserve my stack.

Heads-up on the button (small blind) with T♣4♠ — a marginal hand but perfectly playable in heads-up because position postflop is a big advantage. Calling 50 is cheap, but open‑raising is standard here: it wins the blinds outright a lot, gives us initiative, and lets us play postflop in position with a wide range. Flash (BB) has shown willingness to defend and be active, but a 2x raise is small relative to both stacks and will either take the pot or let us play a controlled pot in position. Plan: raise to 200 total; if he calls, continue with standard c-betting on favorable textures and fold to a large 3‑bet (T4o has poor equity vs a polarized 3‑bet).

In a heads-up situation, Queen-Nine offsuit (Q9o) is a strong hand to defend the Big Blind with against a button min-raise. I am getting 3-to-1 pot odds (calling 100 into a pot of 300) to see a flop. Q9o has plenty of equity against a wide button opening range. A call allows me to keep the pot manageable and see how the board develops without committing too much of my stack prematurely.

I have middle pair (Nines) and a gutshot straight draw (to a Ten) on a very connected board. In this heads-up situation, checking to the pre-flop aggressor is the standard and most flexible play. It allows me to see how Minni reacts to this board before committing more chips. If Minni bets, I have a clear continue with my pair and draw, but leading out myself (donk betting) doesn't achieve much against a wide button range that could easily have a Jack or better draws.

Heads‑up on J♣9♠8♥ with T♣4♠. I have an open‑ended straight draw (need a Q or 7) — 8 outs, roughly 31–32% to hit by the river. Opponent in the BB checked the flop, which represents a wide, often weak/calling range in heads‑up. This is a classic semi‑bluff spot: betting wins the pot now against many hands (Kx, Ax, missed broadways, small pairs) and builds a pot where I have strong rollup equity if called. I’ll keep the size reasonable to preserve fold equity while avoiding overcommitting into a range that often has a pair or better — half pot is appropriate. If he raises big I’ll re‑evaluate and fold to heavy pressure since I only have a draw and no pair; if called I’ll proceed to extract value when I hit or use pot control if I miss. Bet 200 (≈1/2 pot).

I have middle pair (Nines) with a Queen kicker and a gutshot straight draw on a coordinated board of Jack-Nine-Eight. Minni's continuation bet of 200 into a 400 pot is standard. Folding would be far too weak given my equity and the strength of a pair of nines in a heads-up pot. While raising is an option to protect my hand against overcards, I prefer calling to keep the pot manageable and avoid being pushed off my equity if Minni has a Jack or a stronger draw. I am getting 3-to-1 on a call, which is more than sufficient for my hand's current value and potential to improve.

On the turn, the Jack of Spades pairs the board. I currently hold two pair (Jacks and Nines) with a Queen kicker. While the second Jack reduces the probability of my opponent having one, it's still a very real possibility given their button opening range. Checking is the most prudent play here to control the pot size and see how Minni responds. My hand has value as a bluff catcher, but leading out into the pre-flop aggressor on this card doesn't serve much purpose other than potentially overbuilding a pot where I might be second-best to a Jack or a straight.

I bet the flop as a semi‑bluff with an OESD and was called. On the turn the board paired (Jc Js 9s 8h) and Flash checked. With Tc4s I still have 8 outs (Q or 7) but only one card to come (~17% to hit). The paired board increases the chance my opponent has a made hand (trip J or two‑pair), which reduces my fold equity and makes a turn semi‑bluff marginal — a bet here will often be called by hands that already beat me and could induce a shove I shouldn’t call with ~17% equity. I’m in position and can control the pot, so I’ll check back the turn to preserve chips, realize my equity on a free river, and re‑evaluate based on his river action (call small bets that offer correct pot odds, fold to large jams).

On the river, the board is Jc-9s-8h-Js-7s. I hold two pair, Jacks and Nines, with a Queen kicker. The Seven on the river is a significant card because any Ten now completes a straight (7-8-9-T-J). Minni, the pre-flop aggressor, bet the flop but checked back the turn. This turn check often indicates a marginal made hand or a capped range, making it less likely she holds a Jack for trips. However, she could easily have a Ten in her range that just improved to a straight. By checking, I can reach a cheap showdown or potentially induce a bluff from a missed draw. Betting doesn't achieve much value as most hands that call me (like a Ten or a Jack) have me beaten, and hands I beat (like an 8 or a 7) are unlikely to call a bet on such a coordinated board.

River completed my straight. I hold T♣4♠ and the final board is Jc 9s 8h Js 7s — I now have J‑10‑9‑8‑7 (a made straight). Opponent called my flop bet and then checked the turn and river, which is consistent with medium pairs, missed draws, or slowplays. Hands that beat me are limited (higher straights like Q10, spade‑flushes from two spades in hand, or rare full houses such as J+7/J+9 or pocket 7s/9s), but most of his calling range on the flop (9x, 8x, Tx, Kx/Ax, some pocket pairs) are behind my straight or will pay to see a smallish river bet.

Given stacks and the line, this is a clear value‑bet spot rather than checking behind and letting him bluff. I want a size that gets called by single pair/weak two‑pair type hands without giving correct odds to any remaining drawing combos (though most draws are dead now). I will bet about half the pot to extract value while keeping weaker hands in. If he check‑raises massively I’ll re‑evaluate (large check‑raise likely indicates a flush/full house combo and I’ll be cautious), but against a single bet I expect many calls and positive EV.

On the river, the board is Jc-9s-8h-Js-7s. I hold two pair (Jacks and Nines) with a Queen kicker. Minni's line is interesting: she raised pre-flop, bet the flop, but checked the turn when the second Jack appeared. This check on the turn often caps her range, making it less likely she holds a Jack for trips. The 7 on the river completes a straight for any Ten (T-x). Minni's bet of 400 into 800 (half pot) is a sizing that could represent a value bet with a straight or a bluff with a missed draw. Given that I am getting 3-to-1 pot odds and her turn check showed weakness, my two pair is a strong enough bluff-catcher in a heads-up match to justify a call. I am losing to a Ten or a slow-played Jack, but I beat missed draws and potentially lower pairs that she might be betting for thin value or turning into a bluff.

I am on the button with 4h 5h, a solid suited connector that plays well in position. My stack is relatively short compared to Minni's, but I still have over 60 big blinds. Raising here is the standard play to take control of the pot and potentially take it down pre-flop. If called, I have a hand that flops well.

Flash opened to 300 from the button (standard, likely wide). 7♦5♣ is marginal but has decent heads-up playability and we get good pot odds to defend: call 200 more into a 400 pot (we'd need ~25% equity). I don't want to 3-bet OOP with an offsuit junk hand and risk getting isolated or facing a shove from a shorter-stacked opponent, nor do I want to fold too narrowly heads-up. Plan: call to 300 total, see the flop, and proceed cautiously out of position — continue with pairs/strong draws and fold to heavy aggression with nothing.

Heads-up out of position with 7♦5♣ on T♠ J♥ 6♦ — no pair, no meaningful draw (only weak backdoor straight prospects if the turn brings an 8 or 4). Pot is 600 and I don’t want to build a big pot OOP with air. Check to control the size, induce a c-bet, and preserve my deep stack. Plan: call a small c-bet (≈ ≤1/3 pot, ~200) to realize backdoor equity; fold to medium/large c-bets (>~50–60% pot) since I have very poor equity and playability OOP. If Flash checks back I’ll take a free turn card and re-evaluate (only continue with a pair/clear draw or a very favorable turn).

I am the pre-flop aggressor with 5h 4h. The flop of Ts Jh 6d is relatively coordinated but provides me with very little direct equity. However, as the pre-flop raiser, this board texture hits my perceived range (Overpairs, AJ, KJ, QJ) well enough to attempt a continuation bet. Minni has checked to me, and a standard half-pot bet often wins the pot immediately against hands that missed this flop. If called, I have some backdoor potential, but I will likely look to give up on the turn unless the board improves my equity significantly.

Board T♠ J♥ 6♦ — my 7♦5♣ has no pair and no clean draw, but Flash's bet is very small (≈1/3 pot). Pot is 900 and to call 300 gives ~4:1 pot odds (need ~20% equity). My chance to pair by the river (~26–27%) plus the likelihood Villain is c-betting a wide HUD range heads-up justifies a cheap call to realize equity and keep the pot manageable out of position. Raising OOP with air would be speculative and folding here would be too tight given the odds. Plan: call 300, see the turn; fold to significant turn aggression if I don't improve, but continue/raise if I hit a pair or pick up a strong draw.

Check. Out of position with 7♦5♣ on T♠ J♥ 6♦ 4♦ — I have no pair and only backdoor outs. I count ~14 clean outs (three 7s, three 5s, four 3s, four 8s) — roughly a 30% chance to improve on the river. Pot is 1,200 and Flash has ~5,600 behind. Betting OOP with air simply bloats the pot and loses to his calling/3-betting frequency; checking preserves pot control and lets me realize equity cheaply. Plan: check and evaluate his action — call small turn/river bets (~≤1/3 pot, ~≤400) to see the river, but fold to large overbets or a shove since I have no made hand and poor playability OOP. If he checks back I get a free river and can re-evaluate with any pair or completed straight.

On the turn 4d, I have improved to bottom pair (fours). The board (Ts Jh 6d 4d) is quite wet with several straight and flush draws possible. Minni called my flop continuation bet, which suggests she likely has a piece of the board (a Jack, a Ten, or a draw). By checking back here, I control the size of the pot with a hand that has some showdown value but is unlikely to be ahead if she calls another bet. It also allows me to see a river card for free, where I might improve to two pair or trips, or simply get to showdown cheaply against her missed draws.

I just rivered a 4-5-6-7-8 straight (my 7♦ + 5♣ with board T♠ J♥ 6♦ 4♦ 8♦). Heads-up this is a very strong hand — the only realistic hands that beat me are diamond flushes (villain would need two diamonds). Flash’s line (button open, c-bet flop, then check on the diamond turn) makes a two-diamond flush less likely; many of his holdings are single pairs or missed broadway hands that will call a modest river bet. I want to extract value from one-pair and second-pair type hands without bloating the pot against the small flush risk, so I’ll bet about half the pot. If he raises/shoves big I’ll respect that as a likely flush and fold.

I have bottom pair (fours) on a board that is now very coordinated (Ts Jh 6d 4d 8d). The river 8d completes several straights and the diamond flush. Minni led for half the pot after I checked back the turn. Given that my hand loses to almost any pair or completed draw, and the board texture favors many hands in Minni's calling range, I cannot justify a call here with such weak showdown value.

Heads-up on the button with 8h5h — a suited, playable hand with good postflop playability in position. Pot is 150 and a call would only complete to 100 and miss the chance to seize initiative. I want to open-raise wide from the button to get fold equity and play the hand in position, but I don't want to bloat the pot with a marginal holding. A raise to 300 (≈3x the BB) is standard here: it pressures the big blind, keeps the pot manageable if called, and preserves maneuverability postflop. Plan: if Flash folds we pick up the pot; if he calls we use position to c-bet or realize equity; if he 3-bets heavily we fold this marginal holding.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Five offsuit. This is a solid hand to defend in a heads-up situation against a button raise. Folding is too tight, and while a 3-bet is an option to try and take the pot now, calling allows me to play a pot out of position with an Ace in my hand while keeping the pot size controlled given my shorter stack compared to Minni.

I have bottom pair with an Ace kicker on a coordinated board of 5-6-8. Checking to the pre-flop aggressor is the standard play here to see how they react. I plan to call a reasonably sized continuation bet given I have a pair and overcard outs, while being mindful of the straight possibilities on this texture.

I have two pair (8s & 5s) on a 5c 8s 6d flop with position. This is a very strong heads‑up holding but vulnerable to sets/rare straights, so I want to charge worse pairs and strong draws while denying correct odds to continue. C‑bet for value and protection: ~2/3 pot is appropriate to extract from single pairs and draws without bloating the pot against a deeper stack. If called I’ll re‑evaluate on the turn; if check‑raised big I’ll assume a set and proceed cautiously (pot odds/stack dependent).

I have bottom pair with an Ace kicker on a coordinated board of 5-8-6. While the board is quite connected and hits many parts of a button's range, my hand is too strong to fold to a single continuation bet in a heads-up pot. Minni is an aggressive chip leader, and this bet could easily be made with overcards or air. Calling allows me to see a turn card and decide how to proceed based on the next card and Minni's subsequent action, while keeping the pot manageable for my stack size.

On the turn, the board is 5c-8s-6d-Tc. I have bottom pair (5s) with an Ace kicker. The Ten of Clubs is generally a better card for the pre-flop aggressor's range, although it doesn't change the straight possibilities much. Given that I checked and called the flop, checking the turn is the standard play to see how Minni proceeds. My hand has some showdown value and potential to improve to two pair or trips, but it is currently weak against many hands in Minni's opening range (any 8, 6, 10, or higher pocket pair). By checking, I control the size of the pot and look for more information based on Minni's action.

I have two pair (8s & 5s) on 5c-8s-6d-Tc in position heads‑up — a very strong hand that only really loses to sets. Flash is short (≈4,900) and called my flop c‑bet, so his continuing range is likely single pairs (6x, Tx, 8x), gutters (7x), and some pocket pairs. I want value and to deny correct odds to drawing hands, so I’ll bet for value/protection rather than check behind. The pot is 1,400; sizing around 2/3–3/4 pot is appropriate — large enough to extract from top pairs and charge draws but small enough to get called. I’ll bet 1,000. If he shoves I’m generally willing to call because two pair is ahead of most of his continuing range and his stack is small relative to mine; if he folds I collect the pot.

On the turn, the board has become increasingly coordinated with 5c-8s-6d-Tc. I hold bottom pair with an Ace kicker. Minni's bet of 1000 into a 1400 pot is a significant commitment and indicates continued strength. The Ten of Clubs likely improves many of the hands in Minni's button-raising range (such as AT, KT, QT, JT, T9). Furthermore, the board texture offers numerous straight possibilities (79, 47) and two-pair combinations that beat my bottom pair. With only five potential outs to improve (Aces or Fives) and facing a player who is likely to continue aggression on the river, calling 1000 chips with a marginal hand is not mathematically sound or strategically wise for my current stack. I will fold to preserve my chips for a more favorable situation.

I am significantly behind in stack size compared to Minni. Hand 8-4 offsuit is one of the weakest starting hands in poker. While I am in position, the risk of playing a junk hand against a large-stack, aggressive opponent outweighs the potential reward of seeing a flop for 50 more chips. Folding allows me to preserve my stack for a better opportunity.

Heads-up on the button (small blind) — I act first preflop. 2♣7♠ is a junk hand, but in heads-up position I should be opening very wide to pressure the BB and pick up the pot preflop. I’ll make a standard 3x open to 300 to fold out weaker holdings and keep the initiative. If Flash 3-bets big I’ll let this hand go (very poor equity vs a reraise); if he calls I’ll use position to proceed cautiously postflop and fold to heavy resistance when I miss.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Five offsuit (Ac 5d). Minni, the chip leader, has raised to 300 from the Button. While my stack is significantly smaller than Minni's, 47 Big Blinds is enough to play post-flop. Ace-Five is a strong enough hand to defend in a heads-up scenario, as it is ahead of a wide Button opening range. I will call to see a flop and control the pot size rather than 3-betting and potentially facing a 4-bet shove from a dominant stack.

On a flop of Qs-4s-5h, I have hit bottom pair (fives) with an Ace kicker. Given that I am out of position against the pre-flop aggressor (Minni), the standard play is to check to the raiser. This allows me to evaluate the strength of her hand based on her betting action. Bottom pair is a hand with some showdown value but is vulnerable, so I want to keep the pot small for now. I will check and likely call a standard-sized continuation bet.

I was the preflop aggressor (3x open) and have position. The flop Q♠ 4♠ 5♥ is fairly dry and Flash checked — his calling range is wide heads-up. I hold 7♠2♣: no pair and only a single spade (a very weak backdoor draw), so this is a pure continuation-bluff. A c-bet here will fold a lot of non-made hands (overcards without spade, small connectors, low pairs) and preserves our initiative. I’ll size up to ~2/3 pot to apply meaningful pressure but keep the bet small relative to my huge stack; if he raises I’ll fold (very poor equity), if he calls I’ll proceed cautiously on the turn and reevaluate.

I have bottom pair (fives) with the best possible kicker (Ace) on a board of Qs-4s-5h. Minni's 2/3 pot bet of 400 into 600 is a standard continuation bet from a button raiser. My hand is too strong to fold at this stage, as it beats all of her air and missed high-card hands (like Ace-high or King-high combinations that didn't hit). However, raising would likely only get called by better hands like a Queen or a strong draw, and would isolate me against a range that crushes me. Calling allows me to keep the pot manageable and see how the turn develops while keeping her potential bluffs in the hand.

On the turn, the Jack of Spades completes the spade flush draw and adds another overcard to my pair of fives. As I am out of position against a significantly larger stack, I must continue to play cautiously. My hand has some showdown value against her bluffs or smaller pairs, but it cannot withstand heavy pressure. Checking is the most appropriate action here to see how Minni responds to the new board texture; it allows me to potentially see a cheap river or fold if she demonstrates significant strength.

I opened wide preflop and c-bet the flop; Flash called. The turn (Qs 4s 5h Js) brings a third spade to the board and significantly increases the chance he already has a made flush (two spades in hand) or has improved to a Jx. My hand (7♠2♣) has no pair and only a one-card spade backdoor — very little showdown equity against his calling range. Pot is 1400 and his effective stack (after his calls) is ~3550, so a large turn shove or oversized bet risks a big chunk of chips when I’m usually behind. I planned to proceed cautiously after a flop call, so I’ll control the pot size: check the turn. If he bets I’ll fold to a value-sized bet (call only very small bluffs); if he checks back I can evaluate the river (I pick up a ~19% river spade to make a flush and can then value/raise). Checking preserves chips and keeps pressure options later.

On the river 2d, the board is Qs-4s-5h-Js-2d. I have bottom pair (fives) with an Ace kicker. The turn went check-check after Minni bet the flop. Her check on the turn after the spade flush completed suggests she either has a marginal hand she is trying to get to showdown with (like a small pair or a Jack) or she was bluffing and has given up. My hand has some showdown value but is not strong enough to bet for value, as I'm unlikely to get called by worse. Checking is the most prudent play to see if I can win at showdown or to evaluate a potential bluff from Minni.

Heads-up, I opened preflop and c-bet the flop; villain called the flop and both of us checked the turn. The river (2♦) gives me a small pair (deuces) on a Q♠4♠5♥J♠2♦ board. Many hands in his calling range (Qx, Jx, any spade flush, 4s/5s) still beat me, but he also has a lot of missed broadways and single-card hands (A/K highs) that will call a small river bet. My goal is to extract thin value from worse hands and folding to a raise is acceptable — I have very little equity vs a raise-heavy line. I’ll make a modest value bet (~40% pot) to target sticky worse hands; if he raises I fold.

On a river board of Qs-4s-5h-Js-2d, I hold bottom pair with an Ace kicker. The turn action is the most critical part of this hand: after Minni's flop continuation bet and my call, she checked back on the turn when the third spade and the Jack appeared. This check-back typically narrows her range significantly; she likely doesn't have a flush or a strong Queen, as those hands would usually continue betting for value or protection on such a coordinated board. Her river bet of 600 into 1400 is a relatively small sizing (around 43% of the pot), which could represent thin value with a hand like a Jack or a medium pocket pair, but it also serves as a tempting price for a bluff with missed straight draws (like 6-7, 7-8, or 8-9). Given the pot odds (getting 3.3 to 1 on a call), my pair of fives is a viable bluff-catcher against a wide button-opening range that has given up on the turn and is now taking a final stab at the pot.

I am holding 2-7 offsuit, which is widely considered the worst starting hand in Texas Hold'em. Given the stack sizes and the need to preserve my chips for better opportunities to close the gap with the chip leader, there is no justification for playing this hand. Folding is the standard and correct play here.

Heads-up on the button (small blind) I want to be aggressive and take the initiative. Q♠5♠ is a suited queen with decent playability in heads-up — good blocker and enough equity to open. A standard 3x BB raise to 300 applies pressure, preserves fold equity, and keeps the pot a manageable size versus Flash’s 6000 stack. Plan: fold to a large 3-bet, but if called use position to c-bet favorable flops and steer the hand. Raising to 300 is the right mix of aggression and pot control here.

I am in the Big Blind with J-3 offsuit. Minni, who has a significant chip lead, has raised to 300. J-3 is a very weak hand, even in a heads-up situation, and I am out of position. Given the stack sizes and the hand's lack of equity or playability, folding is the most prudent decision to preserve my stack.

I am on the button with 5s6s, a strong speculative hand. In a heads-up situation against Minni, who has a significant chip lead, I should maintain an aggressive but controlled strategy. Raising here is standard to take control of the hand and capitalize on the playability of suited connectors post-flop.

Heads-up in the big blind with JJ — a very strong hand against a SB open to 300 (to call 200, pot 400). Flash has a wide opening range from the button/SB and only 5,700 behind (effective stack ~57BB). I want to take the initiative and get value/fold equity rather than just giving a free/cheap flop, so I’ll 3-bet to build the pot and isolate. A 3-bet to 1,200 total (~12 BB) is large enough to deny correct odds to many speculative hands and extract value from worse pairs/overcards, while not committing my entire stack; if Flash shoves I’ll have a strong call with JJ, and if he calls we play postflop with initiative.

I have 5s6s on the button (SB) in a heads-up match. I opened for 300, and Minni 3-bet to 1200. Minni has a massive chip lead (over 300bb vs my 57bb). While 5s6s is a great speculative hand with high post-flop playability, a 4x 3-bet from a huge chip leader when I'm relatively short-stacked is quite strong. However, in heads-up play, folding suited connectors to a single 3-bet is often too tight. Calling allows me to see a flop with a hand that can flop very strongly or provide semi-bluffing opportunities. I need to be careful not to overcommit if I hit only a marginal piece, but the pot odds and hand potential justify a call to see a flop.

I 3-bet preflop to 1,200 with JJ and was called by a wide-opening SB, so Flash’s range is fairly wide (lots of two-gappers/suited connectors, broadways and small/medium pairs). The flop T♠-6♣-5♣ gives me an overpair and a club blocker (J♣). This is a textbook continuation-bet spot: I want to get value from worse tens/small pairs/second-pair hands and deny correct odds to club and straight draws. I’ll bet ~2/3 pot (1,600 chips) — large enough to charge draws and get value, but not overcommit against his 4,800 effective stack. If he calls we can proceed cautiously on turn; if he shoves, I’ll give his range credit for many draws/bluffs and will be willing to call (or re-evaluate depending on turn texture), but for now the best line is to bet for value and protection.

I have flopped bottom two pair (5s and 6s) on a 5-6-10 board with two clubs. In a heads-up 3-bet pot, this is an exceptionally strong hand. The current pot is 4,000 and I have 4,800 remaining, giving us a stack-to-pot ratio (SPR) of 1.2. At this SPR, I am committed to the hand. Minni's range for 3-betting pre-flop includes strong overpairs like JJ, QQ, KK, and AA, all of which I am currently ahead of, as well as flush draws and big suited connectors. By raising all-in now, I maximize value against her overpairs and draws while protecting my vulnerable two pair from many scary turn cards (any club, 7, 8, 9, or T).

I have JJ (Jc Jh) — an overpair to the T♠-6♣-5♣ flop and I hold a club blocker (Jc). I c-bet 1,600 and Flash shoved to 4,800 (he’s now all-in). The pot is 8,800 and I must call 3,200 more; after my call the pot will be ~12,000 so I need ≈26.7% equity to break even. Flash called my 3-bet preflop and jams this two-club board with a wide mix of club draws, some overcards/air, and a minority of sets/top-pair hands. JJ is ahead of most of that range (and the Jc blocks some strong club combos), so my equity comfortably exceeds the break-even threshold. Raising beyond his all-in is pointless (it only creates a side pot), and folding a live overpair for ~3.2k is too nitty. Call for value.

Heads-up on the button I have position and should be playing a wide, aggressive preflop range. 8♣5♦ is marginal but perfectly fine to open-raise in HU — raising takes the initiative, picks up folds from many BB hands, and lets me play postflop in position when called. I’ll open to a standard 3x BB (300 total). If Flash 3-bets large I’ll fold this hand; if he calls I can use position to extract value or realize equity.

I have been dealt pocket sixes in the big blind. In a heads-up scenario, this is a very strong starting hand. Minni has raised to 3BB from the button. To take initiative and build a pot with a hand that is likely ahead of her opening range, I will put in a standard 3-bet to 900. This also helps me define her hand strength if she decides to 4-bet or just call.

I opened to 300 on the button with 8♣5♦ and Flash 3‑bet to 900. Folding now is too tight — I already have 300 in and 8‑5o is playable in position against a likely wide HU 3‑bet range. Calling 600 more (900 total committed) gives me ~1:3 pot odds (need ≈25% equity) and keeps the pot manageable with the positional advantage; SPR after a call is ~6, which lets me maneuver postflop. A 4‑bet bluff here is thin (deep stacks, opponent could call or shove), so I’ll call, see the flop, and use position to float, realize equity, or fold to heavy resistance.

The flop of 4h-Qd-Ac is quite unfavorable for my pocket sixes as it contains two overcards. While my pre-flop 3-bet represents a strong range, this board texture hits many of the hands Minni would call a 3-bet with, such as Ax, Qx, or broadway combinations. Betting here into a calling range that likely contains many Aces and Queens is risky and turns my hand into a bluff with very little equity if called. I will check to control the size of the pot and see how Minni reacts. If she bets significantly, I will likely have to fold as my hand is now essentially a low-pair bluff catcher.

I opened and called Flash’s 3‑bet preflop and the flop comes A♣ Q♦ 4♥ — I have 8♣5♦: no pair, no real draw (only a remote runner/runner straight). Flash’s 3‑betting range heads‑up is heavy in Ax/Qx/pairs and will defend these boards a lot, so a continuation‑bluff here is unlikely to fold enough to be +EV. Pot is 1,800 with effective stacks ≈11,100 (SPR ≈ 6), so pot‑control and using position is preferable. I’ll check back to see a turn, realize equity, and look for a better bluffing spot or fold to turn aggression; only barrel if I pick up meaningful equity or clear fold equity.

The board has become even more dangerous for my pocket sixes with the addition of the King of Diamonds. The board now includes three overcards (Ace, King, Queen) that hit many hands in Minni's 3-bet calling range. Since the flop checked through, it's possible neither of us has a strong hand, but my sixes have very little equity here. I will check again to keep the pot small and avoid over-committing with a hand that is likely beaten.

I opened on the button and called Flash’s 3‑bet preflop; the flop A♣ Q♦ 4♥ was checked through and the turn K♦ is checked by him again. I hold 8♣5♦ with no pair and no real draw. Flash’s HU 3‑bet range contains lots of Ax, QQ/KK, strong broadways and pocket pairs — many of those hands will happily call a turn c‑bet and some will be checking to control pot or trap. Betting the turn as a bluff has poor fold equity against this range, and there’s no thin value to extract. Pot is 1,800 and effective stacks ≈11,100 (SPR ≈6), so pot‑control is sensible: keep the pot small, use position, and wait for a river that either improves me or gives a better bluffing spot. I’ll check the turn. Plan: if Flash fires the river I’ll fold most reasonable bet sizes with nothing; if he checks again I’ll evaluate the river texture and consider a targeted delayed bluff only on very favorable cards or simply check back to preserve chips.

The river is a four, which pairs the board and gives me two pair: sixes and fours. However, the board is extremely coordinated with an Ace, King, and Queen. In a 3-bet pot, Minni's calling range consists of many broadway cards and pairs. Almost any card she called with (Ace-x, King-x, Queen-x, or any pair higher than sixes) now beats my hand. Furthermore, a straight is possible with Jack-Ten. Since the flop and turn both checked through, it is possible Minni is weak, but my pocket sixes have very little relative strength on this board. Betting would not serve as a value bet because I'm unlikely to be called by a worse hand, and it's unlikely to work as a bluff given the board texture. I will check and hope to take it down at a cheap showdown if she has complete air or a smaller pocket pair.

Check. I hold 8♣5♦ on A♣ Q♦ 4♥ K♦ 4♣ — no pair, no meaningful draw and I block none of Flash’s biggest value combos. Flash 3‑bet preflop and then checked through; his continuing range is heavy in Ax/Kx/QQ/KK and other strong broadways and pairs that will call a river bluff far more often than fold. The pot is 1,800: a half‑pot bluff (~900) needs >33% fold equity to breakeven (smaller bluffs still need ~20–25%), which I don’t expect versus a checked 3‑bet line. There’s no thin value to extract and little credible fold equity, so I’ll preserve chips and check to showdown (fold to any river raise).

In Hand #123, I am on the button with T3o, which is a weak holding. Looking at the current stack sizes, Minni has a significant lead with 27,000 chips while I have 12,850. Given the hand history, I have been playing somewhat aggressively but have also been folding frequently to pre-flop pressure or on unfavorable flops. T3o is not a hand that plays well out of position or even as a steal attempt against a larger stack in the big blind who has shown a willingness to defend or re-raise. To conserve my stack and wait for a more advantageous situation, I will fold this hand.

Heads-up on the button (SB) with A♥T♦ — a strong, standard opening hand. Calling 50 is passive and gives Flash the initiative; I want to take control and build the pot with a hand that plays well postflop when I hit an Ace or a Ten. A raise to 300 (total) is a normal heads-up sizing here: it applies fold equity vs his wide BB defending range, preserves maneuverability with my deep stack (27,100), and prices out some of his speculative hands. If he 3-bets big I'll re-evaluate (likely fold to an oversized 3-bet, or call/float vs small sizing), but the default +300 raise maximizes expected value in position.

I am holding 7-5 offsuit in the Big Blind. Minni, who currently holds a significant chip lead, has raised to 300 from the button. This hand is near the bottom of my range and performs poorly out of position. While I need to defend my big blind frequently in heads-up play, 7-5 offsuit is a clear fold against a 3x open raise. Preserving my stack for better spots is more important right now given the stack disparity.

I am on the button with Ace-Eight suited (clubs). This is a strong starting hand in a heads-up situation. My standard open sizing throughout this tournament has been 300 (3x the big blind). I will continue with this strategy to build the pot with a hand that has good post-flop potential and high card value.

Heads-up in the big blind with J♥9♥. Opponent (SB) opened to 300 (3x). Calling costs me 200 more (300 total) into a 400 pot — roughly 3:1 pot odds, so this is a cheap defend. J9s has strong postflop playability (flush/straight potential, decent showdown value heads-up) and will perform well against a wide SB opening range. I'm out of position postflop, so I want to avoid inflating the pot with a marginal small 3-bet; a larger 3-bet could be an option exploitatively but is unnecessary here given stack sizes and opponent tendencies. I’ll call to see a flop, realize equity, and proceed based on the texture and opponent’s actions.

Heads-up out of position with J♥9♥ on Q♠ 8♥ 7♣. I have no pair but a reasonable amount of equity (a gutshot to a 10 plus backdoor hearts) versus a wide SB opening range. The board is relatively dry and I don’t want to inflate the pot out of position with a marginal hand, so pot-control is best. I’ll check to allow Flash to c-bet and then call small-to-medium bets to realize equity; if he checks back I can consider a turn stab on a safe card (a J/9/heart) or take showdown cheaply. I’ll fold to a very large polarized shove or an overbet that shows strong value, but otherwise plan to check-call and re-evaluate on the turn.

I have middle pair (Eights) with the best possible kicker (Ace) on a relatively coordinated board (Q-8-7). While my hand is strong in a heads-up context, it is not a powerhouse. Checking back here achieves several goals: it controls the size of the pot, protects my checking range, and prevents me from being forced off the hand by a check-raise from a Queen or a semi-bluff. I am happy to see a turn card and evaluate Minni's action then.

Out of position on a paired turn (Qs Qh 8h 7c) with J♥9♥ I have only draw equity (about 12 unique outs to a straight/flush, ~25-26% to improve). Flash checked the flop and checked to me on the turn, which often indicates either a missed flop or pot-control with a medium strength hand. Betting here as a bluff is thin — many hands that beat me (Qx, any pair, strong Ax/Kx) will either call or raise, and a small c-bet will get called by worse hands and fold only the very weakest. With a healthy stack lead and a relatively small pot (600), the lowest-variance, highest-expected-value line is to keep the pot under control and realize my equity. I will check back the turn, plan to call reasonable river bets when I hit or when the price is right, and fold to large polarized overbets or jams if I miss the river.

I have a solid hand with two pair (Queens and Eights) and the best possible kicker (Ace). Minni's checks on both the flop and the turn suggest she is unlikely to hold a Queen or a better Eight. By betting 300 (50% of the pot), I am looking to extract value from marginal hands in her range, such as a Seven, lower pocket pairs (22-66), or straight draws (J10, 910, 69) that might be willing to pay to see a river card. This bet also serves to protect my hand and maintain the initiative in the pot.

Flash bets a small 300 into a 900 pot on the turn. I hold J♥9♥ on 8♥ Q♠ 7♣ Q♥ — I’m on a very strong one-card draw: a 4‑to‑a‑flush (9 hearts left) plus straight and two‑pair outs (three non‑heart 10s to complete 7‑8‑9‑10‑J, three J’s and three 9’s to make two pair). Counting overlaps properly that's roughly 18 meaningful outs (~39% to improve on the river). Calling 300 into a pot that would become ~1500 requires only ~20% equity to break even, so this is a cheap, +EV call vs Flash’s likely wide opening range. I’m out of position so I don’t want to bloat the pot with a marginal raise — calling keeps the pot controlled, realizes my equity, and lets me re‑evaluate on the river. Plan: call the 300; if I hit (flush/two‑pair/straight) I’ll extract value on the river; if I miss I’ll fold to large polarized river overbets.

I improved on the river to two pair (Queens and Nines) with J♥9♥ on 8♥ Q♠ 7♣ Q♥ 9♣. This is a decent showdown hand vs Flash’s wide button opening range and many missed turn/river draws, but it’s vulnerable to any Q (trips/full house) and some combos that include a 9+Q (full house/quads). Flash’s line (checking flop, small 300 turn bet) looks like a mixed range of thin value and bluffs, so checking risks letting worse hands check back and losing value. I want to extract thin value from Kx/Ax/Jx/pocket pairs and missed draws without bloating the pot when I’m behind to Qx, so I’ll make a medium value bet — roughly half the pot. If he raises I’ll assume a very strong holding (Qx or better) and fold to avoid getting exploited.

On the river, the board is 8h-Qs-7c-Qh-9c. I hold Ace-Eight, giving me two pair (Queens and Eights) with the best possible kicker. Looking back at the action: I checked the flop for pot control and to protect my range. When Minni checked the turn, I bet 300 for value, and she called. The river 9 is a somewhat coordinated card, completing a potential straight for J-T, but it also misses many other draws. Minni's lead of 600 into the 1200 pot (a half-pot bet) could represent a Queen, a completed straight, or a bluff with a missed draw (like hearts). In a heads-up match, folding an Ace-high two pair in this spot would be overly tight, as I only need to be right 25% of the time to break even. I will call to see the showdown.

Open-raise to seize initiative in heads-up. T♢4♢ is marginal but suited and plays well in position; with 28400 (284 BB) vs Flash’s 11450 (114 BB) I can apply pressure without risking too much. A 3x open to 300 gives good fold equity versus his wide defending range, avoids the passivity of a limp/call, and keeps the pot manageable if he calls. Plan: fold to a large 3-bet (this hand is too weak to continue vs a big reraise); if called, C‑bet selectively on favorable/dry flops and pursue backdoor diamond/straight equity when appropriate. Raising now maximizes opportunities to pick up the blind and use positional advantage postflop.

I am in the Big Blind holding Jack of Hearts and Four of Clubs, which is a very weak starting hand. Minni, who is the current chip leader, has raised to 300 from the Button. While the price to call is only 200 more into a 400 pot, playing a hand as weak as J4o out of position against an aggressive big stack is generally unprofitable. My objective is to preserve my stack and look for better spots as we approach the final hands of the tournament. Folding here is the safest and most standard play.

In heads-up play, while the Small Blind/Button has the advantage of position post-flop, Jack-Deuce offsuit (J2o) is a very weak starting hand. Given my current stack size of 114 big blinds and the fact that there are 24 hands remaining in the tournament, there is no immediate pressure to play sub-optimal hands. Minni has been quite aggressive from the Big Blind, and J2o does not play well against a potential 3-bet or in large pots. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity to utilize my positional advantage.

Heads-up on the button I should be aggressive and open a very wide range. 5♣9♦ is marginal but perfectly fine to raise in heads-up; raising takes the initiative, uses position postflop, and generates fold equity against Flash. I’ll raise to the minimum allowed (200 total) — it's a standard 2xBB open that keeps the pot manageable if called. Plan: fold to a large, polarized 3-bet; call small/medium 3-bets only with a plan to play cautiously postflop and c-bet favorable textures when appropriate.

In a heads-up situation, King-Six offsuit is a hand that should definitely be played against a small opening raise. Minni raised to 200 (a min-raise), meaning I only need to call 100 more chips to see the flop. With pot odds of 3-to-1 and a hand that has reasonable equity against a wide button-opening range, calling is the standard and correct play to see how the board develops.

On this Ace-high flop of 4s-7s-Ah, the board heavily favors the pre-flop aggressor's range. Holding King-Six offsuit, I have no pieces of the board and no significant draws. My best play is to check and see how Minni reacts. If she makes a continuation bet, I will most likely fold as King-high has little equity here against her likely range.

Heads-up I opened a very wide range preflop, but this flop (A♥ 7♠ 4♠) strongly favors the BB’s calling range — lots of A-x, small pairs and spade holdings. My 5♣9♦ has no pair, no spade draw and therefore very little equity or meaningful blockers. A c-bet here would be a pure bluff that will frequently be called by any Ace, pair, or spade draw and put me in difficult spots with deep stacks. I’ll control the pot and keep options open by checking to see the turn; if Villain shows aggression I can fold or consider a targeted bluff depending on the turn card and sizing.

The flop was checked by both players, which suggests Minni likely doesn't have a strong Ace or was playing a marginal hand cautiously. The turn card pairs the board with a second four. Holding King-high, I have very little showdown value and no draws. I will check again to see if Minni continues to check behind. If she bets, I will likely fold, but there is no reason to commit chips to the pot with air while out of position.

Heads-up I opened wide preflop but on this turn (board 4♠ 7♠ A♥ 4♥) my 5♣9♦ has no pair and no spade draw. The turn pairs the board and strongly favors the BB’s calling range (A‑x, small pairs, and spade holdings). Betting here would be a thin bluff with poor fold equity against a range that called the flop, so I should control the pot and keep the initiative optional for the river. I’ll check to see how Flash reacts; plan to fold to meaningful turn aggression and re-evaluate the river for a targeted bluff only if the card and sizing make it profitable.

The hand has been checked down to the river on a board of 4s-7s-Ah-4h-3h. Holding King-high, I have reasonable showdown value in a pot where the pre-flop aggressor (Minni) has shown no interest in betting on an Ace-high or paired board. Minni's checks on the flop and turn suggest she likely has a weak hand, a missed draw, or a marginal pair she is trying to take to a cheap showdown. By checking, I am hoping to win the pot with King-high against her missed draws or lower high-card hands. There is no merit in betting, as I likely only get called by hands that beat me (like a 7 or a small pocket pair) and I already beat the hands she would fold.

I missed — 5♣9♦ never improved on 4♠7♠A♥ / 4♥ / 3♥. Flash took a passive line (checked flop and turn), which is exactly how many showdown-value hands (A‑x, small pairs, pocket 4s) or two-heart flushes will play. Betting the 400‑chip pot would be a thin bluff with very poor fold equity against that range and no blockers to remove their best hands. With stacks deep relative to the pot and essentially no value, the highest EV line is to check to showdown and conserve chips. (If Flash now fires a meaningful river bet I will fold — this hand has no calling merit versus a bet.)

I am holding 65 suited on the button. In heads-up play, this is a strong hand that should almost always be opened. Given I am trailing Minni in chips and there are only 21 hands remaining in the tournament, I need to look for profitable opportunities to accumulate chips. A standard raise to 300 allows me to take control of the hand and put pressure on the big blind while retaining the ability to navigate post-flop with a hand that has good connectivity and flush potential.

Heads-up vs the button (Flash) who opened to 300. I’m in the big blind with A♥3♠. The pot is 400 and it’s 200 to call (300 total put into the pot from my seat). Against a wide heads-up opening range A3o is strong enough to continue — I also get good pot odds: risk 200 to win 600 (≈33%). A small 3-bet to the min (500) would buy little fold equity and still leave me out of position postflop; a larger 3-bet is unnecessary with a marginal kicker and deep stacks. I’ll call, keep the pot manageable, see the flop, and proceed based on texture (value/raise on ace or good draw, fold or pot-control vs heavy action on bad boards).

Flop checked to me with A♥3♠ on A♣ J♣ 2♦. I have top pair but a very weak kicker and the board has two clubs (club draws) plus Jx/Ax combos in Villain’s wide heads‑up opening range.
