Checking lets many club draws and worse Aces see a free turn; betting charges draws and extracts value from worse Aces and Jx. I’ll make a value/protection bet of ~2/3 pot to deny good odds to draws and get called by worse hands. If he raises big I’ll re‑evaluate (large raises often indicate two‑pair/sets or strong Jx/Ax combos).

On a flop of Ad Jc 2d, my 6d 5d has very little equity. Minni's lead for 400 into the 600 pot (making it 1000 total) suggests she either has a piece of the board or is testing my continuation. Given the presence of an Ace and a Jack, along with a club flush draw, my hand is currently 6-high with only a backdoor diamond draw. Continuing here against a large stack is unnecessarily risky with such a weak hand. I will fold and wait for a better spot to utilize my remaining chips.

Heads-up on the button (I’m the SB) with K♥Q♦ — a strong, playable hand with good equity and post‑flop playability against a wide BB defending range. The pot is 150 and I only need to call 50, but I want to seize the initiative and build the pot from position while folding out weaker holdings. I’ll raise to the minimum open of 200 (2xBB) — a standard, inexpensive sizing that applies pressure yet keeps the pot manageable given our deep stacks. Plan: if called I’ll C‑bet favorable K/Q/high‑card boards and proceed cautiously on highly coordinated/flushy textures; if Flash 3‑bets to a small/moderate size I may call to see a flop, but I’ll fold to large, polarized 3‑bets. Raising here maximizes EV in heads‑up play and preserves maneuverability for the remaining hands.

I am holding 2d 4c, which is one of the weakest hands in poker. Although the pot odds are 3:1 to call the small raise from Minni, this hand plays very poorly out of position and has very little equity against a standard opening range. Given the stage of the tournament and my chip deficit relative to Minni, I prefer to fold and wait for a better opportunity rather than potentially losing more chips in a marginal spot.

In a heads-up situation, $Qc 6s$ is a reasonable hand to open from the Button/Small Blind. While it's not a powerhouse, I want to maintain pressure and not let the Big Blind win the blinds too easily, especially since I need to close the gap on Minni's chip lead. A standard raise to 300 is appropriate here to take control of the hand with position.

Call to 300. Heads-up QTo is well within a defendable range vs a 300 open — it has good equity and can make top pair and straights. Calling here gives ~33% pot odds and keeps the pot controlled out of position; I don’t want to inflate the pot OOP with a marginal hand by making a small 3-bet. With deep stacks I can play postflop (be aggressive if I hit a Q/T or a strong draw, fold or pot-control if I miss and face heavy action).

Dry, rainbow flop 9s-6c-5d. I hold Qh-Tc — two overcards and only very weak backdoor possibilities. I missed and I'm out of position versus an opponent who opened preflop and will c-bet a wide range. I want to keep the pot under control and realize equity rather than inflate the pot OOP with a marginal hand. Check and see what Flash does: plan to call small-to-medium continuation bets (≈≤1/2 pot, ~≤300) to chase my overcards and potential backdoor hearts; fold to large C-bets/overbets (>~2/3 pot) unless I pick up a real improvement. If Flash checks back I’ll evaluate the turn and either take a stab when I improve (pair or a strong draw) or check down to a cheap showdown. This preserves my 28.5k stack and keeps leverage against his 10.9k stack while avoiding difficult commit decisions out of position.

I have middle pair (sixes) with a Queen kicker on a 9-6-5 rainbow board. Minni checked to me after calling pre-flop. While middle pair is often a strong candidate for checking back to control the pot size and realize showdown equity, betting here serves to protect my hand against overcards and various straight draws (like 7-8 or 4-7) that may have missed. A half-pot bet of 300 is a standard continuation bet that can take the pot down immediately or define where I stand in the hand. If Minni raises, I can comfortably fold knowing my pair is likely beat on this connected board.

Heads‑up against a button open and a small C‑bet on this dry, rainbow 9♠-6♣-5♦ flop, Q♥T♣ has two overcards and respectable equity versus a wide continuation range. I’m out of position and don’t want to inflate the pot with a marginal hand, so I’ll take the cheap line and realize equity. Calling 300 keeps the pot controllable, gives me the chance to improve to a pair or pick up backdoor outs, and preserves stack flexibility versus Flash’s 10.6k. Plan: fold to large turn overbets/shoves if I completely miss; if I pair a Q or T (or get a good turn draw) I can apply pressure or extract value. Calling 300 is the lowest-variance, +EV choice here.

Check. Pot is 1,200, my stack 28,200 vs Flash 10,600. I hold Qh Tc on 9s-6c-5d-9d — two overcards, no flush and essentially no real straight draw. I only have 6 clean outs to improve to a pair (~6/46 ≈ 13%), so this is a marginal hand. Flash (preflop raiser) fired a small c-bet on the flop and then checked the turn, which is consistent with either pot-control or weakness but also with hands that now have a 9. Betting here would be a thin bluff with limited fold equity against a caller-heavy range and would inflate a pot OOP with a hand that rarely improves. With a big chip lead and many hands left, I prefer pot control and to realize equity — check to see a cheap river and re-evaluate. If I hit a Q or T on the river I’ll consider value action; if I miss I’ll fold to large river aggression and only call small/medium river bets when appropriate.

On this turn card, the nine pairs the board. I have two pair, Nines and Sixes, with a Queen kicker. Minni has check-called both pre-flop and on the flop. If I bet here, I'm rarely getting called by worse hands than mine, except perhaps a five or a straight draw. However, I risk being check-raised by a nine or a straight (7-8). Checking back allows me to control the pot size, realize my showdown equity, and see a river card for free. My hand is strong enough to reach showdown but not necessarily strong enough to value bet three streets on this board.

I check to a cheap showdown. I missed the river with Qh Tc on 9s-6c-5d-9d-4c — no pair and only two overcards. Flash (preflop raiser) c-bet the flop then checked the turn, which is consistent with pot-control with medium hands or with a made 9; there’s almost no value to extract and bluffing OOP into his range is thin with limited fold equity. Pot is 1,200 and I have a large stack advantage (28.2k vs his 10.6k), so the lowest-variance, +EV line is to check and see if he bluffs or fires a small value bet. I’ll call small river bets (~≤1/3–1/2 pot) that look like bluffs but fold to large value bets/overbets since I have no pair.

I have two pair, Nines and Sixes, with a Queen kicker on a board of 9-6-5-9-4. Minni has check-called on the flop and checked the turn and river. While my hand is reasonably strong for a heads-up situation, there is little value in betting. Most hands that I beat (like a 5, a 4, or a low pocket pair) might fold to a bet, while hands that beat me (a 9, or a straight like 7-8 or 2-3) would likely check-raise. Checking back allows me to take the hand to showdown and win the pot with my two pair while minimizing risk against a potential trap.

Heads-up on the button with A♣? Actually A♥2♣ (A2o) — this is a strong heads-up hand: good top-pair potential and it blocks many A-x combos. I have a large effective stack (28,150 vs 11,700) and will want to take the initiative and use position postflop. Calling 50 is too passive and lets the BB see a cheap flop with a wide range; folding is obviously too tight. A standard open to 300 (3x BB) applies pressure, isolates the BB, and keeps the pot a workable size (if he calls the pot will be ~600). I’ll raise to 300 to take the lead and play position postflop.

I am in the Big Blind with KQo, which is a very strong hand in a heads-up situation. Minni, who has a significant chip lead, has raised to 300 from the Small Blind/Button. While I could 3-bet here to take control of the pot, calling allows me to see a flop with a hand that plays well post-flop while keeping the pot manageable against a larger stack. Given the current tournament status (hand 132 of 150), I want to accumulate chips but avoid unnecessary high-variance spots unless I have a clearer edge. Calling the 200 to see the flop is a standard and solid play with this holding.

I have top pair with a strong kicker (King) on a board of Qs 9s 4d. This is a very strong hand in heads-up play. Standard procedure is to check to the pre-flop aggressor to see how they react. If Minni bets, I will definitely continue, either by calling to keep her bluffs in or raising for value and protection against the spade flush draw. Checking allows me to control the pot size and observe her betting behavior on this texture.

Heads-up on the button with A♥2♣ on a 4♦ 9♠ Q♠ flop. I raised preflop and Flash (BB) just checked — his calling range is wide (Qx, 9x, spade draws, Kx, small pairs, suited connectors). My hand is ace-high: not strong but has decent showdown value and blocks many A‑high/Q‑A combos. With position and the opponent showing weakness by checking, I should take the initiative and use a small c-bet to pick up the pot versus missed hands and charge spade draws. A 1/3 pot bet (~200 into 600) gives good fold equity, keeps the pot manageable if called, and lets me fold to significant aggression (or call small raises and re-evaluate). Plan: bet 200 now; if he folds I collect; if he calls I proceed cautiously on turn and give up to a large raise unless I improve.

I have flopped top pair with a strong King kicker on a board that contains a spade flush draw and some straight possibilities. Minni's small continuation bet of 200 into a 600 pot is typical for her aggressive style. While calling is an option to keep her bluffs in, I prefer raising for value and protection here. There are many cards on the turn that could kill the action or give her a draw for free. By raising to 700, I charge the flush and straight draws and get value from weaker Queens or middle pairs that she may be unwilling to fold yet. This also helps build a pot in case I need to commit more chips on later streets to capitalize on my strong hand.

Flash check‑raised my 1/3 pot c‑bet to 700 — a polarizing line that includes strong value (Qx, 9x, maybe sets) and spade semi‑bluffs. I hold A♥2♣: ace‑high with a useful Ace blocker to many A‑X and A‑Q combos but not a made hand. The pot is 1500 and I need to call an additional 500 (700 total), which gives me ~25% break‑even equity. Given villain’s range and the fact I beat bluffs and many draws, my realized equity is likely at or above that threshold. I have position and a much larger stack, so there’s no need to bloat the pot with a thin 3‑bet — raising to 1200 would overcommit with only ace‑high and allow him to comfortably respond (call or shove) from many better hands. Calling keeps the pot manageable, preserves fold equity on later streets, and lets me realize equity or improve on the turn; if the turn is harmless and he checks I can take the initiative, but if he barrels large and I haven’t improved I can fold without risking a big portion of my stack. Action: call 700.

I have top pair with a King kicker (Ks Qc) on a board of Qs 9s 4d 8d. After check-raising the flop and getting a call from Minni, I have the initiative. The turn 8d is relatively safe but adds a second flush draw. I will bet 1000 into the 2000 pot to continue extracting value from draws and weaker pairs while protecting my hand. Checking might allow Minni to take a free card or put me in a tough spot with a polarizing bet.

I c‑bet small on the Q♠9♠4♦ flop and called a large check‑raise — on the turn Flash barrels 1,000 into a 3,000 pot. Calling 1,000 here requires ~25% equity (1k to win ~4k), but A♥2♣ has almost no clean outs: only the three remaining Aces (~6–7%) to realistically become best. Villain’s line (check‑raise flop then a turn barrel) is weighted toward value (Qx, 9x, sets) with some semi‑bluff spade combos mixed in, but not nearly enough bluffs to justify a call. Raising as a bluff is too thin — he can peel or shove with hands that dominate or fold only the very weakest parts of his range. I stick to my earlier plan (give up to large turn aggression when I haven’t improved) and preserve my big stack for better spots. Fold.

I am on the button with a pocket pair of deuces (2s, 2c). In a heads-up situation, any pocket pair is a strong starting hand. I want to take the initiative and build the pot or take it down pre-flop. My standard opening raise has been 300 throughout the tournament, so I will maintain that sizing to keep my range balanced and apply pressure to the Big Blind.

Flash opened to 300 from the button — a wide, standard heads‑up open. I have QcTh, which is a strong defensive hand heads‑up with good playability and decent equity against an opening range. Calling 200 more (300 total committed including my BB) keeps the pot controlled, defends a reasonable portion of my range, and lets me realize equity postflop OOP. A small 3‑bet is possible as a bluff, but QTo is marginal as a 3‑bet and I prefer to see a flop and play a position‑dependent plan: continuation‑bet or fold to heavy action on dry boards, and extract value when I hit top pair or good draws.

The flop is Kd Jd Ac and my hand is Qc Th — I have the Broadway straight (A-K-Q-J-T), effectively the nut on this board. Flash opened wide from the button so his range includes many Ax, Kx, Jx and diamond draws that will happily call a value bet. I want to extract value and charge draws while keeping worse hands in, so I bet ~2/3 pot. This sizing (400 into 600) gets called by A/K/J pairs and many draws but is large enough to deny free cards; if he check-raises big I’m happy to call or jam with the best hand.

On a board of Kd-Jd-Ac, my pocket deuces are in terrible shape. This flop heavily connects with many hands in the Big Blind's calling range, including any Ace, King, Jack, or Broadway draw. Minni's lead into me (a donk bet) of 400 into a 600-chip pot signals strength or at least a high-equity draw. Continuing with the lowest possible pair on such a coordinated Broadway board is a losing play. I have no meaningful equity and should fold to preserve my stack.

Heads-up on the button with 5c6d — a hand I want to open in position. With deep stacks and heads-up dynamics I should be raising a very wide range to take the initiative and leverage position postflop. I'll open to 3x the big blind (raise to 300 total) — larger than the minimum to narrow the BB's defending range and build pots when I'm ahead. If Flash 3-bets big I'll re-evaluate (likely fold or call depending on size); if called I'll use position to c-bet or control the pot based on the board texture.

I am in the big blind with Queen-Trey offsuit. While I am getting decent odds to call Minni's 3x open (200 to call into a 400 pot), Q3o is a very weak hand that performs poorly out of position. Given that I have a significant stack and want to minimize unnecessary losses while trailing in the tournament, I will fold this marginal hand and wait for a better spot.

I am on the button with King-Ten offsuit. This is a strong starting hand in heads-up play, well above the average range. Minni has a significant chip lead, but with 123.5 big blinds, I still have a deep stack. I will raise to 300 to take control of the hand and build the pot.

Heads-up vs a 3x open (300) from the button. Q♦2♠ is marginal but has some showdown value and reasonably blocks some Qx combos; in heads-up I defend a wide range from the big blind. I don't want to 3-bet as a bluff with such poor blockers and then play a big pot out of position against a wide but often sticky opener. Calling keeps the pot manageable, gives me a chance to hit a queen or backdoor equity, and lets me re-evaluate on the flop (folding to heavy action without improvement). I will call the 200 more (300 total committed) to see a flop.

Out of position on A♦ 3♥ 7♦ with Q♦2♠ — no pair and only marginal showdown value (one diamond that gives only runner-runner backdoor potential). The button’s 3x open in heads‑up means his range contains a lot of A‑x and broadway combos that hit this flop well, so I don’t want to bloat the pot OOP with a weak hand or bluff here. I’ll check to control the pot and see what line Flash takes. Plan: check; if he C‑bets a small size (≈ ≤1/3 pot ≈ 200) I may call once to see a turn because the price is cheap, but I’ll fold to medium/large bets (>200) unless I improve to a real hand (pair/strong draw). If he checks back I can show down my Q‑high or re‑evaluate on the turn. Preserve stack and wait for a better spot to attack.

I was the pre-flop aggressor and have position. The flop of Ace-high generally favors the raiser's range more than the caller's range. Although I missed the board entirely with King-Ten, this is a standard spot for a continuation bet. A bet of 300 (half the pot) allows me to represent the Ace and potentially fold out better non-Ace hands like pocket pairs or various drawing hands. If Minni calls, I can re-evaluate on the turn and likely check back to see a cheap showdown unless my hand improves.

Flash (button) c-bets 300 into a 900 pot on A♦ 3♥ 7♦. I hold Q♦2♠ — no pair but a diamond and some cheap showdown value. I checked to keep the pot manageable and intended to call small c-bets; 300 here is ~1/3 pot so it fits that plan.

Pot math: calling 300 into a 1200 pot requires ~25% equity. I have 6 clean outs to pair by the river (3 queens + 3 deuces) → ~24.1% to hit at least one by river, and an additional small chance (~4.2%) of a runner-runner diamond flush. That puts my realistic improvement chance roughly around the break-even region. Against a wide heads‑up c-bet range there will also be bluffs and non-Ax hands I can beat, but many of Flash’s value hands will include A‑x, where even a made queen often loses.

Strategy: I don’t want to 3‑bet bluff out of position with poor blockers, and folding surrenders too much given the small bet. Calling keeps the pot under control, realizes my equity, and allows me to fold to heavy turn aggression if I don’t improve or continue if I do. I will call the 300 and reassess on the turn.

Board: 3♥ A♦ 7♦ 9♣, hand Q♦2♠. Flash opened, c‑bet small on the flop and again with a ~300 turn bet into the ~900 pot (pot ~1200 after his bet). I have no pair — only six clean outs (three queens, three deuces) to make a pair on the river: 6/46 ≈ 13% equity. Calling 300 into the 1200 pot requires roughly 20% equity to break even (300/(1200+300) ≈ 20%), so this is slightly behind by pure equity. 

However, this is heads‑up and Flash’s line (small flop c‑bet and small turn bet) includes a fair number of bluffs/missed broadways and thin value hands. The bet is small (1/3 pot), I block some Qx combos, and folding here surrenders too much versus a wide frequency. Raising as a bluff out of position with poor blockers is unattractive. 

Plan: call the 300 to keep the pot manageable and realize equity; reassess on the river. If I pair the river I can extract value or continue; if I miss and face significant river aggression I’ll fold. Therefore I will call the turn bet (total committed on the hand after this call = 900).

After Minni called my continuation bet on the Ace-high flop, her range is likely comprised of an Ace, a middle pair (like 7x), or a flush draw. The 9 on the turn is a blank that doesn't significantly change the board texture. Since I have King-high with no draw, betting again is risky as Minni is unlikely to fold an Ace, which is a significant part of her calling range. By checking back, I exercise pot control, keep the pot small, and take a free card to potentially hit a King or Ten on the river, or simply get to a cheap showdown against her missed draws.

Opponent opened wide from the button, I defended preflop and called a small flop bet. I never improved — river 8♣ leaves me with Queen-high (Q♦2♠) and no realistic value to extract. Flash checked the turn and checked the river, showing he’s either giving up bluffs/draws or slow-playing a made hand; out of position, leading a bluff here is low EV because many worse hands will fold and many better hands will call. Pot is only ~1200 and stacks are deep, so preserving chips and taking the free showdown is correct. I will check to show down rather than bluff into a checked pot.

On the river, the board is 3h-Ad-7d-9c-8c. After checking the turn for pot control, the river has also been checked by Minni. My King-high has very little showdown value given that Minni called a bet on an Ace-high flop, which usually indicates she has at least a piece of the board or a draw. However, since she has checked twice now, she likely has a marginal hand like a 7 or a 3, or a missed diamond draw. While a bluff might occasionally fold out a weak pair, Minni is the chip leader and has shown a willingness to call down in previous hands. Checking is the most prudent play to see a cheap showdown, as I may occasionally win against busted draws like Qd-Jd or Qd-Td, and I avoid losing more chips in a hand where I am likely behind.

Heads-up on the button with Ah6o is a hand I want to be the aggressor with — I have position and an Ace which plays very well postflop. Limping/calling 50 is cheap but passive and gives Flash good odds to see flops; raising to the min (200) takes the initiative, creates fold equity against his wide defend/call range, and lets me c-bet many flops from position. Stacks are deep enough to play postflop, and if Flash 3-bets I can re-evaluate facing a strong range. Raise to 200 (min-raise) to open the pot and exploit position.

In Hand #136, I am in the Big Blind with Queen-Nine offsuit. Minni has min-raised to 200 from the Small Blind. Given that I only need to call 100 more into a pot of 300, I am getting 3:1 pot odds. Q9o is a strong enough hand to defend my big blind against a wide heads-up opening range, so I will call to see the flop.

I have flopped middle pair (Nines) with a Queen kicker on a board of Ace-Trey-Nine. Minni, who raised pre-flop, has checked. In this scenario, checking back is a solid play. It allows me to control the size of the pot with a medium-strength hand and protects my checking range. Betting might get folds from worse hands or calls from better hands (Aces), whereas checking can induce bluffs on the turn from hands I am currently beating.

I have top pair (A♥6♦) on A♣ 3♦ 9♣ — heads‑up this is a strong holding but with a weak kicker and a two‑club board that gives lots of flush equity. Flash called my preflop min‑raise with a very wide defending range, so his checking the flop is consistent with both weak Ax, middle pairs, floats and club draws. I want value from worse A‑x and 9x hands and to charge/deter club draws, so I should maintain aggression. A ~60% pot c‑bet balances value and protection — big enough to charge draws and get value from worse aces, but not so large that it folds out too many calling hands or commits me unnecessarily with a marginal kicker. If he check‑raises large I’ll give up (he’s representing sets/two‑pair), but I’ll call smaller turns/raises and re‑evaluate. Bet to extract value and protect: 250 chips.

Flash has flopped middle pair with a Queen kicker on an Ace-high board. While Minni, the pre-flop raiser, is representing strength with a continuation bet, the bet size is relatively standard for a heads-up match. Given the pot odds of approximately 2.6:1, folding middle pair here would be too tight. Raising would likely only be called by better hands (Aces), so calling is the best option to keep the pot manageable and evaluate the turn card. This allows me to see if Minni continues the aggression or checks, which might signal a bluff or a weaker holding.

I am first to act on the turn with middle pair (Nines). On an Ace-high board, middle pair is a classic 'bluff catcher' or pot-control hand. I checked the flop and called a bet, and the deuce on the turn is a brick that doesn't change the board texture significantly. Leading out now would likely only get called by better hands (Aces) and fold out the bluffs I want to keep in. Checking is the most sound play to keep the pot manageable and see how Minni responds.

I have top pair with a weak kicker (A♥6♦). I was the preflop aggressor and c‑bet the flop; Flash called, which gives him a wide continuing range (worse A‑x, 9x, small pairs, and club draws on the two‑club flop). The 2♥ on the turn is a blank for most of that range and he checked, so I retain the initiative. I want value from worse aces/9x and to charge any club draws (they have ~19–20% to hit the river if they hold two clubs), while avoiding giving a free river. A sizable but controlled continuation bet (~60% pot) accomplishes that balance — it extracts value, taxes draws, and keeps me from over‑committing with a marginal kicker. If he check‑raises large (polarized to sets/two‑pair/very strong), I’ll fold; if he calls I’ll re‑evaluate on the river and proceed cautiously if a club or a pairing card shows. Bet 540 chips into the 900 chip pot.

On the turn, the board has become A-3-9-2. The 2 of hearts is a complete brick that does not complete any likely draws. I am holding middle pair (nines) with a Queen kicker. In the previous street, I called a bet from Minni, identifying my hand as a bluff-catcher. Minni has followed up with a 540 bet into a 900 pot (roughly 60%). While an Ace is definitely in Minni's range, Minni's frequent aggression suggests a wide range of semi-bluffs (like flush draws or straight draws) and air. Given the pot odds (calling 540 to win 1980, requiring about 27% equity), folding second pair here would be over-folding against an aggressive opponent on such a dry turn card. Raising doesn't accomplish much, so I will call to see the river and re-evaluate.

I am first to act on the river with third pair (nines). The board has run out Ace-Trey-Nine-Deuce-Jack with three clubs. The Jack is an overcard to my pair, and the third club completes a potential flush. I have played this hand as a bluff-catcher since the flop. Leading out now would not serve any clear purpose; I won't get called by worse hands, and I'm unlikely to fold out an Ace or a Jack. Checking is the standard play here, allowing me to see if Minni checks back for showdown or fires a third barrel. Given the pot size and the board texture, I will check and evaluate Minni's final action.

Check. The J♣ on the river makes a three‑club board and completes a lot of hands that beat my A6 (any two clubs = a flush) while also keeping in Ax and 9x that called flop/turn. I have top pair with a very weak kicker after leading flop and turn; betting here only extracts thin value from worse A‑x/9x but will get called by all flushes and can be check‑raised by very strong hands. Pot control and showdown protection are the priority — check to avoid bloating the pot with a marginal holding. If Flash bets a small/medium size I can call for value; if he jams or puts in a large polarized raise I can fold.

I am in the Small Blind/Button position with Jack-Trey offsuit. While J-high hands can sometimes be played in heads-up situations, J3o is a very weak hand. Given my current stack disadvantage (approximately 1:2.3 ratio) and looking at the remaining hands in the tournament (137 of 150), I need to be selective about the pots I enter. My opponent, Minni, has shown a tendency to defend her big blind and call raises. Folding here preserves my stack and avoids a potentially difficult post-flop situation with a weak hand, allowing me to wait for a stronger opening to commit chips and potentially double up.

Heads-up on the button (I’m the SB with positional advantage). 3c6h is a weak holding but in heads-up the open-raise range should be wide — I want to take the initiative, pick up the blinds, and play post-flop in position. The pot is 150 and the min raise is 200 (2x BB). I will raise to 200 total to apply pressure and avoid a bloated multistreet pot out of position. Plan: fold to a large 3-bet with this rag hand, call if Flash just flats and then c-bet on many dry flops or proceed cautiously on coordinated boards, extracting small pots and leveraging position. Raising here balances my range and is the most +EV option heads-up.

I am holding Queen-Ten suited, which is a premium hand in heads-up play. Minni has min-raised from the button. While calling is a standard and safe play to realize equity, 3-betting allows me to take initiative and build a pot with a hand that has high playability and significant strength against a wide button opening range. Given I am trailing in chips, I will raise to 700 to exert pressure and define the range.

I opened to 200 on the button with 3♣6♥ and Flash 3‑bet to 700. The pot is 900 and I must call 500 to continue, which would require roughly 500/(900+500) ≈ 36% equity to break even. 3‑6 is a very weak hand vs a typical 3‑bet range (pairs, Ax, broadways) and has poor playability and implied odds postflop even in position. I’m also the clear chip leader (27.8k vs his 11.3k) late in the 150‑hand match, so there’s no need to gamble marginally. My prior plan was to fold this rag to a large 3‑bet — that’s still the correct, +EV decision here. Fold and pick better spots in position.
I am on the button with Ace-Five offsuit in a heads-up situation. This is a standard opening range hand. I'll raise to 300 to take control of the pot and see how Minni reacts.

Facing a 3x open to 300 from the SB and I'm the big blind with Kc3h. Pot is 400, call costs 200 (I would commit 300 total) so break-even equity is ~33%. Against a very wide heads‑up opening range I have roughly that or better equity, and calling is a small, low‑variance defense given my very deep stack. A 3‑bet bluff here is unnecessary — Flash has shown willingness to call/continue fairly often, so a bluff 3‑bet will often bloat the pot out of position with a marginal hand. I’ll defend by calling: keep the pot contested, realize equity, and play the flop cautiously out of position (check/fold most complete misses, value/raise when I pair my King or have strong improvement). If Flash 4‑bets big I will fold K3o unless I get excellent odds to continue.

Pot is 600 and I’m OOP with Kc3h on 7s-8c-Jd — I have no pair, only 6 low outs to make a pair (Kings or Threes) and essentially no meaningful draws. Flash opened wide preflop and will c-bet a high frequency, but I don’t want to bloat a big pot out of position with a complete miss. Checking preserves pot control and lets me realize equity; I’ll call small continuation bets to see a turn but fold to large, pot‑committing raises. If I pair my King or pick up a strong turn, I can take an aggressive line then. Default action: check and proceed cautiously.

On a board of 7-8-J rainbow, Minni as the Big Blind defender likely has many pieces of this range (7x, 8x, Jx, or straight draws like T9, 96). My Ace-Five offsuit has one overcard and some showdown value, but it does not benefit much from betting into a board that connects so well with a typical defending range. Checking back allows me to see a turn for free, preserve my stack, and potentially realize equity if an Ace falls or if the hand goes to showdown cheaply.

I’m OOP with K♣3♥ on 7♠-8♣-J♦-9♣. I have no pair, no meaningful draw and only 6 outs (3 kings + 3 threes) — ~13% to hit the river. Flash checked both flop and turn, showing either weakness or a hand they don’t want to build a pot with. Betting here would bloat the pot with very little equity and expose me to raises I should fold. Pot control is the correct line: check to keep the pot small, realise my equity, call a small river bet if appropriate, and fold to large, pot‑committing aggression. If I pair on the river or get a favorable card I can re-evaluate for value or a targeted bluff then.

The board is now 7-8-J-9, which is extremely connected. Almost any two cards in Minni's defending range could have a piece of this board, and several combinations (like any Ten or 5-6) have made a straight. Although Minni checked the turn, this board is too dangerous to bluff with only Ace-high and no real draw. I will check back to see a free river and try to reach showdown or improve to a winning hand if an Ace falls.

I have Kc3h — no pair and no meaningful draws on 7s‑8c‑Jd‑9c‑2d. Flash checked both the flop and the turn, signaling weakness or a non‑committed holding; while their preflop raising range is wide, they tend to be sticky and will call small river bluffs. The pot is only 600 and there’s no value to betting K‑high, while a bluff here has limited fold equity and risks getting called or raised out of position. With deep stacks and a small pot, pot control is the correct line: check and take the showdown. If Flash raises, I’ll fold to sizable aggression.

The board is highly connected (7-8-J-9-2), making many straights possible (any Ten or 5-6). However, the action has been checked all the way through since the pre-flop raise. With Ace-high, I have decent showdown value against missed draws or lower high-card hands. Betting here is unlikely to fold out a better hand (any pair will likely call a small bet) and I don't want to turn my hand into a bluff on such a dangerous board. Checking back allows me to see the showdown for free in a small pot.

Heads-up on the button with K♦4♣ — a marginal hand but perfectly playable in this spot. Pot is 150, to call 50. I want to take the initiative and use position postflop, plus I have a sizable stack advantage versus Flash, who defends the BB often. Open to a standard 3x BB sizing to 300 to apply pressure and get fold equity; a tiny min-raise would invite more calls/3-bets. Plan: if he 3-bets big I will fold K4o; if he calls I’ll c-bet favorable flops, control pot size on coordinated boards, and barrel selectively. Raising now maximizes expected value in heads-up play while keeping postflop options.

I am holding 7-2 offsuit, which is the weakest starting hand in poker. Although the pot odds for a call are 2:1 (calling 200 to win 400), this hand performs exceptionally poorly post-flop and is difficult to play out of position against an aggressive opponent. With only 11 hands remaining in the tournament and a substantial stack of over 120 big blinds, I should not be defending with the bottom of my range. I will conserve my chips for a better spot to close the gap on Minni's lead.

I am in the Small Blind with 3s 5h, a very weak hand. The pot is 150 and it costs me 50 to call. However, with only 10 hands remaining in the tournament and my stack being significantly smaller than Minni's, I need to be selective about the pots I enter. 3-5 offsuit is near the bottom of my range and does not play well post-flop, especially against an opponent who has shown aggression. I will fold and wait for a better spot to utilize my stack.

Heads-up on the button with 7♦6♦ — a very playable, suited connector that has good multi-street equity and plays well in position. I have a large chip advantage (27.6k vs 12.2k) and it’s late in the tournament, so I want to take the initiative without overcommitting. Open-raising is standard here to deny free flops and build a pot I can play last in. I’ll raise to 300 total (≈3x the BB) — big enough to apply pressure and narrow ranges but small enough to keep weaker hands and draws in. If Flash 3-bets big I’ll re-evaluate/fold; if called I’ll use position to play aggressively postflop.

I am currently holding 7-2 offsuit, which is widely considered the weakest starting hand in Texas Hold'em. I am in the big blind and Minni has raised to 300 from the small blind. While I am getting 2:1 pot odds to call, playing this hand out of position for the rest of the hand is likely to lead to a loss of chips. With only 8 hands remaining in the tournament, I should conserve my stack (12,210) for better opportunities rather than defending with trash hands. Folding is the most mathematically and strategically sound decision here.

I have QJs on the button (Small Blind) in a heads-up situation. This is a very strong starting hand. With only 8 hands remaining in the tournament and my stack significantly behind Minni's, I need to look for opportunities to build the pot or take it down early. I will put in a standard raise to 300 to take control of the hand.

Heads-up vs the button (Flash) who opened to 300 from the SB. I’m in the BB with K♥8♥. Pot is 400 and it costs 200 to call (pot odds ~3:1). K8s is playable heads-up with good equity (top‑pair potential and nut‑flush possibilities) and fits a calling/defending range vs a wide SB opener. 3‑betting K8s out of position is thin against an aggressive opener—I prefer to keep the pot controllable and see a flop. Plan: call to 300, continue aggressively on favorable flops (K or hearts) and fold or pot‑control against heavy action when missed.

I have top pair (K♥8♥) on a dry Ace-high flop (Ad 4c Ks). Heads-up versus a wide SB opener who checked to me, I’m usually ahead of a lot of his missed hands and behind only A‑x and some two‑pair/sets. With position I want to extract value from worse Kx and charge any floats/draws while keeping the pot controllable against A‑x. The board is relatively dry so a half‑pot bet is the right balance — it gets value from worse hands, folds pure air, and avoids bloating the pot if he shows strength. Plan: bet 300. If he folds I take it; if he calls I continue cautiously (tighten up vs a turn lead or big raise, but look to extract further value on blank turns).

On a board of Ad-Ks-4c, I have Queen-high with a gutshot straight draw (needing a Ten). My opponent has led into me (a donk bet) for 300 into a 600 pot. This board texture heavily favors my pre-flop raising range, but Minni's lead suggests she has connected with the board, likely holding at least a King or a weak Ace. While I have a gutshot, calling with only 8 hands remaining in the tournament and being significantly out-chipped is risky, especially since I have very little showdown value and am drawing to only four outs. Raising as a semi-bluff is an option, but if she has an Ace, she is unlikely to fold. I will preserve my stack for a better spot to close the gap.

Heads-up on the button with pocket Jacks — a very strong hand preflop. Calling 50 is too passive and the minimum raise to 200 is smaller than the table’s typical open; I want to take the initiative, deny cheap flops, and extract value from worse hands (Ax, Kx, Qx, suited connectors) while keeping a sensible pot size. I’ll raise to 300 (≈3x BB), a standard sizing here that gives good fold equity vs the big blind and leaves room to play postflop. If Flash 3-bets large I’ll re-evaluate based on sizing and pot odds, but open to 300 now.

I am in the Big Blind with Ten of Clubs and Deuce of Diamonds, which is a very weak starting hand. Minni has raised from the Small Blind to 300. While I am getting 2:1 odds on a call, the hand is difficult to play out of position, and with the tournament reaching its final hands (144 of 150), I prefer to wait for a stronger holding or a better spot to commit my remaining chips rather than defending with trash.

I am on the button with K3s. In a heads-up situation, a King-high suited hand is well above the average starting hand strength. Minni has a significant chip lead, so I should play relatively aggressively to pick up pots but remain cautious of her ability to pressure my stack. I will raise to 300 to take control of the hand pre-flop.

I have K♠ Q♥ in the big blind facing a button open to 300. Heads-up this is a strong, high-card hand and the button’s opening range is wide. I’m out of position postflop, so I want to take the initiative preflop: 3-betting extracts value from worse Kx/Qx and many broadway hands, gains fold equity against suited connectors/weak pairs, and isolates the shorter-stacked opponent. With deep stacks (my stack 28,090, Flash 11,510; effective ≈11.5k) I don’t want to overcommit but also want meaningful pressure—raising to 900 (~3x the open) is a standard, balanced size. If Flash 4-bets large I’ll re-evaluate (likely fold); if he calls, I’ll c-bet appropriate flops and use position awareness. Therefore: 3-bet to 900.

I have King-Trey of hearts on the button. I opened to 300, and Minni 3-bet to 900. While K3s is a decent heads-up hand, it struggles against a 3-bet range from a chip leader who has been playing aggressively. However, given we are deep-stacked and it's hand 145 of 150, I need to look for opportunities to close the gap. Calling the 600 more is acceptable to see a flop in position, but if I miss, I must be ready to let it go. Folding is safe, but calling allows me to play the hand with position.

I 3-bet preflop and was called; on the J♥-2♥-5♠ flop I hold K♠Q♥ — two overcards and a backdoor heart, acting first. As the preflop aggressor I have a range advantage and good fold equity vs the caller’s wide calling range (lots of missed broadways, suited connectors and small pairs). A ~50% pot continuation bet (900 into 1800) puts pressure on many hands that missed and charges draws, while keeping the pot controllable with effective stacks ~11.5k. Plan: take it down if he folds; if he calls I’ll re-evaluate the turn and continue on safe cards or check when the runout strongly helps his range (hearts or A-high); if he check-raises large I’ll give credit to a made Jx or strong A♥ and fold rather than bloating the pot out of position.

I have the nut-flush draw (with the King of hearts) on a Jack-high board. Minni 3-bet pre-flop and has led out for 900 on the flop, which is a standard continuation bet. With my current stack size and the tournament nearing its end (hand 145 of 150), I need to maximize my chances of winning a large pot to catch up to Minni's lead. Calling is the best option here because it allows me to realize my equity and keep Minni's range wide. If I hit my flush on the turn, I will be in a perfect position to value bet or raise. Raising now might force out her bluffs or weaker hands that I still have high equity against, and I am in position, which allows me to see how she reacts to the turn card.

The turn A♣ is exactly the kind of card that helps Flash’s calling range (AK/AQ/Ax, A‑heart combos, some AJ/Jx) and is very bad for my two overcards K♠Q♥. I have no pair and only a runner‑runner heart possibility, so barreling again out of position would be thin and risks bloating the pot with a hand that is almost always behind. Pot is 3,600 and Flash still has ~10k effective — I should prioritize pot control and avoid a marginal bluff at this stack depth. I will check to keep the pot manageable, allow him to bluff or give a price to continue with draws, and plan to call small turn bets (or fold to a large, polarized shove/check‑raise). If he checks behind I’ll re‑evaluate the river and look for a good bluff spot on a safe card.

The turn brings the Ace of Clubs. Minni 3-bet pre-flop and led the flop but has now checked on a card that should theoretically favor her range. While I could bet to represent the Ace, I have the nut flush draw and plenty of equity. Checking back is the most prudent play here as it guarantees I see the river for free to realize my equity. If I hit the flush, I can bet for value; if I miss, I can evaluate based on the river card and her action. Given I am trailing in the tournament, preserving my stack while keeping the door open for a large pot on the river is the best strategy.

I check. I’ve got K♠Q♥ — no pair, only two overcards and a single heart. Flash called my 3‑bet and the flop c‑bet, so his continuing range is heavily weighted to Jx, Ax and medium/low pocket pairs; most of those hands will call a river bluff. The river (2♠) just pairs the board, which reduces my fold equity and increases the likelihood he has a pair or better. With stacks deep enough that a bluff could be raised off, pot control and preserving chips is best here — check to show down and fold to a large river raise (or call a very small blocker bet only if the price is attractive).

The river brings a second deuce, pairing the board. My flush draw has missed, leaving me with only King-high. Minni 3-bet pre-flop and led the flop, but has checked both the turn and the river. This line often indicates a marginal made hand like a Jack or a medium pocket pair that is wary of the Ace or is looking for a cheap showdown. While King-high might occasionally win against a missed lower heart draw, it loses to almost everything else in Minni's 3-betting range. However, bluffing into a chip leader with only five hands remaining is risky, as she can afford to call with any pair to preserve her lead. Given I still have 100 big blinds, I will check behind and preserve my stack for a more favorable opportunity to close the gap in the final few hands.

Heads-up on the button with 7♦6♠ — a playable, connected hand where position after the flop is a big advantage. Limping/calling 50 is passive and surrenders initiative; opening lets me steal the blind and play the pot on my terms. I'll open-raise to a standard 3x BB (300 total) to apply pressure, keep the range wide, and either take the pot preflop or play position postflop. If Flash 3-bets big I can re-evaluate and fold to preserve my large stack.

I am in the Big Blind with 6c 7h. The opponent, Minni, has raised to 300 from the Button/Small Blind. I have 9910 chips left, and the pot is 400. To call, I need to commit another 200 chips. 6-7 offsuit is a marginal hand, but given that we are heads-up and I have over 90 big blinds, I can justify a call to see a flop, especially since I am closing the action pre-flop. Folding is too tight for heads-up play against a standard button raise.

The flop is 3d 9s 9c. I have missed completely with my 7-high and have no significant draws on this paired board. As the player out of position, checking to the pre-flop aggressor is the standard play. I will wait to see Minni's action, but without a pair or a strong draw, I am likely folding to any significant bet.

I opened to 300 preflop and got called from the BB. Flop comes 3♦ 9♠ 9♣ — I hold 7♦6♠ (no pair, no real draw). Heads-up as the preflop aggressor I have initiative and this board is reasonably good for a c-bet: many of Flash’s preflop calls are wide and he often checks back marginal hands. A small continuation bet around half-pot applies pressure, folds out unpaired overcards and some mid-strength hands, and keeps the pot controllable if I get called. I’ll bet 300 (≈50% pot). Plan: fold to a large check-raise; if called I’ll reassess the turn and barrel selectively on safe cards or pot-control with showdown value hands.

On a paired flop of 3d-9s-9c, I have 7-high with no draws and no pair. Minni, who was the pre-flop aggressor, has followed up with a 1/2 pot continuation bet of 300. While this is a standard board for a c-bet, I have zero equity and no realistic way to win the hand without a significant bluff. With only five hands remaining in the tournament, I should not waste chips on a complete air-ball. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity to defend or attack in the final few hands.

In a heads-up situation with only a few hands remaining, K6o is a relatively strong starting hand from the button. I will put pressure on the big blind by raising to 300, which has been my standard opening size, to take control of the pot pre-flop.

Facing a standard button open to 300. I’m in the big blind with 4♦5♠ offsuit. Heads-up the opener’s range is wide and this hand has reasonable equity (straight/two-pair potential). Calling 200 into the current pot (~400) requires ~33% equity, which this hand has vs a wide opening range. I don’t want to 3‑bet bluff out of position and bloat the pot with a weak offsuit hand, so I’ll defend and see a flop. Plan: call to see the flop, fold to heavy turn/river aggression if I completely miss, otherwise continue with pairs or strong draws.

I defended preflop with 4♦5♠ to see a flop against a wide button opening range — that was fine. The flop Q♦ 8♠ 7♥ is fairly dry and heavily favors the raiser’s range (Qx, 8x, 7x, broadways, pairs). My hand has only a 4‑card gutshot (four 6s) — 4 outs — which is ~8.5% to hit the turn and ~16.4% to hit by the river. Out of position with marginal equity, I want pot control and to avoid bloating the pot with a weak hand. I’ll check and let the opener show aggression; I’ll only continue with very small bets from them (they’d have to give me >~6:1 pot odds — roughly bets ≤ ~120 into the 600 pot) or if I improve (6 or pair). If they fire a standard continuation bet (>~1/3 pot) I’ll fold and preserve my large stack.

I have King-high with an overcard on a Queen-high board. As the pre-flop aggressor with position, a continuation bet is standard here to maintain initiative and attempt to take the pot down. The board texture (7h-Qd-8s) contains some potential draws for Minni, but a half-pot bet should be enough to force folds from many of her missed hands while allowing me to control the pot size if I choose to check the turn.

I fold. I defended preflop to see a flop vs a wide button range, but the board Q♦ 8♠ 7♥ and Flash’s 300 c-bet into the 900 pot is a standard-sized bet that disfavors my hand. Calling 300 to win a 1,200 pot requires ~25% equity; 4♦5♠ only has a thin 4-card gutshot to a straight and weak pair-outs that are frequently behind against a c-betting range heavy in Qx/8x/7x and broadway overcards. I’m out of position, the bet is not a tiny probe, and I have a very large stack to preserve — this is a marginal spot to gamble 300 chips. Fold and wait for a better opportunity.

Heads-up on the button with QQ — a very strong, value hand that's ahead of almost everything except AA/KK. Pot is 150 and it only costs 50 to see the flop, but I want to build the pot and isolate the big blind. A standard heads-up open (3x BB) and my table habit is to raise to 300. With deep stacks I don't want to overcommit preflop (no shove) but I want to apply pressure and get value from worse hands. If Flash 3-bets big I'll re-evaluate on stack depth and pot odds. So I raise to 300.

We are at hand 148 of 150. I am trailing Minni by approximately 20,000 chips. Minni has been consistently raising from the button, but J6o is a very weak hand to defend from the Big Blind, especially out of position. While the pot odds are 3:1, the poor post-flop playability of this hand makes it a clear fold. I will preserve my remaining 99.1 big blinds for the final two hands, looking for a better starting hand to potentially double up or accumulate chips.

I am dealt Ace-Jack offsuit on the button in a heads-up situation. This is a premium hand in this context. With only two hands remaining in the tournament and being significantly behind in chips, I need to look for opportunities to build the pot when I have a strong equity advantage. Raising is the standard play here to take control of the hand and extract value.

Heads-up vs the button who opened to 300. I hold K♦Q♦ — a strong suited broadway that fares well against a wide button opening range. Pot is ~400 and a call would be 200 more (total 300), but I want to apply pressure and isolate rather than play a large multi-street pot OOP. I’ll 3-bet to a standard, respectable size (raise to 900 total) — big enough to fold out weaker holdings and deny favorable odds to speculative hands, but small relative to effective stacks so I’m not overcommitted. If he 4-bets big I’ll fold (or re-evaluate vs a small squeeze), if he calls I’ll continue with C-bets on favorable textures and play straightforwardly postflop. This maximizes EV against his wide opening range while preserving stack for the final hand.

I have been dealt Ace-Jack offsuit on the button, which is a premium hand in a heads-up match. I opened for 300, and Minni has 3-bet to 900. While AJ is strong enough to consider a 4-bet, calling is often the superior play heads-up to keep the opponent's range wide and utilize my positional advantage post-flop. With the tournament nearly over (Hand 149 of 150), I want to maximize my stack, but I don't necessarily want to risk a 96-big-blind shove pre-flop with AJ unless forced. Calling allows me to evaluate the flop and maintain control of the pot size.

Flop Td Ts Kc with Kd Qd: I have top pair with a good kicker. Flash called my 3‑bet preflop, so his range is wide (Kx, Ax, broadways, 22‑QQ, suited connectors) — I’m ahead of a lot of that range but vulnerable to tens (sets/quads) or strong AK/KA. The board is fairly dry, so I should extract value but keep the pot controllable. I’ll bet ~50% pot (900) to get called by worse kings and medium pairs and deny cheap turns to speculative holdings. Plan: if he calls I’ll re‑evaluate on the turn and continue for value on blanks; if he check‑raises big I’ll give credit to sets/full houses and fold or proceed very cautiously.

On a paired board of [Td, Ts, Kc], I hold Ace-Jack (Ace high) with a gutshot draw to the nut straight (any Queen). Minni's 3-bet pre-flop followed by a lead on the flop is a strong line, but on this board texture, she could easily be continuation betting with a wide range, including hands like AQ, QJ, or even air. Given the pot odds (900 to call into a pot of 2700, or 3:1), my overcards, and the gutshot potential, calling is the most appropriate move. It allows me to realize my equity and see how the turn develops without overcommitting my remaining stack on a marginal hand.

I hold K♦Q♦ on Td Ts Kc 3♦ — top pair with a very strong turn improvement: I’ve picked up a four‑card diamond flush draw (two diamonds in hand + Td + 3♦ = 4 diamonds, ~9 outs) plus the top pair and a good kicker. Flash called my 3‑bet preflop and my flop c‑bet, so his range is wide (worse Kx, Qx, A‑x, some medium pairs and single tens/trips). I’m ahead of a lot of that range and have significant equity to improve. I want to extract value from worse kings/queens and charge his draws while keeping the pot controllable against the very few hands that currently beat me (trip tens, two pair). A half‑pot turn bet achieves this: it gets called by worse hands and some draws, denies free rivers, but isn’t so large that I’m uncomfortably committed to fold to a big check‑raise. Betting 1800 into the 3600 pot is my plan.

On the turn of hand 149 of 150, I am faced with a bet of 1800 into a 3600 pot. Holding Ace-Jack, I currently have Ace-high and a gutshot draw to the nut straight (any Queen). Given the pot odds of 3:1, I need roughly 25% equity to make a call profitable. While my current hand strength is marginal against Minni's aggressive line, the potential to hit a Broadway straight and the possibility that my Ace-high is still the best hand against semi-bluffs like Queen-Jack or suited connectors make calling the correct play. With the tournament ending in the next hand, I must realize my equity and seek opportunities to improve my stack position.

I have K♦Q♦ on Td Ts Kc 3♦ 8♣ — top pair with a very strong kicker. My diamond flush missed on the river, but I was the preflop aggressor and Villain (Flash) has called a 3‑bet and two sizable bets, so his continuing range is wide and contains many hands I beat (weaker Kx, Qx, Ax, Jx, small pairs) as well as the minority of hands that currently beat me (any Ten or K with A kicker). He checked the river, which is consistent with pot‑control or a marginal holding. I want to extract value from worse hands while keeping the sizing disciplined against the hands that beat me. A ~50% pot value bet (3600 into a 7200 pot) will get called by a lot of worse K/Q/medium pairs and charge any remaining draws, while not overcommitting me to an unlikely check‑raise that represents trips/full houses. If he folds I pick up the pot; if he calls we often still win; if he raises big I can give credit to a very strong holding and fold.

We are currently on Hand 149 of 150 in a heads-up match. I hold Ace-Jack (Ace high) on a board of Td-Ts-Kc-3d-8c. Minni has been the aggressor throughout the hand, 3-betting pre-flop and triple-barreling all three streets. On this paired board with a King, my Ace-high is purely a bluff-catcher. Given Minni's line, she is representing a very strong range (a King, a Ten, or a high pocket pair). While the pot odds are 3:1 (requiring 25% equity to call), Ace-high is extremely weak here as it loses to every single pair, including any missed semi-bluffs that might have rivered a pair of 8s or 3s. Calling here would risk more than half of my remaining stack, leaving me with only 27 big blinds for the final hand if I lose. Folding allows me to preserve my 63 big blind stack to look for a better spot in the final hand of the tournament to maximize my position.

Heads-up on the button (SB) with Tc Jd — a strong, playable hand in position. I want to take the initiative preflop, build the pot when I have the range advantage, and deny cheap odds to the BB's speculative hands. Opponent has a healthy stack (~62 BB), so a standard 3x BB open is appropriate: it’s big enough to thin the field and extract value but not overly commit. If Flash 3-bets I can reevaluate based on sizing and frequency; versus a call I keep positional advantage going to the flop. Raise to 300 (3x BB).

This is the final hand of the tournament. I hold a premium hand, Ace-Queen of Diamonds, which is extremely strong in a heads-up situation. My stack is significantly smaller than Minni's, and since the goal is to maximize my stack size, I should look to build a large pot or double up. Minni has been frequently raising from the button. I will put in a 3-bet to 1000 to extract value from her wide opening range and potentially set up a commitment for the rest of my stack on favorable flops.

Heads-up on the button (I opened to 300) with J♦T♣ facing a 3-bet to 1000 (to call 700). Opponent has ~53BB and that 3-bet size is consistent with a mix of value and some light hands. JTo is a very playable heads-up hand and, most importantly, I’ll have position postflop. 4-betting here would either be a thin value line (only vs very strong hands) or a bluff — but JTo is not a good 4-bet bluff candidate because I don’t want to bloat a pot out of position when called by stronger hands. Calling 700 keeps the pot manageable, preserves position, and allows me to realize equity on many flops; the pot odds (~700 into ~2000) make a call reasonable vs a typical 3-bet range. Plan: see the flop, c-bet/continue on favorable textures where I hit or can credibly represent, and fold to heavy aggression when not connecting. Therefore I will call the 700.

I 3-bet pre-flop with Ace-Queen of Diamonds and was called by Minni. The flop of 4-6-9 rainbow is relatively dry and likely misses much of Minni's calling range for a 3-bet. As the pre-flop aggressor, I will make a continuation bet of 1000 (half the pot) to maintain pressure, extract value from weaker hands, and potentially force folds from small pairs or overcards that have missed. Since this is the final hand of the tournament, I want to maximize my potential stack by staying aggressive with a strong high-card hand that still has significant equity against her range.

Call 1000. On a dry 4♥6♦9♠ flop my T♣J♦ has two live overcards and reasonable equity versus Flash’s polarized 3‑bet range (pairs, AK/AQ, some broadways). Flash’s flop bet is only ~1/3 pot (1000 into 3000), so I’m getting ~3:1 pot odds (need ~25% equity). Raising here would be a thin bluff against a range that contains many made pairs that will continue or shove; I don’t want to bloat the pot out of position with a marginal hand. In position I can control the pot size, realize equity, and use the turn to either take the lead if I improve or fold to heavy action. I’ll call and reassess on the turn based on the card and opponent’s reaction.

On the turn, the Queen of Clubs pairs my Ace-Queen, giving me top pair with the best possible kicker on a relatively safe board. The pot is currently 4000, and I have 4310 chips remaining. Since this is the final hand of the tournament and I have a very strong hand in a heads-up pot, my goal is to maximize value. A bet of 2000 (half the pot) is large enough to extract significant value from hands like mid-pairs (9x, JJ, TT, 88) or a weaker Queen, while still leaving room for Minni to call or potentially bluff-shove. I am prepared to commit my entire stack on this hand given the strength of my TPTK.

Pot is 6,000 and Flash has bet 2,000 (leaving him ~310 behind). Calling 2,000 would make the pot 8,000 so I need ~25% equity to break even. With Tc Jd I have roughly 10 clean outs (3 Js, 3 Ts, 4 eights) ≈ 10/46 ≈ 21.7% to improve to a pair or a wheel straight — slightly below pure pot‑odds break‑even. Opponent 3‑bet preflop and has continued with a flop c‑bet and a turn bet, so his range is value‑heavy (QQ/TT/99, overpairs, AK/AQ) but with only ~310 left his turn bet is also a commit bet and may include some bluffs. Raising here would bloat the pot with a marginal hand and mostly get called by better hands; folding surrenders too much equity given my live outs and positional advantage. I will call the 2,000 to realize equity and use position on the river to decide the final line.

This is the final hand of the tournament and I have Top Pair Top Kicker (TPTK) with Ace-Queen on a relatively safe board. My remaining stack of 2310 is small compared to the 8000 chip pot, and since the goal is to maximize my final stack, I must bet my remaining chips for value. Minni has called three streets, suggesting she has a hand she is unwilling to fold, such as a weaker Queen, a Nine, or a middle pocket pair. Shoving is the only move to extract the maximum value before the tournament ends.

Pot is 10,310 and I must call 2,310 to see showdown (calling makes the final pot 12,620) so I need ≈2310/12620 ≈ 18.3% equity to break even. I hold J♦T♣ with no pair on 4♥ 6♦ 9♠ Q♣ 2♠ — there are no more cards to come, so this is pure showdown value: J-high only beats missed bluffs and weaker high-card hands. Flash’s line (preflop 3‑bet, flop c‑bet, turn bet, then a small shove/all‑in on the river) is value‑heavy but also polarised — heads‑up 3‑betters will include some bluffs (Axs, Kx, light combos) that can miss this board. The river shove is relatively small vs the pot, giving me attractive pot odds; the call is a small percentage of my stack (~7.8%) and will be profitable if Flash is bluffing at least ~18% of the time. Folding here denies too many thin bluffs and is overly nitty given the price, so I will call the all‑in.
